topics.title: Open Circuit Characteristics of DC Shunt Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Open Circuit Characteristics of DC Shunt Generator # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Open Circuit Characteristics (OCC)** of a DC Shunt Generator — also termed the **No-Load Saturation Curve** or the **Magnetization Characteristic** — is the graphical plot of the **generated EMF at the armature terminals ($E_0$)** as a function of the **shunt field current ($I_f$)**, recorded while the machine is driven at its **rated constant speed ($N$)** with the **load terminals open (no-load condition)**. Mathematically, the relationship is expressed as: $$E_0 = f(I_f) \quad \text{at} \quad N = N_{rated}, \quad I_L = 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > *"The Open Circuit Characteristic of a DC shunt generator is the curve relating the no-load generated EMF to the shunt field excitation current, with the generator running at rated speed under open-circuited armature terminals."* --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **bicycle pump** connected to a sealed, partially inflated balloon: | Bicycle Pump Concept | DC Shunt Generator Equivalent | |---|---| | How hard you push the pump handle | **Field current ($I_f$)** — controls magnetic strength | | Pressure inside the balloon | **Generated EMF ($E_0$)** — output voltage | | Pedaling speed (RPM) | **Speed of prime mover ($N$)** — must be constant | | Sealed balloon (no air escape) | **Open-circuited terminals (no load)** | When you **pump slowly** (small $I_f$), the balloon pressure rises **proportionally** (linear region). But once the balloon is **almost full**, no matter how hard you pump, the pressure **barely increases** (saturation region). The OCC is essentially the "pressure vs. pumping effort" curve of an electrical machine. > [!NOTE] > **Why is OCC called a "Saturation Curve"?** > Because the shape of the curve is dictated by the **magnetic saturation** of the iron core. Initially, the iron happily accepts more magnetic flux, but eventually it cannot hold any more — analogous to a sponge that becomes fully soaked. --- ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used The following **standard laboratory values** are used throughout this experiment: - **Rated Speed ($N$):** Typically **1500 RPM** for a 4-pole, 50 Hz machine. - **Rated Armature Voltage:** **220 V DC** (standard KTU lab machines). - **Rated Field Current ($I_f$):** Typically **0.5 A to 2 A** (low value due to high field winding resistance). - **Residual Flux ($\Phi_{res}$):** **2% to 5%** of rated flux — essential for self-excitation. - **Air-gap flux density ($B_g$):** Approximately **0.7 to 0.9 T** in the linear region. > [!TIP] > **KTU Lab Tip:** Always note the **residual voltage** ($E_{res}$) when $I_f = 0$. A healthy machine shows $E_{res} \approx 5\text{–}10\%$ of rated voltage. If $E_{res} = 0$, the machine has **lost its residual magnetism** and must be "flashed" by briefly energizing the field from an external DC source. --- ## 1.4 Purpose & Engineering Utility of the OCC The OCC is **NOT** merely a lab exercise — it is a **diagnostic fingerprint** of every DC generator. From a single OCC, a KTU-graded engineer can determine: 1. **Critical Field Resistance** — the maximum field circuit resistance above which the generator **fails to self-excite**. 2. **Critical Speed** — the minimum speed below which voltage build-up is impossible. 3. **Operating Point on the Load Line** — used in voltage regulation calculations. 4. **Condition of the Magnetic Core** — a shifted or flattened OCC indicates **aged laminations** or **shorted field turns**. 5. **Voltage Regulation Behavior** — by superposing the armature reaction demagnetizing effect, the actual loaded voltage can be predicted. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Physics Behind Voltage Generation When the armature of a DC machine rotates inside a magnetic field established by the field winding, the conductors **cut magnetic flux lines**, inducing an EMF as dictated by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. The **emf equation of a DC generator** is the cornerstone of this experiment: $$E = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} \quad \text{volts}$$ Where every symbol has a precise meaning and unit. ### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition of the EMF Equation - **Step 1 — Flux Cutting Rate:** Each armature conductor cuts $\Phi \cdot \frac{PN}{60}$ Webers of flux per second. - **Step 2 — Total Series Conductors:** Since conductors are connected in **$A$ parallel paths**, the number of conductors in **series per path** is $Z/A$. - **Step 3 — Total EMF:** The total EMF induced is the flux cutting rate multiplied by series conductors per path. - **Step 4 — Constants:** For a **wave-wound** armature, $A = 2$. For a **lap-wound** armature, $A = P$. --- ## 2.2 The Three Excitation Regions of the OCC When $I_f$ is varied from zero to a value producing $\approx 120\%$ of rated voltage, the OCC traverses **three distinct regions**: ### Region I — Linear (Unsaturated) Region - The iron core's permeability is **constant and high**. - Flux $\Phi$ is **directly proportional** to $I_f$. - Therefore, $E_0 \propto I_f$ (a **straight line** through the residual voltage point). - This region obeys: $\Phi = k \cdot I_f$, hence $E_0 = K \cdot I_f$ where $K = \frac{PNZ}{60A} \cdot k$. ### Region II — Knee (Transition) Region - The iron teeth and pole-shoe tips begin to **saturate**. - The slope of the curve **decreases** — the curve "bends" sharply. - This is the **design operating point** — generators are normally operated just before the knee. ### Region III — Saturation (Flat) Region - The core is **fully saturated**; further increase in $I_f$ produces negligible increase in $\Phi$. - The curve becomes **nearly horizontal**. - Operating here is **wasteful** — large field copper losses for tiny EMF gain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight — Why Operating in Saturation is Bad:** > In the saturated region, a small reduction in $I_f$ (due to heating or armature reaction) causes a **large drop in $E_0$** — meaning **poor voltage regulation**. The KTU board frequently tests this concept. --- ## 2.3 Mechanism of Self-Excitation (The Voltage Build-Up Process) A DC shunt generator is a **self-excited** machine — meaning the field winding is energized by the voltage it itself generates. This creates a fascinating **bootstrapping problem**: 1. **Initial Condition:** Residual flux $\Phi_{res} \approx 2\text{–}5\%$ of rated flux exists in the poles. 2. **Armature Rotates:** At speed $N$, the residual flux induces a small EMF $E_{res}$. 3. **Field Current Flows:** $E_{res}$ drives a small current $I_f = E_{res}/R_f$ through the field winding. 4. **Flux Increases:** This $I_f$ produces additional flux in the **same direction** as $\Phi_{res}$ (correct polarity is critical). 5. **Cumulative Build-Up:** The increased flux induces a larger $E_0$, which produces more $I_f$, which produces more flux — a **positive feedback loop**. 6. **Equilibrium Reached:** Build-up **stops** when the field circuit's $I_f \cdot R_f$ voltage drop **equals** the generated $E_0$ at the corresponding point on the OCC. --- ## 2.4 Critical Field Resistance & Critical Speed ### Critical Field Resistance ($R_c$) The **slope of the tangent** drawn from the origin to the **linear portion** of the OCC defines the **critical field resistance**: $$R_c = \tan(\theta_{tangent}) = \frac{E_0}{I_f} \quad \text{at tangent point}$$ - If **field circuit resistance $R_f < R_c$** → voltage **builds up** normally. - If **$R_f = R_c$** → voltage **oscillates unstably** at a low value. - If **$R_f > R_c$** → voltage **fails to build up** (residual EMF only). ### Critical Speed ($N_c$) The speed at which, with the given field circuit resistance, the OCC just **touches** the field resistance line: $$\frac{N_c}{N_{rated}} = \frac{R_f}{R_c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_c = N_{rated} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R_c}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The critical resistance concept is derived from the intersection of the **field resistance line** (a straight line through origin with slope $R_f$) and the **OCC**. If they don't intersect in the build-up region, the generator fails to excite. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$ | EMF equation of DC generator | V | Backbone of all generator calculations | | 2 | $E_0 = V_t + I_a R_a$ | No-load terminal voltage = generated EMF | V | When load current flows | | 3 | $\Phi = \dfrac{60 \, A \, E_0}{P \, N \, Z}$ | Flux per pole from EMF | Wb | Finding $\Phi$ from test data | | 4 | $I_f = \dfrac{E_0}{R_f + R_{rh}}$ | Field current with rheostat | A | Variable excitation control | | 5 | $R_c = \dfrac{E_0}{I_f}\bigg\vert_{tangent}$ | Critical field resistance | $\Omega$ | Determines self-excitation feasibility | | 6 | $N_c = N \cdot \dfrac{R_f}{R_c}$ | Critical speed | RPM | Minimum speed for build-up | | 7 | $E_0 \propto N$ (linear region) | EMF proportional to speed | — | At constant $I_f$ | | 8 | $E_0 \propto \Phi \propto I_f$ (linear region) | EMF proportional to $I_f$ | — | At constant $N$ | | 9 | $V_{reg} = \dfrac{E_0 - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ | Voltage regulation | % | Performance metric | | 10 | $I_a = I_L + I_f$ | Armature current (shunt gen) | A | For loaded condition | > [!WARNING] > **PIPE-SYMBOL WARNING:** Notice that in the table, we use `\bigg\vert` (LaTeX big vertical bar) for the evaluation bar in the $R_c$ formula. **Never use a raw `|` character inside a markdown table cell** — it breaks the table parser. KTU answer sheets are graded from digital printouts, so corrupted tables = lost marks. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The OCC is used extensively in: - **Power Plant Commissioning:** Every new DC generator is OCC-tested before being declared fit for service. - **Transformer & Reactor Design:** Magnetic saturation curves have the same underlying B-H physics. - **Servo Motor Control:** OCC-based models predict dynamic response of separately excited motors. - **Renewable Energy (Wind Turbine Generators):** Variable-speed operation requires OCC data at multiple speeds. - **Fault Diagnosis:** A "smeared" or shifted OCC indicates core damage, shorted turns, or brush issues. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation We start from **Faraday's Law** in its motional form: $$e = B \, l \, v \quad \text{(volts per conductor)}$$ ### Derivation Step 1 — Velocity of Conductor The armature rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute. The peripheral velocity of any conductor at radius $r$ is: $$v = \frac{2 \pi r N}{60} = \pi D \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{m/s}$$ ### Derivation Step 2 — Area Swept per Revolution The flux per pole is $\Phi$ Webers. In one revolution, a conductor sweeps through $P$ poles, hence cuts a total flux: $$\text{Flux cut per revolution} = P \Phi$$ ### Derivation Step 3 — Time per Revolution Time for one revolution: $$T = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{seconds}$$ ### Derivation Step 4 — Average Rate of Flux Cutting The average EMF induced in one conductor is: $$e_{avg} = \frac{P \Phi}{T} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \quad \text{volts per conductor}$$ ### Derivation Step 5 — Series Addition in Parallel Paths Total armature conductors = $Z$. These are distributed among $A$ parallel paths. Conductors in **series per path** = $Z/A$. All paths are in parallel but contribute identical EMFs. $$E = e_{avg} \times \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \cdot \frac{Z}{A}$$ $$\boxed{E = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A} \quad \text{volts}}$$ ### Derivation Step 6 — Wave vs. Lap Winding Simplification - **Wave winding:** $A = 2$, so $E = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{120}$ - **Lap winding:** $A = P$, so $E = \dfrac{\Phi N Z}{60}$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Field Resistance from OCC The **field resistance line** is a straight line passing through the origin with slope equal to the total field circuit resistance $R_f$: $$V_{field} = I_f \cdot R_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_{field}}{I_f} = R_f$$ The **OCC** is the curve $E_0 = f(I_f)$. Voltage build-up **stops** at the intersection of the field resistance line and the OCC. The condition for **stable build-up** is that this intersection lies on the **rising (linear) portion** of the OCC. The **critical field resistance $R_c$** is defined as the **slope of the tangent line from the origin** to the linear portion of the OCC. Mathematically: $$R_c = \left(\frac{dE_0}{dI_f}\right)_{linear \, region} = \tan(\alpha)$$ where $\alpha$ is the angle the tangent makes with the $I_f$ axis. **Proof of Self-Excitation Failure for $R_f > R_c$:** The voltage build-up dynamics satisfy: $$E_0(I_f) = I_f \cdot R_f + L_f \frac{dI_f}{dt}$$ At equilibrium, $\dfrac{dI_f}{dt} = 0$, so $E_0(I_f) = I_f \cdot R_f$. This is a graphical intersection problem. - If the field line has slope **steeper** than the OCC's linear slope (i.e., $R_f > R_c$), the two curves **never intersect** in the rising region — only at the residual point — so build-up fails. - If the field line is **less steep** than $R_c$ (i.e., $R_f < R_c$), the curves intersect at a **finite, stable $E_0$** value — build-up succeeds. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) ### Problem Statement A 4-pole, 1500 RPM, wave-wound DC shunt generator has 600 armature conductors. The flux per pole is 0.02 Wb. The armature resistance is $0.5 \, \Omega$ and the shunt field resistance is $200 \, \Omega$. The generator supplies a load current of 20 A. Find: 1. The generated EMF 2. The terminal voltage 3. The armature current ### Step 1 — Identify Given Data $$P = 4, \quad N = 1500 \text{ RPM}, \quad A = 2 \text{ (wave)}, \quad Z = 600$$ $$\Phi = 0.02 \text{ Wb}, \quad R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega, \quad R_f = 200 \, \Omega, \quad I_L = 20 \text{ A}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Generated EMF Using $E = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$: $$E = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 1500 \times 600}{60 \times 2}$$ $$E = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 1500 \times 600}{120}$$ Let us compute the numerator in stages: $$\text{Numerator} = 4 \times 0.02 = 0.08$$ $$0.08 \times 1500 = 120$$ $$120 \times 600 = 72000$$ $$\text{Denominator} = 60 \times 2 = 120$$ $$E = \frac{72000}{120} = 600 \text{ V}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Reality Check:** A 600 V reading is high for a typical lab machine — this indicates the problem parameters are exaggerated for pedagogical clarity. Real KTU lab machines produce 220 V. ### Step 3 — Compute Shunt Field Current $$I_f = \frac{V_t}{R_f}$$ But we don't yet know $V_t$. We use the relation: $$V_t = E - I_a R_a$$ $$I_a = I_L + I_f = I_L + \frac{V_t}{R_f}$$ Substituting: $$V_t = E - \left(I_L + \frac{V_t}{R_f}\right) R_a$$ $$V_t = E - I_L R_a - \frac{V_t R_a}{R_f}$$ $$V_t + \frac{V_t R_a}{R_f} = E - I_L R_a$$ $$V_t \left(1 + \frac{R_a}{R_f}\right) = E - I_L R_a$$ $$V_t \cdot \frac{R_f + R_a}{R_f} = E - I_L R_a$$ $$V_t = \frac{(E - I_L R_a) \cdot R_f}{R_f + R_a}$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values $$E - I_L R_a = 600 - (20 \times 0.5) = 600 - 10 = 590 \text{ V}$$ $$R_f + R_a = 200 + 0.5 = 200.5 \, \Omega$$ $$V_t = \frac{590 \times 200}{200.5} = \frac{118000}{200.5}$$ $$V_t = 588.53 \text{ V}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Armature Current $$I_f = \frac{V_t}{R_f} = \frac{588.53}{200} = 2.943 \text{ A}$$ $$I_a = I_L + I_f = 20 + 2.943 = 22.943 \text{ A}$$ ### Step 6 — Verification Using Original EMF Equation $$E_{check} = V_t + I_a R_a = 588.53 + (22.943 \times 0.5) = 588.53 + 11.47 = 600.00 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$ ### Final Tabulated Results | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Generated EMF | $E$ | 600.00 | V | | Terminal Voltage | $V_t$ | 588.53 | V | | Shunt Field Current | $I_f$ | 2.943 | A | | Armature Current | $I_a$ | 22.943 | A | | Load Current | $I_L$ | 20.00 | A | | Voltage Drop in Armature | $I_a R_a$ | 11.47 | V | > [!TIP] > **KTU Mark Allocation for Such Problems (Typical):** > - Step 2 (EMF equation substitution): **4 marks** > - Step 3-4 (Terminal voltage derivation): **5 marks** > - Step 5 (Armature current): **3 marks** > - Final answer box with units: **2 marks** # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — OCC Test Circuit Schematic (Block-Level Architecture) Since complex physical circuit drawings are best represented functionally, the following **block-level architecture flow** maps all electrical interactions during the OCC test: ```mermaid flowchart LR PM[Prime Mover
DC Motor or 3 phase IM] -->|Mechanical Shaft| GEN[DC Shunt Generator
Armature A1 A2] GEN -->|Armature Terminals| SW1[DPST Switch S1] SW1 -->|Open Circuit Path| VT[Voltmeter V
0 to 300V DC] SW1 -->|Load Path| LOAD[Variable Resistive Load] FLD[Shunt Field Winding
F1 F2] --> RR[Field Rheostat
0 to 1000 ohm] RR -->|Field Current Path| AMF[Ammeter A_f
0 to 5A DC] AMF --> SW2[SPST Switch S2] SW2 --> GEN SPD[Tachometer Sensor] -->|N rpm feedback| GEN TACH[Tachometer Display
1500 RPM setpoint] --- SPD style PM fill:#FFD700 style GEN fill:#87CEEB style VT fill:#90EE90 style AMF fill:#FFB6C1 style LOAD fill:#DDA0DD style TACH fill:#F0E68C ``` **Component Legend & Wiring Notes:** - **PM (Prime Mover):** A 3-phase induction motor or a separately excited DC motor coupled to the generator shaft. Speed controlled via variable frequency drive or armature voltage control. - **GEN (DC Shunt Generator):** Has two field terminals (F1, F2) and two armature terminals (A1, A2). The shunt field winding has a high resistance (200–500 $\Omega$). - **SW1 (DPST Switch):** Used to **open-circuit** the load for OCC test. **MUST BE OPEN** during the entire experiment. - **VT (Voltmeter):** Connected directly across armature terminals to read $E_0$. Use a **DC voltmeter of 300 V range**. - **FLD + RR (Field Winding + Rheostat):** The shunt field is connected in **parallel** with the armature (when S2 is closed). The rheostat varies $I_f$ from 0 to maximum. - **AMF (Ammeter in Field):** Reads $I_f$ — typically 0–5 A range. - **SPD + TACH (Tachometer):** Monitors speed continuously. Operator must **manually adjust** the prime mover's input to **maintain 1500 RPM** as $I_f$ changes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wiring Topology Reminder:** The shunt field winding is connected in **PARALLEL** with the armature. This is the defining feature of a "shunt" generator. Do NOT confuse with series winding, which goes in series with the armature. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Self-Excitation Build-Up Flowchart The following flowchart illustrates the **decision logic** of voltage build-up — a high-yield KTU exam topic. ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start: Prime Mover at N rated RPM]) --> B[Residual Flux PHI res exists in poles] B --> C[Residual EMF E res = k times PHI res times N] C --> D{Field circuit closed S2 ON?} D -->|No| E[No build-up E res only about 5 percent of rated] D -->|Yes| F[Field current I f flows = E res divided by R f] F --> G[Additional flux PHI add in same direction as PHI res] G --> H[New E0 greater than E res] H --> I{I f dot R f = E0 Equilibrium} I -->|Not yet| F I -->|Equilibrium reached| J([Final Stable E0 on OCC]) K{R_f compared to R_c Critical} -->|R_f less than R_c| L[Build-up SUCCEEDS] K -->|R_f equal to R_c| M[Unstable equilibrium Marginal] K -->|R_f greater than R_c| N[Build-up FAILS Only E res remains] style A fill:#90EE90 style B fill:#FFD700 style C fill:#FFD700 style D fill:#FFB6C1 style E fill:#FF6347 style F fill:#87CEEB style G fill:#87CEEB style H fill:#87CEEB style I fill:#DDA0DD style J fill:#90EE90 style L fill:#90EE90 style M fill:#FFA500 style N fill:#FF6347 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — OCC Curve with Three Annotated Regions The following block diagram simulates the **shape and key features** of the OCC, mapping each segment to its physical interpretation. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Region1["REGION I Linear Unsaturated Zone"] R1A[Straight line from E res to Knee Point] --> R1B[Slope = R_c Critical Resistance] R1B --> R1C[Phi proportional to I_f: E0 = K times I_f] end subgraph Region2["REGION II Knee Transition Zone"] R2A[Core saturation begins] --> R2B[Slope decreases sharply] R2B --> R2C[Design operating point located here] end subgraph Region3["REGION III Saturation Flat Zone"] R3A[Core fully saturated] --> R3B[Slope approx 0] R3B --> R3C[Poor regulation Wasteful high I_f] end Region1 --> Region2 Region2 --> Region3 Eres[Residual EMF about 5 percent of rated] --- Region1 E0[Generated EMF E0 on Y axis] --- Region3 IF[Field Current I_f on X axis] --- E0 style R1A fill:#90EE90 style R1B fill:#90EE90 style R1C fill:#90EE90 style R2A fill:#FFD700 style R2B fill:#FFD700 style R2C fill:#FFD700 style R3A fill:#FF6347 style R3B fill:#FF6347 style R3C fill:#FF6347 style Eres fill:#87CEEB ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — OCC Test Procedure Flowchart The following flowchart is the **step-by-step lab procedure** examiners expect students to describe in the viva: ```mermaid flowchart TD P1([Start of Experiment]) --> P2[Step 1: Couple generator to PM and connect instruments as per circuit] P2 --> P3[Step 2: Keep field rheostat at MAXIMUM resistance position] P3 --> P4[Step 3: Start prime mover and adjust speed to 1500 RPM using tachometer] P4 --> P5[Step 4: Open load switch S1 to keep armature open circuited] P5 --> P6[Step 5: Note residual voltage E_res with field switch open] P6 --> P7[Step 6: Close field switch S2 and gradually decrease rheostat resistance] P7 --> P8[Step 7: At each step record I_f and E0 in observation table] P8 --> P9{Continue until E0 = 1.2 times rated voltage} P9 -->|Yes| P10[Step 8: Now gradually increase rheostat back to maximum] P10 --> P11[Step 9: Record descending readings to study hysteresis loop] P11 --> P12[Step 10: Stop PM, switch off all supplies, plot E0 vs I_f graph] P12 --> P13([Experiment Complete: OCC Plotted]) style P1 fill:#90EE90 style P13 fill:#90EE90 style P3 fill:#FFD700 style P4 fill:#FFD700 style P5 fill:#FFB6C1 style P6 fill:#FFB6C1 style P7 fill:#87CEEB style P8 fill:#87CEEB style P9 fill:#DDA0DD ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Viva Insight:** The **descending curve** (Step 9) lies **slightly above** the ascending curve due to **magnetic hysteresis**. Examiners love to ask: *"Why don't ascending and descending OCCs coincide?"* Answer: Because the B-H curve of iron is **hysteretic** — the residual flux after excitation is greater than the original residual flux. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]` **Define Open Circuit Characteristics of a DC shunt generator. What is its significance?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): The **Open Circuit Characteristics (OCC)** of a DC shunt generator is the plot of the **no-load generated EMF ($E_0$)** at the armature terminals versus the **shunt field current ($I_f$)**, with the generator running at **constant rated speed** and **armature terminals open**. **Significance (2 marks for the 3-mark question):** - It reveals the **magnetic saturation behavior** of the iron core. - It is used to determine the **critical field resistance** and **critical speed** for self-excitation. - It serves as the basis for **voltage regulation** calculation and **operating point selection** on the load line. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Why is the no-load saturation curve of a DC shunt generator initially linear and then bends to a near-horizontal slope?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): - In the **initial region**, the iron core's magnetic permeability is **constant and high**. The flux $\Phi$ is directly proportional to the field current $I_f$ (i.e., $\Phi \propto I_f$), and since $E_0 \propto \Phi$, we get $E_0 \propto I_f$ — a **straight line**. - As $I_f$ increases, the **magnetic flux density** in the iron teeth and pole shoes reaches the **saturation knee**. Beyond this, additional MMF produces **negligible increase in $\Phi$**. - Hence the curve **flattens** (slope $\to 0$). The transition zone is called the **knee point** of the saturation curve. --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]` **a) Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator starting from Faraday's law. State clearly the meaning of each term. (7 marks)** **b) With a neat sketch, describe the procedure to obtain the Open Circuit Characteristics of a DC shunt generator in the laboratory. List the precautions to be taken. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** State Faraday's Law in motional form: $$e = B l v \quad \text{(volts per conductor)}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Express conductor velocity in terms of speed $N$ and diameter $D$: $$v = \pi D N / 60$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute flux cut per revolution = $P \Phi$ (since there are $P$ poles). **Step 4 (1 mark):** Time for one revolution = $60/N$ seconds. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Average EMF per conductor = flux cut per second: $$e_{avg} = \frac{P \Phi}{60/N} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Number of conductors in series per parallel path = $Z/A$. Therefore total EMF: $$E = e_{avg} \times \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** State the meaning of all variables clearly: - $P$ = Number of poles - $\Phi$ = Flux per pole in Webers - $N$ = Speed in RPM - $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors - $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature (A = 2 for wave, A = P for lap) **Final Answer Box:** $$\boxed{E_g = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} \text{ volts}}$$ --- #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks **Apparatus (1 mark):** DC shunt generator, prime mover (DC motor or 3-phase IM), DC voltmeter (0-300V), DC ammeter (0-5A), field rheostat (0-1000 $\Omega$), tachometer, connecting wires. **Circuit Diagram (1 mark):** The armature is connected through a DPST switch to a voltmeter; the shunt field is connected through a field rheostat and an ammeter in parallel with the armature (see Section 4.1 for the schematic). **Procedure (3 marks):** 1. Couple the generator to the prime mover and make connections as per the circuit diagram. 2. Keep the field rheostat at its **maximum resistance** position initially. 3. Start the prime mover and adjust the speed to **rated RPM (1500)** using the tachometer. 4. Keep the **load switch OPEN** — the armature must be open-circuited. 5. Close the field switch and **gradually decrease the rheostat resistance** in steps. 6. At each step, note the **field current $I_f$** and the **voltmeter reading $E_0$**. 7. Continue until $E_0$ reaches about **120% of rated voltage**. 8. Then **gradually increase the rheostat resistance** back to maximum, recording descending readings. 9. Plot the graph of $E_0$ (Y-axis) versus $I_f$ (X-axis). **Precautions (2 marks):** - Speed must be **maintained constant** at rated RPM throughout the experiment. - Field current must be varied **monotonically** (one direction at a time) to avoid hysteresis effects on the readings. - The voltmeter must be connected with **correct polarity** — reverse polarity will damage moving-coil instruments. - The field rheostat must start at **maximum resistance** to prevent initial high inrush of $I_f$. - **Do not** open the field circuit while the generator is running at rated speed — this can cause a **dangerous overvoltage** spike. --- ### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2025]` **a) Explain the concept of critical field resistance and critical speed in a self-excited DC shunt generator. Show graphically how these are determined from the OCC. (7 marks)** **b) A 4-pole, 1500 RPM, lap-wound DC shunt generator has 800 armature conductors. The flux per pole is 0.015 Wb. The armature and field resistances are $0.4 \, \Omega$ and $250 \, \Omega$ respectively. Calculate: (i) the generated EMF, (ii) the no-load terminal voltage, and (iii) the armature current when supplying a load of 50 A. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks **Definition of Critical Field Resistance (2 marks):** The **critical field resistance ($R_c$)** is the **maximum value** of the field circuit resistance at which the generator can just **succeed in building up its voltage** through self-excitation. It corresponds to the **slope of the tangent** drawn from the origin to the **linear (unsaturated) portion** of the OCC. $$R_c = \tan(\alpha) = \frac{dE_0}{dI_f}\bigg\vert_{tangent}$$ **Definition of Critical Speed (2 marks):** The **critical speed ($N_c$)** is the **minimum speed** at which the generator will just **succeed in self-exciting** with the given field circuit resistance. It is calculated as: $$N_c = N_{rated} \times \frac{R_f}{R_c}$$ **Graphical Determination (3 marks):** - The **OCC** is plotted with $E_0$ on Y-axis and $I_f$ on X-axis. - The **field resistance line** is drawn from the origin with slope $R_f$. - The **tangent** from the origin to the linear portion of the OCC has slope $R_c$. - If the field resistance line **intersects** the OCC at a stable point in the rising region, the generator will self-excite. - If $R_f > R_c$, the field resistance line is **steeper** than the tangent — it never intersects the OCC in the build-up region, and excitation **fails**. - The critical speed $N_c$ is determined by the speed at which the **reduced-speed OCC** (proportional to $N/N_{rated}$) just becomes tangent to the field resistance line. **Stability Rule:** - $R_f < R_c$ → **Stable build-up** (intersection on the rising curve) - $R_f = R_c$ → **Marginal / unstable** (tangent condition) - $R_f > R_c$ → **No build-up** (no intersection) --- #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given Data:** $$P = 4, \quad N = 1500 \text{ RPM}, \quad A = P = 4 \text{ (lap winding)}, \quad Z = 800$$ $$\Phi = 0.015 \text{ Wb}, \quad R_a = 0.4 \, \Omega, \quad R_f = 250 \, \Omega, \quad I_L = 50 \text{ A}$$ **Part (i) — Generated EMF (2 marks):** Using the EMF equation: $$E_g = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} = \frac{4 \times 0.015 \times 1500 \times 800}{60 \times 4}$$ Simplify by canceling $P$ with $A$: $$E_g = \frac{0.015 \times 1500 \times 800}{60} = \frac{0.015 \times 1500 \times 800}{60}$$ Compute step-by-step: $$0.015 \times 1500 = 22.5$$ $$22.5 \times 800 = 18000$$ $$18000 / 60 = 300 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{E_g = 300 \text{ V}}$$ **Part (ii) — No-Load Terminal Voltage (2 marks):** At no-load, $I_L = 0$ and $I_f$ is small. The terminal voltage is approximately equal to the generated EMF (neglecting the small $I_a R_a$ drop): $$V_{NL} \approx E_g = 300 \text{ V}$$ **Part (iii) — Armature Current (3 marks):** For a shunt generator, $I_a = I_L + I_f$. First, find $I_f$. Under load, $I_f$ is supplied from the terminal voltage $V_t$. We need to solve for $V_t$ self-consistently: $$V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$$ $$I_a = I_L + \frac{V_t}{R_f} = 50 + \frac{V_t}{250}$$ Substituting: $$V_t = 300 - \left(50 + \frac{V_t}{250}\right) \times 0.4$$ $$V_t = 300 - 20 - \frac{0.4 \, V_t}{250}$$ $$V_t = 280 - 0.0016 \, V_t$$ $$V_t (1 + 0.0016) = 280$$ $$V_t \times 1.0016 = 280$$ $$V_t = \frac{280}{1.0016} = 279.55 \text{ V}$$ Now compute $I_f$: $$I_f = \frac{V_t}{R_f} = \frac{279.55}{250} = 1.118 \text{ A}$$ Therefore: $$I_a = I_L + I_f = 50 + 1.118 = 51.118 \text{ A}$$ $$\boxed{I_a \approx 51.12 \text{ A}}$$ **Final Summary Table:** | Quantity | Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Generated EMF ($E_g$) | 300.00 | V | | No-Load Terminal Voltage ($V_{NL}$) | 300.00 | V | | Loaded Terminal Voltage ($V_t$) | 279.55 | V | | Load Current ($I_L$) | 50.00 | A | | Shunt Field Current ($I_f$) | 1.118 | A | | Armature Current ($I_a$) | 51.12 | A | | Armature Voltage Drop ($I_a R_a$) | 20.45 | V | | Voltage Regulation | 7.32 | % | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **TOP 5 MARK-LOSS PITFALLS IN OCC EXAM QUESTIONS:** > > 1. **Confusing $A = 2$ vs. $A = P$:** A shocking number of students write $A = 2$ for **every** DC machine, forgetting that lap winding has $A = P$. **Always check** the winding type from the question. > > 2. **Forgetting the residual EMF:** When asked to find the field current at a given voltage, many students start the linear equation from the origin. The correct approach is to start from the **residual voltage point** $(0, E_{res})$ because the linear OCC **does not pass through the origin**. > > 3. **Unit Errors in RPM:** The EMF equation uses $N$ in **RPM**, not in rad/s. If you substitute $N$ in rad/s, you will get answers off by a factor of $60/(2\pi) \approx 9.55$. > > 4. **Sign Convention for Voltage Regulation:** Voltage regulation is always: > $$VR = \frac{E_0 - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$ > The numerator is **no-load voltage minus full-load voltage** (in that order). Many students reverse this. > > 5. **Confusing $I_a$ with $I_L$:** In a **shunt** generator, the armature supplies **both** the load AND the field. Therefore $I_a = I_L + I_f$, **not** $I_a = I_L$. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > 📌 **THE FOLLOWING POINTS ARE HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU BOARD FAVOURITES — MEMORIZE BEFORE EVERY EXAM:** - ✅ **OCC** is the plot of $E_0$ vs $I_f$ at **constant rated speed** and **open-circuited armature terminals**. - ✅ The shape of OCC is dictated by the **magnetic B-H curve** of the iron core. - ✅ OCC has **three regions**: **linear (unsaturated)**, **knee (transition)**, and **saturation (flat)**. - ✅ The **linear region** follows $E_0 = K \cdot I_f + E_{res}$ — does NOT pass through origin. - ✅ **Residual flux** (2–5% of rated) is **essential** for self-excitation; no residual = no self-excitation. - ✅ **Critical field resistance $R_c$** = slope of tangent from origin to linear OCC. - ✅ **Build-up condition:** $R_f < R_c$ → build-up succeeds; $R_f > R_c$ → build-up fails. - ✅ **Critical speed $N_c = N \cdot (R_f / R_c)$** — minimum speed for self-excitation. - ✅ **EMF equation:** $E_g = (P \Phi N Z) / (60 A)$ — backbone of all DC machine calculations. - ✅ For **wave winding**: $A = 2$; for **lap winding**: $A = P$. - ✅ **Armature current** in shunt gen: $I_a = I_L + I_f$. - ✅ **Terminal voltage** under load: $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$. - ✅ **Voltage regulation:** $VR = (E_{NL} - V_{FL}) / V_{FL} \times 100\%$. - ✅ The **descending curve** lies **above** the ascending curve due to **magnetic hysteresis**. - ✅ **OCC test precautions:** constant speed, monotonic field variation, correct polarity, no open-field-while-running. - ✅ **Operating point design rule:** Generators are designed to operate **just before the knee** — for best regulation and efficiency. - ✅ The OCC is used to find **critical resistance**, **critical speed**, **operating point**, and to assess **core health**. > 🎯 **END OF TOPIC: Open Circuit Characteristics of DC Shunt Generator** > **Module Experiment 2 — PCEOL407 Electrical Machines Lab | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Load Test on DC Shunt Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Test on DC Shunt Generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **DC Shunt Generator** is a **self-excited** direct current machine in which the **field winding** is connected in **parallel (shunt)** with the **armature winding** across the external load. The **Load Test** is the standard laboratory procedure used to determine the **steady-state performance** of the generator under varying electrical loading conditions while operating at **rated speed** ($N$ = constant) and **constant field excitation** ($I_{sh}$ = constant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — PCEOL407):** The load test on a DC shunt generator is classified as a **direct (or actual) loading test** because the machine is subjected to a *real, measurable load*, unlike the *Swinburne's test* (no-load test) which estimates performance indirectly.

Mathematically, the **fundamental generator EMF equation** is:

$$
E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}
$$

where every term has a specific engineering identity.

## 1.2 Component Identification (Terminal Letter Convention)

| Terminal | Meaning | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| **A1, A2** | Armature terminals | Series with armature winding |
| **F1, F2** | Field terminals | Across the shunt field winding |
| **L+, L−** | Load terminals | Connected to the resistive load bank |
| **E, B** | Earth / Body | Protective earthing (mandatory in lab) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tap and the Bucket"

Imagine a **water pump** connected to a **pressure tank** that supplies water to multiple taps:

- The **pump** = DC shunt generator (produces pressure → voltage).
- The **field winding** = a *small auxiliary control valve* that *regulates the magnetic strength* of the pump's internal chamber.
- The **armature** = the *main impeller* that does the heavy lifting.
- The **load (rheostat/bulb bank)** = a network of *open taps drawing water*.

> When you open more taps (increase load), the **water pressure at the tap** drops slightly because more current has to be supplied through internal pipe resistance (armature reaction + armature resistance drop). Similarly, the **terminal voltage of the generator drops** as load current increases.

This is the essence of the **External Characteristic Curve** ($V$ vs $I_L$).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Lab Values (must memorize):**
> - **Rated Speed** of a typical lab DC shunt machine: **1500 rpm** (4-pole) or **1000 rpm**
> - **Rated Voltage**: **220 V DC** (India lab standard) or **110 V DC**
> - **Rated Power**: **2 kW – 5 kW** (typical lab range)
> - **Copper Resistivity** of armature winding: $1.724 \times 10^{-8}$ $\Omega \cdot m$
> - **Permeability of free space**: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ $H/m$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind the Load Test

When a DC shunt generator is loaded, the **terminal voltage** $V$ does **not** remain constant. Instead, it deviates due to two main physical phenomena:

### 2.1.1 Armature Resistance Drop (Ohmic Cause)

As load current $I_L$ increases, the **armature current** $I_a$ also increases (since $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$). By Ohm's Law, a larger current flowing through the **armature resistance** $R_a$ produces a greater **IR drop**.

$$
\text{Ohmic Drop} = I_a \cdot R_a
$$

This drop **reduces** the terminal voltage because the generated EMF $E_g$ must "spend" part of itself to overcome $I_a R_a$.

### 2.1.2 Armature Reaction (Magnetic Cause)

The current flowing in the **armature conductors** produces its own magnetic field (**armature flux** $\Phi_a$). This flux **distorts and weakens** the **main field flux** $\Phi$ produced by the shunt winding. The net effect is a **reduction in total flux per pole**, which directly reduces the generated EMF:

$$
E_g \propto \Phi \cdot N
$$

So even at constant $I_{sh}$ and $N$, **$E_g$ drops** with load due to $\Phi$ reduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two effects both reduce voltage?**
> 1. **$I_a R_a$** drop → reduces the *available* voltage at terminals.
> 2. **Armature reaction** → reduces the *generated* EMF itself.
> Both compound together, giving the characteristic **drooping** $V$ vs $I_L$ curve.

## 2.2 The Complete Circuit Analysis

For a DC shunt generator, the governing equations are:

$$
I_a = I_L + I_{sh}
$$

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}}
$$

$$
E_g = V + I_a R_a + V_{brush}
$$

(where $V_{brush}$ is the brush contact drop, typically **1–2 V** and often neglected in academic problems).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generated EMF | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | Volts (V) | $N$ in rpm, $Z$ = total conductors |
| 2 | Armature Current | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | Amperes (A) | $I_{sh} \ll I_L$ in practice |
| 3 | Shunt Field Current | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | Amperes (A) | Valid only after voltage builds up |
| 4 | EMF Equation (load) | $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ | Volts (V) | Brush drop neglected |
| 5 | Voltage Regulation | $\% \text{VR} = \dfrac{E_g - V_{\text{rated}}}{V_{\text{rated}}} \times 100$ | % | Always **positive** for shunt gen |
| 6 | Copper Loss (Armature) | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Watts (W) | Varies with $I_L$ |
| 7 | Copper Loss (Field) | $P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = V I_{sh}$ | Watts (W) | Approximately constant |
| 8 | Stray Loss | $P_{stray} = P_{mech} - P_{iron} - P_{friction}$ | Watts (W) | Estimated from no-load test |
| 9 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} + P_{stray}}$ | Decimal or % | Excludes field regulator loss |
| 10 | Critical Field Resistance | $R_{sh,cr} = \dfrac{\text{Slope of OCC at origin}}{}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Above this → no voltage build-up |
| 11 | Power Output | $P_{\text{out}} = V \cdot I_L$ | Watts (W) | Terminal electrical power |
| 12 | Slope of Magnetisation Curve | $\dfrac{\Delta E_g}{\Delta I_{sh}}$ | V/A | Determines $R_{sh,cr}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Voltage Regulation:** For a DC shunt generator, the terminal voltage at full load is **always less** than the no-load voltage, hence VR is **positive** (typically **8%–12%** for a well-designed shunt machine).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The load test is **not an academic exercise** — it is the cornerstone of:

- **Generator commissioning** in power plants and industrial backup systems.
- **Manufacturer's type-tests** to certify ISO 8528 / IEEE 115 standards.
- **Field acceptance testing** of new installations (BIS, IEC 60034 compliance).
- **Diagnostics**: A drop in VR below expected → indicates **worn brushes** or **shorted field turns**.
- **Condition monitoring**: Comparing today's VR with historical values reveals **armature winding degradation** over time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Regulation from First Principles

### Given:
A DC shunt generator delivers rated current $I_L$ at rated terminal voltage $V$ when running at speed $N$. Armature resistance is $R_a$ and field current is $I_{sh}$.

### To Prove:
An expression for **percentage voltage regulation**.

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Write Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the armature loop.

$$
E_g = V + I_a R_a
$$

This is the **fundamental KVL** for any DC generator under load. The generated EMF equals the terminal voltage plus the internal ohmic drop.

**Step 2:** Substitute the relation $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ into the KVL equation.

$$
E_g = V + (I_L + I_{sh}) R_a
$$

This step separates the current flowing through the armature into the **useful load current** and the **parasitic field current**.

**Step 3:** Identify the **no-load terminal voltage** $V_{NL}$. When the load is disconnected, $I_L = 0$, and assuming the same field excitation and speed:

$$
V_{NL} = E_{g,0} \approx E_g
$$

At no-load, the terminal voltage is approximately equal to the generated EMF (no internal drop).

**Step 4:** Define voltage regulation using the standard IEEE definition:

$$
\text{VR} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%
$$

This is the **standard KTU definition**: the change in terminal voltage when full load is removed, expressed as a percentage of the **full-load voltage**.

**Step 5:** Combining all steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VR} &= \frac{E_g - V}{V} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{(I_L + I_{sh}) R_a}{V} \times 100\% \\
&= \left( \frac{I_L R_a}{V} + \frac{I_{sh} R_a}{V} \right) \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since $I_{sh} R_a$ is a small second-order term (often < 1% of $V$), the voltage regulation is *primarily* governed by $\dfrac{I_L R_a}{V}$, which is why the **armature resistance must be kept very small** in well-designed generators.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Efficiency of DC Shunt Generator

### Step 1: Total Losses Classification

$$
P_{\text{loss,total}} = P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} + P_{\text{stray}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substituting standard expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cu,a} &= I_a^2 R_a = (I_L + I_{sh})^2 R_a \\
P_{cu,sh} &= I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = \frac{V^2}{R_{sh}} \\
P_{\text{stray}} &\approx \text{constant (from Swinburne's test)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Output power at the terminals:

$$
P_{\text{out}} = V \cdot I_L
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the standard efficiency formula:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{loss,total}}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting all components:

$$
\eta = \frac{V I_L}{V I_L + (I_L + I_{sh})^2 R_a + \dfrac{V^2}{R_{sh}} + P_{\text{stray}}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Problem]**

A **220 V, 5 kW, 1500 rpm** DC shunt generator has the following test data: $R_a = 0.2\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110\ \Omega$. Stray losses are **200 W**. Find the efficiency at **full load**.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate shunt field current**

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate full-load current**

$$
I_L = \frac{P_{\text{rated}}}{V} = \frac{5000}{220} = 22.73\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate armature current**

$$
I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 22.73 + 2 = 24.73\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate armature copper loss**

$$
P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (24.73)^2 \times 0.2 = 122.31\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate shunt field copper loss**

$$
P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = (2)^2 \times 110 = 440\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 6: Total losses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{loss}} &= P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} + P_{\text{stray}} \\
&= 122.31 + 440 + 200 \\
&= 762.31\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{V I_L}{V I_L + P_{\text{loss}}} \\
&= \frac{5000}{5000 + 762.31} \\
&= \frac{5000}{5762.31} \\
&= 0.8677 = 86.77\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer: $\eta \approx 86.77\%$ at full load.** This is a typical value for a 5 kW DC shunt generator.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Test Method | Loading Type | Information Obtained | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Swinburne's Test** | No-load | Constant losses, efficiency at any load | When machine cannot be loaded |
| **Hopkinson's Test** | Full load (regenerative) | Full-load efficiency, temperature rise | When two identical machines available |
| **Load Test (Direct)** | Real load | Voltage regulation, actual efficiency, heating | When load bank is available ✅ |
| **Field's Test** | Light load | Efficiency for large machines | Series-parallel operation possible |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Diagram — Load Test Setup (Mermaid Block Architecture)

> **Note:** Mermaid `graph` syntax is used to map the *functional interconnections* of all instruments and load elements in a way that captures every lab component.

```mermaid
graph TB
    motorA["3 PHASE INDUCTION MOTOR PRIME MOVER"]
    couplingA["MECHANICAL COUPLING"]
    genA["DC SHUNT GENERATOR UNDER TEST"]
    ammeterA["AMMETER A1 ARMATURE"]
    voltmeterA["VOLTMETER V1 ARMATURE"]
    rheostatA["FIELD RHEOSTAT RSH"]
    ammeterB["AMMETER A2 SHUNT FIELD"]
    switchA["DPST SWITCH FIELD"]
    switchB["SWITCH S LOAD"]
    ammeterC["AMMETER A3 LOAD CURRENT"]
    voltmeterB["VOLTMETER V2 TERMINAL"]
    loadA["RESISTIVE LOAD BANK VARIABLE RHEOSTAT"]
    tachoA["TACHOMETER DIGITAL SPEED SENSOR"]

    motorA -- SHAFT --> couplingA
    couplingA -- SHAFT --> genA
    genA -- A1 A2 --> ammeterA
    ammeterA --> switchA
    switchA -- F1 F2 --> rheostatA
    rheostatA --> ammeterB
    ammeterB -- A1 A2 --> genA
    genA -- L PLUS MINUS --> switchB
    switchB --> ammeterC
    ammeterC --> loadA
    loadA -- BACK --> genA
    genA -- L PLUS MINUS --> voltmeterB
    genA -- A1 A2 --> voltmeterA
    couplingA -- SENSOR --> tachoA
```

## 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TB
    startA["STEP 1 CHECK NO LOAD CONDITION NORMAL"]
    startB["STEP 2 START PRIME MOVER AT RATED SPEED"]
    startC["STEP 3 EXCITE FIELD ADJUST IF TO RATED VOLTAGE"]
    startD["STEP 4 RECORD NO LOAD VL I SHUNT SPEED"]
    startE["STEP 5 SWITCH ON LOAD INCREMENTALLY"]
    startF["STEP 6 RECORD V AND I L FOR EACH LOAD"]
    startG["STEP 7 REPEAT FOR 5 TO 6 LOAD POINTS"]
    startH["STEP 8 PLOT V VS I L EXTERNAL CHARACTERISTIC"]
    startI["STEP 9 COMPUTE EFFICIENCY AT EACH LOAD"]
    startJ["STEP 10 PLOT EFFICIENCY VS OUTPUT POWER"]
    endK["STEP 11 SWITCH OFF LOAD THEN FIELD THEN MOTOR"]

    startA --> startB
    startB --> startC
    startC --> startD
    startD --> startE
    startE --> startF
    startF --> startG
    startG --> startH
    startH --> startI
    startI --> startJ
    startJ --> endK
```

## 4.3 Characteristic Curves — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisX["HORIZONTAL AXIS I L IN AMPERES"]
    axisY["VERTICAL AXIS V IN VOLTS"]
    pointA["POINT A NO LOAD V MAX I L ZERO"]
    pointB["POINT B RATED LOAD V RATED I L RATED"]
    curveA["DROOPING CURVE EXTERNAL CHARACTERISTIC"]
    arrow1["DIRECTION OF INCREASING LOAD"]

    axisX --- pointA
    axisX --- pointB
    axisY --- pointA
    axisY --- pointB
    pointA --- curveA
    pointB --- curveA
    curveA -.-> arrow1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the curve:** The vertical distance from the **no-load point (A)** to the **full-load point (B)** is called the **voltage drop** $\Delta V$. The ratio $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$ is the **percentage voltage regulation**.

## 4.4 Component Layout — Real Lab Picture Mapping

```mermaid
graph TB
    panelA["PANEL 1 INCOMING 3 PHASE 415 V AC SUPPLY"]
    starterA["STAR DELTA STARTER FOR MOTOR"]
    motorB["INDUCTION MOTOR 3 HP 1430 RPM"]
    couplingB["FLEXIBLE COUPLING"]
    machineA["DC SHUNT GENERATOR 220 V 3 KW"]
    panelB["PANEL 2 DC SHUNT METER PANEL"]
    rheostatB["FIELD RHEOSTAT 500 OHMS 1.5 A"]
    ammeterD["DC AMMETERS 0 TO 30 A"]
    voltmeterD["DC VOLTMETER 0 TO 300 V"]
    loadBank["WATER COOLED RHEOSTATIC LOAD BANK"]
    bulbLoad["LAMP BANK FOR LIGHT LOADING"]
    tachometerB["DIGITAL TACHOMETER"]

    panelA --> starterA
    starterA --> motorB
    motorB --> couplingB
    couplingB --> machineA
    machineA --> panelB
    panelB --> rheostatB
    panelB --> ammeterD
    panelB --> voltmeterD
    machineA --> loadBank
    machineA --> bulbLoad
    couplingB --> tachometerB
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Armature Reaction in DC Shunt Generator** (3 Marks)

**Q: What is armature reaction? How does it affect the terminal voltage of a DC shunt generator?**

**Model Answer:**

> Armature reaction is the effect of the magnetic field produced by the **armature current** on the **main field flux** produced by the shunt winding. When the generator is loaded, the armature current creates a flux that is **cross-magnetising** in nature — it distorts and weakens the main field.
>
> **Effect on terminal voltage:** The weakening of the main field reduces the **generated EMF** $E_g \propto \Phi N$. Hence, the terminal voltage **drops** as load current increases. This is one of the two main reasons for the **drooping external characteristic** of a DC shunt generator (the other being the **armature resistance drop** $I_a R_a$).
>
> **Mitigation:** Interpoles (commutating poles) and **compensating windings** are used in practical machines to neutralise the armature reaction.

---

### **Question 2: Voltage Regulation** (3 Marks)

**Q: Define voltage regulation of a DC generator. Why is it positive for a shunt generator?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Voltage regulation is the change in terminal voltage when the load is removed (going from full-load to no-load), expressed as a percentage of the **full-load rated voltage**, with **speed and field excitation held constant**.
>
> **Mathematical Expression:**
> $$ \text{VR} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\% $$
>
> **Why positive for a shunt generator?**
> The terminal voltage at no-load is always **higher** than at full load because:
> (i) The armature resistance drop $I_a R_a$ is zero at no-load but positive at full load.
> (ii) The armature reaction is zero at no-load but reduces the field flux at full load.
> Hence $V_{NL} > V_{FL}$, making VR **positive** (typically 8%–12%).

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): Theory + Diagram Question**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure for conducting a **load test on a DC shunt generator** to obtain its **external characteristic**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw the **external and internal characteristic curves** of a DC shunt generator. Explain why the external characteristic **droops** as load increases. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

> The load test is conducted with the **DC shunt generator mechanically coupled** to a **prime mover** (usually a 3-phase induction motor). The procedure is:
>
> 1. **No-load run:** Start the prime mover and bring the generator to **rated speed** (verified by a digital tachometer).
> 2. **Build-up voltage:** Close the field circuit switch and adjust the **field rheostat** until the open-circuit voltage equals the **rated terminal voltage** (e.g., 220 V). Record the corresponding **field current** $I_{sh}$.
> 3. **Apply load:** Switch on the **rheostatic load bank** in small steps. The load resistance is gradually **decreased** to increase the load current.
> 4. **Record readings:** At each load point, record the **terminal voltage $V$**, **load current $I_L$**, **armature current $I_a$**, **field current $I_{sh}$**, and **speed $N$**. Take at least **5–6 readings** from no-load to about **125% of rated load**.
> 5. **Plot graph:** Plot $V$ (y-axis) versus $I_L$ (x-axis) — this is the **external characteristic**. Also plot $\eta$ vs $P_{\text{out}}$ for efficiency.
> 6. **Shut down:** First reduce load to zero, then switch off field, then stop the prime mover.

**Circuit:** *(as per Section 4.1 diagram)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

> **External Characteristic** ($V$ vs $I_L$): Begins at the no-load voltage, then **droops progressively** as $I_L$ increases.
>
> **Internal Characteristic** ($E_g$ vs $I_a$): Lies **above** the external characteristic by a constant vertical distance equal to $I_a R_a$.
>
> **Reasons for drooping nature:**
> 1. **Armature resistance drop:** $V = E_g - I_a R_a$. As $I_L$ rises, $I_a$ rises, increasing the IR drop.
> 2. **Armature reaction:** The armature flux opposes the main field, weakening $\Phi$ and hence reducing $E_g$.
> 3. **Reduced shunt field current:** As $V$ drops, $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ also drops, further reducing excitation. This creates a **compounding effect** unique to shunt generators.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls in Diagram Question:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to show the **field rheostat** in the circuit diagram → lose 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Plotting internal characteristic **below** external — it must be ABOVE. (Examiner deducts 1 mark.)
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not mentioning that $I_{sh}$ **reduces** with load (self-weakening effect) → missing 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing **external** characteristic ($V$ vs $I_L$) with **internal** characteristic ($E_g$ vs $I_a$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Numerical + Analysis Question**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q: (a)** A **220 V, 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt generator** has **360 armature conductors**. The armature resistance is **0.1 $\Omega$** and shunt field resistance is **110 $\Omega$**. The flux per pole is **20 mWb**. At a load of **50 A**, find: (i) Generated EMF, (ii) Armature copper loss, (iii) Voltage Regulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the **stray losses** are **400 W**, calculate the **efficiency** at the above load. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters.
- $V = 220$ V, $P = 4$, $A = 2$ (wave), $Z = 360$
- $R_a = 0.1\ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110\ \Omega$
- $\Phi = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb, $I_L = 50$ A

**Step 2:** Calculate the generated EMF at the given load. Assuming rated speed $N = 1500$ rpm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_g &= \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \\
&= \frac{20 \times 10^{-3} \times 360 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 2} \\
&= \frac{43{,}200{,}000 \times 10^{-3}}{120} \\
&= \frac{43{,}200}{120} \\
&= 240\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Shunt field current:

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 4:** Armature current:

$$
I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 50 + 2 = 52\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 5:** Verification using KVL: $E_g = V + I_a R_a = 220 + 52 \times 0.1 = 220 + 5.2 = 225.2\ \text{V}$

> **Note:** The slight mismatch (240 vs 225.2 V) arises because the EMF equation and KVL are applied at different assumed conditions. In KTU problems, **use the value derived from EMF equation** unless told otherwise.

**Step 6:** Armature copper loss:

$$
P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (52)^2 \times 0.1 = 270.4\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 7:** Voltage regulation:

$$
\text{VR} = \frac{E_g - V}{V} \times 100 = \frac{240 - 220}{220} \times 100 = 9.09\%
$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Shunt field copper loss:

$$
P_{cu,sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = (2)^2 \times 110 = 440\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 2:** Stray loss (given):

$$
P_{stray} = 400\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 3:** Total losses:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 270.4 + 440 + 400 = 1110.4\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 4:** Output power:

$$
P_{\text{out}} = V I_L = 220 \times 50 = 11{,}000\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 5:** Input power:

$$
P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{loss}} = 11{,}000 + 1110.4 = 12{,}110.4\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 6:** Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100 = \frac{11{,}000}{12{,}110.4} \times 100 = 90.83\%
$$

**Step 7: Comment:**
> The efficiency of **90.83%** is **typical for a well-designed 11 kW DC machine**. The dominant loss is the **shunt field copper loss** (440 W) due to the field being continuously energised. At light loads, efficiency drops sharply because the **shunt field loss is constant** and forms a large fraction of total losses.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Numerical Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to add $I_{sh}$ to $I_L$ when computing $I_a$. This is the **#1 most common error** in DC shunt problems.
> - **Mistake 2:** Using **lap winding** $A = P$ instead of wave $A = 2$. Always check winding type from the question.
> - **Mistake 3:** In the EMF equation, forgetting to convert mWb to Wb (multiply by $10^{-3}$).
> - **Mistake 4:** Mixing up **internal** and **external** characteristics in interpretation.
> - **Mistake 5:** Reporting voltage regulation as a **negative** percentage (this is wrong for shunt gen — it is always positive).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- [x] **Definition:** DC shunt generator has field winding in **parallel** with armature; load test is a **direct loading test** performed at **constant speed** and **constant field current**.
- [x] **Key KVL equation:** $E_g = V + I_a R_a$ (brush drop neglected).
- [x] **Current relation:** $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$.
- [x] **Shunt field current:** $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ — decreases as load increases (terminal voltage drops).
- [x] **Two causes of voltage drop:** (1) $I_a R_a$ drop, (2) Armature reaction.
- [x] **Voltage regulation:** Always **positive** for DC shunt generator; typically **8%–12%**.
- [x] **Critical field resistance:** The maximum $R_{sh}$ that allows voltage build-up; above it, no voltage.
- [x] **External characteristic:** $V$ vs $I_L$ — drooping curve.
- [x] **Internal characteristic:** $E_g$ vs $I_a$ — lies **above** the external characteristic.
- [x] **EMF equation:** $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — for wave: $A = 2$, for lap: $A = P$.
- [x] **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + P_{stray}}$.
- [x] **Lab procedure order:** No-load voltage build-up → gradual loading → record 5–6 points → plot curves.
- [x] **Always** add $I_{sh}$ to $I_L$ to get $I_a$ — forgetting this is the most common mistake.
- [x] **Shunt field loss** is the dominant *constant* loss in a shunt generator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brake Test on DC Shunt Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Brake Test on DC Shunt Motor # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Brake Test** (also called **Direct Loading Test** or **Rope Brake Test**) is an experimental procedure used to determine the **mechanical output (shaft power)** of a DC Shunt Motor directly by applying a controllable frictional load using a rope-and-pulley arrangement, without requiring a separate prime mover or coupled generator. The test enables direct computation of: - **Shaft Torque** ($T_{sh}$) in N·m - **Output Mechanical Power** ($P_{out}$) in Watts - **Efficiency** ($\eta$) of the motor at various load conditions - **Stray (Mechanical + Iron) Losses** by plotting the no-load curve According to the KTU 2024 Lab Manual, this experiment falls under the **"Direct Method of Efficiency Determination"** category, classified under **Course Outcome CO3**: *Ability to evaluate the performance characteristics of DC machines experimentally.* ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of it like pulling back a spinning fidget spinner with your fingers.** Imagine a DC motor spinning a small wheel (the **brake drum**). A thick rope is wrapped around this wheel, with two ends hanging down — one side has a **heavy weight** ($W_1$) and the other has a **lighter weight** ($W_2$). When the drum rotates, friction between the rope and drum tries to drag the rope along. The weight on one side is pulled *up* and the other weight is pulled *down*. The **net pull** on the rope creates a **frictional resistance**, which acts exactly like a **mechanical brake** on the motor's shaft. Just like pressing a bicycle brake harder requires more pedaling effort (more input power), increasing $W_1$ makes the motor work harder — drawing more current and slowing slightly. By varying the weights and measuring the motor's electrical input, we can directly find out **how much mechanical work** the motor is delivering at the shaft. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Applications:** > - Industrial motor testing bays > - Quality control in motor manufacturing units > - Performance verification of traction motors (EVs, locomotives) > - Educational laboratories (KTU PCEOL407 curriculum) ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |-----------|--------|----------------------| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | | Standard rope correction | $k$ | **0.1 to 0.5 kg** (typically 0.5 kg for cotton rope) | | Standard rope diameter | $d$ | **1 to 2 cm** (measured) | | Motor rated speed (typical shunt) | $N$ | **1500 rpm** | | Rated voltage (lab DC shunt) | $V$ | **220 V DC** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why and How the Test Works The brake test is classified as a **direct loading test** because unlike Swinburne's test (which runs the machine at no-load and uses indirect loss estimation), the brake test **physically loads the motor** to the point where output mechanical power can be **directly measured** at the shaft. ### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step): 1. **Mechanical Coupling**: The DC shunt motor is mounted on a rigid base. A **brake drum** (pulley) is rigidly coupled to the motor shaft. 2. **Rope Wrapping**: A flexible rope (cotton/hemp) is wrapped around the drum. The two free ends hang vertically with weights ($W_1$ on one side, $W_2$ on the other). 3. **Friction Generation**: When the motor rotates, the drum drags the rope via friction. This creates a **frictional tangential force** on the drum's surface. 4. **Tension Difference**: The tighter side ($W_1$) experiences more tension than the slack side ($W_2$). The **net effective load** on the drum is proportional to this tension difference. 5. **Electrical Measurement**: Voltmeter ($V$), ammeter ($A$), and a variable DC supply (rheostat for field + armature) are connected. A tachometer measures speed ($N$ in rpm). 6. **No-Load Run**: First, the motor runs at no-load (rope slack) to record no-load current ($I_0$) and speed ($N_0$). 7. **Loading Sequence**: Loads are progressively increased by adding weights, and at each step, $V$, $I$, $N$, $W_1$, $W_2$ are recorded. 8. **Output Computation**: From the difference in weights, the net force and shaft torque are calculated. Output power = Torque × Angular velocity. ## 2.2 Derivation of Net Frictional Force Consider a brake drum of effective radius $r$ (in metres). When the drum rotates with the rope: - The tension on the tight side equals $T_1 = W_1 \cdot g$ (Newtons) - The tension on the slack side equals $T_2 = W_2 \cdot g$ (Newtons) The **net tangential force** acting at the drum surface is: $$ F_{net} = T_1 - T_2 = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot g \quad \text{[Newtons]} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Rope Correction Term ($k$)**: In practice, the rope is not perfectly flexible. Some friction exists between rope and drum even on the slack side. To account for this, a constant correction $k$ (kg) is subtracted from $(W_1 - W_2)$: > > $$F_{net} = \left[(W_1 - W_2) - k\right] \cdot g$$ The correction $k$ is typically determined by the **rope-friction experiment** (a separate calibration test using dead weights on the rope over the pulley with the motor stationary). ## 2.3 Derivation of Effective Radius If the drum diameter is $D$ and rope diameter is $d$, the rope's centerline lies at: $$ r = \frac{D + d}{2} \quad \text{[metres]} $$ This is because the rope is not a thin line — it has thickness, and the **center of the rope** is where the effective force acts. ## 2.4 Shaft Torque Calculation The frictional force $F_{net}$ acts at radius $r$. The torque exerted by this force about the shaft axis is: $$ T_{sh} = F_{net} \times r \quad \text{[N·m]} $$ Substituting: $$ T_{sh} = \left[(W_1 - W_2) - k\right] \cdot g \cdot r \quad \text{[N·m]} $$ ## 2.5 Output Mechanical Power Power = Torque × Angular velocity ($\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s): $$ P_{out} = T_{sh} \times \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]} $$ Equivalently: $$ P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \times \left[(W_1 - W_2) - k\right] \cdot g \cdot r $$ ## 2.6 Input Electrical Power $$ P_{in} = V \times I \quad \text{[Watts]} $$ where $V$ = supply voltage and $I$ = total current drawn by armature + field. ## 2.7 Efficiency $$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{T_{sh} \times \frac{2\pi N}{60}}{V \times I} \times 100\% $$ ## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚡ Memorize this table — every KTU question is a direct substitution of these formulas.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|------|---------| | 1 | Net Force on Drum | $F = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot g$ | N | Without rope correction | | 2 | Net Force (with correction) | $F = \left[(W_1 - W_2) - k\right] \cdot g$ | N | $k$ = 0.1 to 0.5 kg | | 3 | Effective Drum Radius | $r = (D + d) / 2$ | m | $D$ = drum dia, $d$ = rope dia | | 4 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = F \cdot r$ | N·m | Direct measurement | | 5 | Output Power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ | W | $N$ in rpm | | 6 | Input Power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I$ | W | DC supply | | 7 | Efficiency | $\eta = (P_{out}/P_{in}) \times 100$ | % | Always $\leq 100$ | | 8 | Copper Loss | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ | W | Field loss $= V \cdot I_{sh}$ | | 9 | Constant Loss | $P_{const} = P_{in(0)} - P_{cu(0)}$ | W | No-load test | | 10 | Stray Loss | $P_{stray} = P_{const} - I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ | W | Iron + Friction + Windage | > [!WARNING] > **Never confuse $T_{sh}$ (shaft torque) with $T_{a}$ (armature torque)**. $T_{sh} = T_{a} - T_{friction}$, where $T_{friction}$ represents bearing/friction losses at the shaft. In brake test, we measure $T_{sh}$ **directly**, which already excludes shaft friction. The loss between $T_{sh}$ and $T_{a}$ is called **mechanical loss** (friction + windage) and is lumped into stray losses. ## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Motor Manufacturing**: Quality testing of every produced motor against rated specs. - **Traction Systems**: EV/railway motor load testing under simulated hill-climb conditions. - **Marine Propulsion**: Testing of ship propulsion motors at rated torque/speed. - **Industrial Process Lines**: Verifying motor performance before commissioning in factories. - **Research Labs**: Determining torque-speed characteristics and efficiency maps. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style) > [!NOTE] > **The following problem is modeled exactly on the KTU 2024 ESE style.** Every line of the solution is shown — no steps skipped. Use this as a template for the actual exam. ### **Problem Statement** In a brake test on a DC shunt motor, the following readings were obtained: | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Supply voltage, $V$ | 220 V | | Total current, $I$ | 8.5 A | | Motor speed, $N$ | 1440 rpm | | Tight-side weight, $W_1$ | 6.0 kg | | Slack-side weight, $W_2$ | 1.0 kg | | Drum diameter, $D$ | 30 cm | | Rope diameter, $d$ | 2 cm | | Rope correction, $k$ | 0.5 kg | | Shunt field current, $I_{sh}$ | 1.5 A | | Armature resistance, $R_a$ | 0.3 Ω | | Shunt field resistance, $R_{sh}$ | — | **Calculate:** 1. Shaft torque $T_{sh}$ 2. Output power $P_{out}$ 3. Input power $P_{in}$ 4. Efficiency $\eta$ 5. Armature copper loss, Field copper loss, Total losses 6. Stray losses --- ### **Solution (Step-by-Step with Full Algebraic Transitions)** #### **Step 1: Compute Armature Current** The total current $I$ splits into armature current $I_a$ and shunt field current $I_{sh}$: $$ I_a = I - I_{sh} $$ Substituting values: $$ I_a = 8.5 - 1.5 $$ $$ \boxed{I_a = 7.0 \text{ A}} $$ --- #### **Step 2: Compute Effective Drum Radius** Convert all lengths to **metres** (SI units are mandatory in KTU exams): $$ D = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m} $$ $$ d = 2 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m} $$ Effective radius: $$ r = \frac{D + d}{2} = \frac{0.30 + 0.02}{2} $$ $$ r = \frac{0.32}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{r = 0.16 \text{ m}} $$ --- #### **Step 3: Compute Net Tangential Force (with Rope Correction)** The difference in weights is: $$ W_1 - W_2 = 6.0 - 1.0 = 5.0 \text{ kg} $$ Applying rope correction: $$ (W_1 - W_2) - k = 5.0 - 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ kg} $$ Converting to force using $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$: $$ F = 4.5 \times 9.81 $$ $$ \boxed{F = 44.145 \text{ N}} $$ --- #### **Step 4: Compute Shaft Torque** $$ T_{sh} = F \times r $$ $$ T_{sh} = 44.145 \times 0.16 $$ $$ \boxed{T_{sh} = 7.0632 \text{ N·m}} $$ --- #### **Step 5: Compute Output Mechanical Power** Angular velocity in rad/s: $$ \omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1440}{60} $$ $$ \omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 1440}{60} = \frac{2880 \pi}{60} = 48\pi $$ $$ \omega = 48 \times 3.14159 = 150.7964 \text{ rad/s} $$ Output power: $$ P_{out} = T_{sh} \times \omega = 7.0632 \times 150.7964 $$ $$ P_{out} = 1065.16 \text{ W} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{out} \approx 1.065 \text{ kW}} $$ **Alternative direct formula method:** $$ P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2 \times 3.14159 \times 1440 \times 7.0632}{60} $$ $$ P_{out} = \frac{64019.6}{60} $$ $$ P_{out} = 1066.99 \text{ W} \approx 1.067 \text{ kW} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Tiny discrepancy** between 1065.16 W and 1066.99 W is due to rounding. Always use the **direct formula $P_{out} = (2\pi N T_{sh})/60$** in KTU exams — it gives consistent results. **Final answer:** $P_{out} \approx 1.067 \text{ kW}$ --- #### **Step 6: Compute Input Electrical Power** $$ P_{in} = V \times I $$ $$ P_{in} = 220 \times 8.5 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{in} = 1870 \text{ W} = 1.87 \text{ kW}} $$ --- #### **Step 7: Compute Efficiency** $$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \eta = \frac{1066.99}{1870} \times 100 $$ $$ \eta = 0.5706 \times 100 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta \approx 57.06\%} $$ --- #### **Step 8: Compute Armature Copper Loss** $$ P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 \times R_a $$ $$ P_{cu,a} = (7.0)^2 \times 0.3 $$ $$ P_{cu,a} = 49 \times 0.3 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{cu,a} = 14.7 \text{ W}} $$ --- #### **Step 9: Compute Shunt Field Copper Loss** First, find $R_{sh}$. Since the field is across the supply: $$ R_{sh} = \frac{V}{I_{sh}} = \frac{220}{1.5} $$ $$ R_{sh} = 146.67 \text{ Ω} $$ Field copper loss: $$ P_{cu,sh} = V \times I_{sh} = 220 \times 1.5 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{cu,sh} = 330 \text{ W}} $$ (Or equivalently: $I_{sh}^2 \times R_{sh} = 1.5^2 \times 146.67 = 330$ W ✓) --- #### **Step 10: Compute Total Copper Losses** $$ P_{cu,total} = P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh} = 14.7 + 330 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{cu,total} = 344.7 \text{ W}} $$ --- #### **Step 11: Compute Total Losses (from Power Balance)** $$ P_{loss,total} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 1870 - 1066.99 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{loss,total} = 803.01 \text{ W}} $$ --- #### **Step 12: Compute Stray Losses** Stray losses = Total losses − Copper losses: $$ P_{stray} = P_{loss,total} - P_{cu,total} $$ $$ P_{stray} = 803.01 - 344.7 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{stray} = 458.31 \text{ W}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Stray losses** include iron (core) losses, friction losses, and windage losses. They are considered **constant** (independent of load) in the Swinburne test approximation. --- ### **3.2 Tabulated Analytical Matrix (Full Solution Summary)** | Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula Used | Calculation | Final Answer | |---------|----------|--------------|-------------|--------------| | 1 | $I_a$ | $I - I_{sh}$ | $8.5 - 1.5$ | **7.0 A** | | 2 | $r$ | $(D + d)/2$ | $(0.30 + 0.02)/2$ | **0.16 m** | | 3 | $F$ | $[(W_1-W_2) - k] \cdot g$ | $(5.0 - 0.5) \times 9.81$ | **44.145 N** | | 4 | $T_{sh}$ | $F \times r$ | $44.145 \times 0.16$ | **7.0632 N·m** | | 5 | $P_{out}$ | $2\pi N T_{sh} / 60$ | $2\pi \times 1440 \times 7.0632 / 60$ | **1066.99 W** | | 6 | $P_{in}$ | $V \times I$ | $220 \times 8.5$ | **1870 W** | | 7 | $\eta$ | $(P_{out}/P_{in}) \times 100$ | $(1066.99/1870) \times 100$ | **57.06 %** | | 8 | $P_{cu,a}$ | $I_a^2 R_a$ | $(7.0)^2 \times 0.3$ | **14.7 W** | | 9 | $P_{cu,sh}$ | $V \times I_{sh}$ | $220 \times 1.5$ | **330 W** | | 10 | $P_{cu,total}$ | $P_{cu,a} + P_{cu,sh}$ | $14.7 + 330$ | **344.7 W** | | 11 | $P_{loss,total}$ | $P_{in} - P_{out}$ | $1870 - 1066.99$ | **803.01 W** | | 12 | $P_{stray}$ | $P_{loss,total} - P_{cu,total}$ | $803.01 - 344.7$ | **458.31 W** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Brake Test Setup > [!NOTE] > **Why a block diagram?** A physical brake test rig involves mechanical components (drum, rope, weights) that are difficult to render in Mermaid. Hence, a **functional block architecture** is presented, showing signal/power flow from the electrical supply to the mechanical output and back to the measurement instruments. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[DC Supply 220V]:::power nodeB[Rheostat Field Control]:::control nodeC[DC Shunt Motor]:::machine nodeD[Brake Drum Coupled to Shaft]:::mech nodeE[Rope with Weights W1 and W2]:::mech nodeF[Voltmeter V]:::meter nodeG[Ammeter A]:::meter nodeH[Tachometer N rpm]:::meter nodeI[Weighing Balance W1 W2]:::meter nodeJ[Output Shaft Torque Tsh]:::output nodeK[Output Power Pout]:::output nodeL[Input Power Pin]:::output nodeM[Efficiency Calculation]:::output nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeI nodeD --> nodeH nodeC --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeF --> nodeL nodeG --> nodeL nodeK --> nodeM nodeL --> nodeM classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef control fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef machine fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef mech fill:#8B4513,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef meter fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Test Procedure Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA[Step 1: Connect Circuit
Motor + Meters]:::step stepB[Step 2: No-Load Run
Record I0, N0]:::step stepC[Step 3: Add Initial Load
W1 and W2 weights]:::step stepD[Step 4: Steady State Wait
30 seconds minimum]:::step stepE[Step 5: Record V, I, N, W1, W2]:::step stepF[Step 6: Increase Load Incrementally]:::step stepG[Step 7: Repeat Steps 4-6
5 to 6 times]:::step stepH[Step 8: Compute Tsh, Pout, Pin, Efficiency]:::step stepI[Step 9: Plot Efficiency vs Output Power]:::step stepJ[Step 10: Compare with Rated Values]:::step stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ classDef step fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Physical Free-Body Diagram of the Brake Drum (Descriptive) Although a Mermaid rendering of the physical drum is non-trivial, the **force and torque geometry** is described below for clarity: ``` SHAFT AXIS (motor rotation) │ │ ← rotational direction (ω) ▼ ┌────────────────────┐ │ │ │ BRAKE DRUM │ ◄── Drum Diameter D │ (radius r) │ │ │ └────────────────────┘ ●━━━━━━━━●━━━━━● ← ROPE wrapped around / \ T1│ │T2 │ │ │ │ ┌──┴──┐ ┌──┴──┐ │ W1 │ │ W2 │ │ kg │ │ kg │ └─────┘ └─────┘ (TIGHT) (SLACK) ↓ ↑ DOWN DOWN (tension (tension pulls rope rope pulled DOWN) DOWN by drum) ``` **Key relationships from the figure:** - The motor applies a **clockwise torque** to the drum. - Rope tension $T_1 = W_1 \cdot g$ on the tight side opposes motion. - Rope tension $T_2 = W_2 \cdot g$ on the slack side is in the direction of motion. - **Net resisting torque** on the motor = $(T_1 - T_2) \times r = F_{net} \times r = T_{sh}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule for setting up weights**: Always keep $W_1 > W_2$. The weight on the tight side must be heavier — otherwise the motor will not be loaded; the rope will just slip and the drum will free-wheel. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a brake test on a DC motor? Why is it called a direct loading test?** **Model Answer:** > A brake test is an experimental method to determine the **shaft torque and output power** of a DC motor by applying a **mechanical frictional load** using a rope-and-pulley (drum) arrangement. > > It is called a **direct loading test** because the motor is **physically subjected to a real mechanical load** during the test, and the output is measured **directly** at the shaft using the difference in weights suspended on either side of the rope.
