Unlike indirect methods (e.g., Swinburne's test), no pre-calculated or estimated loss data is required. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is a rope correction factor used in the brake test calculation?** **Model Answer:** > The rope correction factor $k$ (typically 0.1 to 0.5 kg) accounts for the **stiffness of the rope and the additional friction** between the rope and the drum on the slack side, which is not visible in the simple weight reading. Without this correction, the calculated shaft torque will be **higher than actual**, leading to a **fictitiously high efficiency** reading. The correction ensures the true net tangential force and hence the true mechanical output is computed. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Rule (per academic regulations):** Every Part B question in the End Semester Examination offers an **internal choice**. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B** (not both). Both questions are designed with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **a) Explain with a neat sketch the procedure to conduct a brake test on a DC shunt motor. List the instruments required and the observations to be recorded. (7 marks)** **b) In a brake test on a DC shunt motor, the following observations were recorded:** | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Supply Voltage | 200 V | | Motor Current | 12 A | | Speed | 1200 rpm | | Tight side weight $W_1$ | 9 kg | | Slack side weight $W_2$ | 1 kg | | Drum diameter | 40 cm | | Rope diameter | 1.5 cm | | Rope correction | 0.5 kg | **Calculate: (i) Shaft torque, (ii) Output power, (iii) Input power, (iv) Efficiency. (7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** ##### **Part (a) — Procedure & Instruments (7 marks)** **Apparatus Required:** 1. DC Shunt Motor (with nameplate details) 2. Brake drum/pulley (rigidly coupled to motor shaft) 3. Rope (cotton/hemp) with two free ends 4. Two sets of slotted weights ($W_1$ and $W_2$) 5. DC Voltmeter (0–300 V) 6. DC Ammeter (0–20 A) 7. Tachometer (digital/analog, 0–5000 rpm) 8. Variable DC supply (with field rheostat) 9. Starter and switch **Circuit Diagram (described):** - Shunt motor armature and field connected in **parallel** across the DC supply. - Voltmeter across supply terminals, ammeter in series with armature. - Field rheostat in series with shunt winding to control field current. - Brake drum coupled to the motor shaft extension; rope wound around with hanging weights. **Procedure (Step-by-Step):** 1. Make all electrical connections as per the circuit. Ensure the rope is loosely placed on the drum (no contact tension initially). 2. Start the motor using the starter, gradually increasing voltage to rated value. Adjust field rheostat to rated speed. 3. **No-Load Reading**: Record $V_0$, $I_0$, $N_0$ (tachometer reading). 4. Add small weights to both sides ($W_1 = W_2 = 1$ kg initially to keep rope taut). 5. Add progressively more weight to $W_1$ side. Wait 30 seconds for steady state. 6. At each load, record: $V$, $I$, $N$, $W_1$, $W_2$. 7. Continue until the rated current or rated torque is reached. 8. Reduce load to zero and switch off the motor. **Precautions:** - Never let the rope become too tight (motor may stall). - Do not exceed rated current to avoid armature damage. - Keep $W_1 > W_2$ always; otherwise the rope slips and no load is applied. - Allow motor to cool between readings. **Observations to be recorded (typical table):** | $V$ (V) | $I$ (A) | $N$ (rpm) | $W_1$ (kg) | $W_2$ (kg) | |---|---|---|---|---| **Calculations to be done:** - $F = [(W_1 - W_2) - k] \cdot g$ - $r = (D + d)/2$ - $T_{sh} = F \cdot r$ - $P_{out} = 2\pi N T_{sh}/60$ - $P_{in} = V \cdot I$ - $\eta = (P_{out}/P_{in}) \times 100\%$ **Plot:** - $\eta$ vs $P_{out}$ curve. - $T_{sh}$ vs $P_{out}$ curve. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for procedure, 2 for precautions, 1 for tabulation)* --- ##### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** - $V = 200$ V, $I = 12$ A, $N = 1200$ rpm - $W_1 = 9$ kg, $W_2 = 1$ kg, $D = 40$ cm, $d = 1.5$ cm, $k = 0.5$ kg **Step 1: Effective Radius** $$ D = 0.40 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.015 \text{ m} $$ $$ r = \frac{0.40 + 0.015}{2} = \frac{0.415}{2} = 0.2075 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2: Net Force** $$ (W_1 - W_2) - k = (9 - 1) - 0.5 = 7.5 \text{ kg} $$ $$ F = 7.5 \times 9.81 = 73.575 \text{ N} $$ **Step 3: Shaft Torque** $$ T_{sh} = F \times r = 73.575 \times 0.2075 = 15.267 \text{ N·m} $$ **Step 4: Output Power** $$ P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 1200 \times 15.267}{60} $$ $$ P_{out} = \frac{2 \times 3.14159 \times 1200 \times 15.267}{60} = \frac{115115.6}{60} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{out} = 1918.59 \text{ W} \approx 1.919 \text{ kW}} $$ **Step 5: Input Power** $$ P_{in} = V \times I = 200 \times 12 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{in} = 2400 \text{ W} = 2.4 \text{ kW}} $$ **Step 6: Efficiency** $$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{1918.59}{2400} \times 100 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta = 79.94\%} $$ **Summary Table:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Effective radius $r$ | 0.2075 m | | Net force $F$ | 73.575 N | | Shaft torque $T_{sh}$ | 15.267 N·m | | Output power $P_{out}$ | 1918.59 W | | Input power $P_{in}$ | 2400 W | | Efficiency $\eta$ | 79.94% | *(7 marks: 1 each for $r$, $F$, $T_{sh}$, $P_{out}$, $P_{in}$, $\eta$, and 1 for unit consistency)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **a) What are the advantages and disadvantages of the brake test? Compare it with the Swinburne's test. (7 marks)** **b) The no-load and loaded readings of a DC shunt motor tested by the brake method are:** **No-load:** $V_0 = 220$ V, $I_0 = 3$ A, $N_0 = 1500$ rpm **Loaded:** $V = 220$ V, $I = 20$ A, $N = 1420$ rpm, $W_1 = 10$ kg, $W_2 = 1$ kg Drum diameter = 30 cm, Rope diameter = 2 cm, Rope correction = 0.5 kg Shunt field current = 1.0 A, Armature resistance = 0.4 Ω **Determine the stray losses of the motor. (7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** ##### **Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks)** | Feature | Brake Test | Swinburne's Test | |---------|-----------|------------------| | Type | Direct loading test | No-load (indirect) test | | Load applied? | Yes, real mechanical load | No, motor runs at no-load | | Output measured? | Directly via weights | Estimated from no-loss extrapolation | | Stray losses? | Determined from loaded data | Assumed constant at all loads | | Speed regulation info? | Yes, speed drop observed | No | | Time required | Longer (multiple load points) | Shorter (single no-load run) | | Accuracy | Higher (direct measurement) | Approximate (assumptions) | | Motor must be loaded? | Yes (suitable for small motors) | No (no mechanical load needed) | | Power consumption during test | Higher (full load) | Very low (no-load only) | | Application | Small to medium DC motors | Large DC motors (where loading is impractical) | **Advantages of Brake Test:** 1. Output is measured **directly** — no assumptions about losses. 2. Stray losses are obtained from real data. 3. Torque-speed characteristic can be plotted. 4. Suitable for **small DC motors** up to ~10 kW. **Disadvantages of Brake Test:** 1. **Power wasted as heat** in the rope friction (brake) — energy inefficient. 2. **Difficult to apply to large motors** due to inability to dissipate large heat. 3. **Cooling required** — rope may catch fire at high loads. 4. **Load cannot be held constant** for long durations easily. 5. **Manual operation** — only steady-state readings possible. *(7 marks: 4 for comparison table, 2 for advantages, 1 for disadvantages)* --- ##### **Part (b) — Stray Loss Calculation (7 marks)** **Given Data:** | Parameter | No-load | Loaded | |-----------|---------|--------| | $V$ | 220 V | 220 V | | $I$ | 3 A | 20 A | | $N$ | 1500 rpm | 1420 rpm | | $W_1$ | — | 10 kg | | $W_2$ | — | 1 kg | - $D = 30$ cm = 0.30 m, $d = 2$ cm = 0.02 m, $k = 0.5$ kg - $I_{sh} = 1.0$ A, $R_a = 0.4$ Ω **Step 1: No-Load Input Power** $$ P_{in,0} = V_0 \times I_0 = 220 \times 3 = 660 \text{ W} $$ **Step 2: No-Load Armature Current** $$ I_{a,0} = I_0 - I_{sh} = 3 - 1 = 2 \text{ A} $$ **Step 3: No-Load Armature Copper Loss** $$ P_{cu,a,0} = I_{a,0}^2 \times R_a = (2)^2 \times 0.4 = 4 \times 0.4 = 1.6 \text{ W} $$ **Step 4: No-Load Shunt Field Copper Loss** $$ P_{cu,sh} = V \times I_{sh} = 220 \times 1.0 = 220 \text{ W} $$ **Step 5: Constant Losses (No-Load)** At no-load, output ≈ 0, so input power equals total losses: $$ P_{const} = P_{in,0} - P_{cu,a,0} = 660 - 1.6 = 658.4 \text{ W} $$ **Step 6: Stray Losses (No-Load)** Stray = Constant losses − Shunt field copper loss: $$ P_{stray} = P_{const} - P_{cu,sh} = 658.4 - 220 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{stray} = 438.4 \text{ W}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Alternative Verification via Loaded Data (Optional — for 2 bonus marks):** > > At load: $I_a = 20 - 1 = 19$ A > > $$ > P_{cu,a} = 19^2 \times 0.4 = 144.4 \text{ W} > $$ > > Effective radius: $r = (0.30 + 0.02)/2 = 0.16$ m > > $$ > F = [(10-1) - 0.5] \times 9.81 = 8.5 \times 9.81 = 83.385 \text{ N} > $$ > > $$ > T_{sh} = 83.385 \times 0.16 = 13.34 \text{ N·m} > $$ > > $$ > P_{out} = (2\pi \times 1420 \times 13.34)/60 = 1983.6 \text{ W} > $$ > > $$ > P_{in} = 220 \times 20 = 4400 \text{ W} > $$ > > Total loss = $4400 - 1983.6 = 2416.4$ W > > Total copper loss = $144.4 + 220 = 364.4$ W > > Stray loss (loaded) = $2416.4 - 364.4 = 2052$ W > > **Discrepancy** between 438.4 W and 2052 W arises because **stray losses are NOT truly constant** — they increase with load (especially at higher armature currents, stray load losses due to distortion). In a true KTU exam, report the **no-load method** result, but mention the variation. *(7 marks: 2 for $P_{const}$, 2 for $P_{cu,sh}$, 2 for $P_{stray}$, 1 for final answer)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert cm to m** — A 30 cm drum radius treated as 30 m will give an efficiency of 0.001%! Always convert to SI units first. > 2. **Using $D/2$ instead of $(D+d)/2$** — Forgetting the rope diameter adds a 1–2% error. The KTU answer key explicitly uses $(D+d)/2$. > 3. **Mixing up $W_1$ and $W_2$** — $W_1$ is the **tight** side (heavier); $W_2$ is the **slack** side (lighter). Reversing them gives negative torque. > 4. **Using $I$ instead of $I_a$ for armature copper loss** — $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$, NOT $I^2 R_a$. Using total current inflates copper loss. > 5. **Not subtracting the rope correction $k$** — Some versions of the problem statement omit the $k$ term; read carefully. If $k$ is given, use it. > 6. **Forgetting the units in the final answer** — KTU deducts 0.5 mark for missing units (e.g., N·m, W, %). > 7. **Calculating $P_{out}$ in kW and $P_{in}$ in W without conversion** — Make units consistent before computing $\eta$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision (5-minute glance)** - [x] **Brake Test = Direct Loading Test** — measures output **directly** via friction. - [x] **Setup**: Rope on drum, $W_1$ on tight side, $W_2$ on slack side; $W_1 > W_2$ always. - [x] **Effective radius formula**: $r = (D + d)/2$ **(not $D/2$)**. - [x] **Net force formula**: $F = [(W_1 - W_2) - k] \cdot g$ with $k$ = rope correction. - [x] **Shaft Torque**: $T_{sh} = F \cdot r$ (N·m) - [x] **Output Power**: $P_{out} = (2\pi N T_{sh})/60$ (W) — $N$ must be in **rpm**. - [x] **Input Power**: $P_{in} = V \cdot I$ (W) — $I$ is **total current** drawn. - [x] **Efficiency**: $\eta = (P_{out}/P_{in}) \times 100\%$ — must be **less than 100%**. - [x] **Armature Copper Loss**: $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (I - I_{sh})^2 R_a$. - [x] **Field Copper Loss**: $P_{cu,sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$. - [x] **Stray Losses** = Total losses − Copper losses (both armature and field). - [x] **Constant losses** = Stray losses + Field copper loss (assumed independent of load). - [x] **Standard values to remember**: $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\pi = 3.14159$. - [x] **Compare** with **Swinburne's test** in Part B — tabulated comparison is a frequent 7-mark question. - [x] **Unit Conversion Mandate**: cm → m before any calculation (1 cm = 0.01 m). - [x] **Plot expected**: $\eta$ vs $P_{out}$ (efficiency curve rises and falls), $N$ vs $I_a$ (drooping characteristic). - [x] **Disadvantages to remember**: Heat dissipation, only for small motors, energy wasted. - [x] **No-load data is essential** to find constant/stray losses in Swinburne-style analysis. - [x] **KTU marking scheme**: Part (a) carries 7 marks (theory), Part (b) carries 7 marks (numerical). The internal choice gives flexibility. - [x] **Always include the rope correction $k$** if the problem provides it — the KTU answer key deducts 1 mark for omission. > [!NOTE] > **🎯 Highest-Yield Topics for KTU 2024 Exam (in order of priority):** > 1. Numerical calculation of $T_{sh}$, $P_{out}$, $P_{in}$, $\eta$ (guaranteed 7 marks) > 2. Circuit diagram + procedure (guaranteed 7 marks) > 3. Comparison with Swinburne's test (frequently asked 7 marks) > 4. Stray loss calculation from no-load data (3-mark part A) > 5. Reasons for rope correction, advantages/disadvantages (3-mark part A)

topics.title: Brake Test on DC Series Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Brake Test on DC Series Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Brake Test (Direct Load Test / Rope-Brake / Prony-Brake Test):**
> A direct method of loading an electric motor in which the mechanical output power is absorbed by a friction brake (rope-and-drum type or water-cooled band brake) rigidly coupled to the motor shaft. The tangential frictional force on the drum, measured using spring balances, gives the shaft torque, while a tachometer gives the speed. This test is the **most direct laboratory method** to determine the **efficiency**, **shaft torque**, and **mechanical output power** of a motor under actual running load conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The brake test is specifically used in Module 2 of PCEOL407 for the DC **series** motor because of a unique property: a series motor develops torque proportional to the **square of armature current** ($T \propto I_a^2$), allowing easy variation of load simply by tightening the brake rope. Unlike a shunt motor, a series motor **cannot be started on no-load** — the brake test therefore begins by applying a small initial mechanical load to prevent runaway.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are riding a bicycle and you want to know how hard your legs are pushing on the pedals. One simple way: clamp a **friction strap** around the rear wheel rim and pull it tight with your hand. The harder you pull, the more the bicycle slows down — but the pulling force × the wheel radius tells you exactly the *resisting torque* your legs are overcoming. That is essentially what a **brake test** does to a motor.

A **springy rope wrapped around a spinning drum** acts like your hand. Two spring balances (one on each end of the rope) measure the difference of tension. That difference × drum radius = the **shaft torque** the motor is delivering. A **tachometer** measures how fast the wheel is spinning, and force × speed = **mechanical power output**. Whatever electrical power you supplied ($V \times I$) minus this mechanical output = the **losses** of the machine. The whole efficiency story of the motor is therefore revealed **without ever opening the machine**.

## 1.3 The DC Series Motor — A Quick Refresher

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| Field connection | Field winding in **series** with armature |
| Flux–current relation | $\phi \propto I_a$ (before magnetic saturation) |
| Torque equation | $T \propto \phi \, I_a \propto I_a^2$ |
| Speed–load characteristic | **Inverse** — speed falls sharply as load increases |
| No-load behaviour | Speed tends to **infinity** (mechanically unsafe) |
| Application | Traction, cranes, electric locomotives |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **square-law torque characteristic** ($T \propto I_a^2$) is the single most important reason the brake test is so elegantly suited to a DC series motor. Tightening the rope increases the load torque, the motor slows down slightly, draws more current, and automatically develops more torque to balance it — a beautiful, self-regulating loop.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- Standard acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Spring-balance readings are conventionally in **kgf**; converted to Newtons by multiplying by $g$.
- Brake-drum effective radius $R$ is in **metres** (m).
- Motor speed $N$ is in **revolutions per minute** (rpm).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Mechanical Coupling:** The motor under test is rigidly coupled (belt-coupled or direct-coupled) to a **brake drum** of known effective radius $R$ mounted on the shaft.
2. **Rope & Spring Balances:** A rope carrying a loading tension is wrapped around the drum. The two ends of the rope are attached to two spring balances $S_1$ (tight side) and $S_2$ (slack side). The **net tangential frictional force** on the drum is $(S_1 - S_2)g$ Newtons.
3. **Load Application:** Mechanical load is varied in steps by tightening the tension screw of the brake. At each step, the motor is allowed to reach steady-state.
4. **Measurement Trio:** At every load step, the following are recorded simultaneously:
   - Voltmeter reading $V$ (V)
   - Ammeter reading $I$ (A)  — note: in a series motor, line current = armature current = field current
   - Tachometer reading $N$ (rpm)
   - Spring-balance readings $S_1$, $S_2$ (kg)
5. **Calculation:** Shaft torque $T_{sh}$, output power $P_{out}$, input power $P_{in}$, and efficiency $\eta$ are computed for each load point.
6. **Plotting:** Performance curves — $P_{in}$ vs $I$, $P_{out}$ vs $I$, $\eta$ vs $I$, $N$ vs $I$, $T$ vs $I$ — are drawn.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why two spring balances?** A single balance cannot isolate frictional force from the static preload. Subtracting the two readings cancels the dead-weight of the rope and gives the **true net friction force**.
- **Why record $V$ and $I$?** The input electrical power $P_{in} = VI$ is the denominator of efficiency. Without it, no efficiency plot.
- **Why a tachometer?** Torque alone is meaningless without rotational speed; power needs both.
- **Why is starting on no-load dangerous?** With $T \propto I_a^2$ and tiny friction, the motor must run extremely fast to generate the tiny counter-torque — speed becomes destructively high.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorise this table as a high-yield revision sheet.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Net tangential force on drum | $F = (S_1 - S_2) \times g$ | N | $S_1, S_2$ in kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 2 | Shaft torque (gross) | $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times g \times R$ | N·m | $R$ = effective drum radius in m |
| 3 | Output (mechanical) power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ | W | $N$ in rpm |
| 4 | Input (electrical) power | $P_{in} = V \times I$ | W | $V$ in V, $I$ in A |
| 5 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \,\%$ | % | Computed per load point |
| 6 | Torque of series motor | $T \propto \phi I_a \propto I_a^2$ | N·m | Before saturation; $\phi \propto I_a$ |
| 7 | Back-EMF | $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | V | $R_{se}$ = series field resistance |
| 8 | Speed (DC motor general) | $N = \dfrac{E_b}{k \phi} \times \dfrac{60 \, A}{P \, Z}$ | rpm | $P$ = poles, $Z$ = conductors, $A$ = parallel paths |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Trap:** In a series motor, $I_{line} = I_a = I_{se}$. Examiners often specify "no-load current" — for a series motor this is the **minimum current needed to overcome friction**, NOT zero.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The brake test is the **industry gold-standard** for small-and-medium motor acceptance testing in manufacturing shops, repair workshops, and college labs because it:

- Uses **no expensive dynamometer**.
- Directly measures the output torque and power the customer will actually draw.
- Reveals **stray load losses, brush friction, and windage** as the gap between $P_{in}$ and $P_{out}$.
- Provides the empirical **efficiency curve** required by **IS 325 / IEC 60034** standards for rating-plate data.

In the field, large DC traction motors are similarly tested on **water-cooled eddy-current dynamometers** — a direct descendant of the prony brake principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Shaft Torque from Spring-Balance Readings

Let the two spring balance readings be $S_1$ (tight side) and $S_2$ (slack side), each in kgf.

**Step 1 — Convert to force in Newtons:**

$$F_1 = S_1 \times g \quad \text{and} \quad F_2 = S_2 \times g$$

**Step 2 — Net tangential friction force on the drum:**

The rope is wrapped around the drum; the tight-side pulls the drum in one direction, the slack-side opposes. The net force that the motor shaft must overcome is:

$$F_{net} = F_1 - F_2 = (S_1 - S_2) \times g$$

**Step 3 — Convert linear force to torque using the drum radius $R$:**

The friction force acts tangentially at radius $R$, so the resisting torque (which equals the developed shaft torque at steady state) is:

$$T_{sh} = F_{net} \times R$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times g \times R \,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Mechanical Output Power

Mechanical power is the rate of doing work. For a rotating shaft, work done per revolution = $T_{sh} \times 2\pi$ (since arc length per revolution is $2\pi R$ and force is $T_{sh}/R$).

**Step 1 — Angular speed in rad/s:**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

**Step 2 — Power = torque × angular speed:**

$$P_{out} = T_{sh} \times \omega = T_{sh} \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60} \text{ Watts}\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Series-Motor Torque Equation

Starting from the general DC-machine torque equation:

$$T = \frac{1}{2\pi} \, P \, \phi \, Z \, I_a \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

**Step 1 — Recognise that in a series motor, the field flux is produced by the armature current itself** (before saturation):

$$\phi = k_f \, I_a$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the torque equation:**

$$T = \frac{1}{2\pi} \, P \, (k_f I_a) \, Z \, I_a = \left(\frac{P \, k_f \, Z}{2\pi}\right) I_a^2$$

Let the bracketed constant be $K_T$:

$$\boxed{\,T \propto I_a^2\,}$$

**Engineering Implication:** Doubling the armature current *quadruples* the torque. This is what makes a series motor produce very high starting torque — exactly what traction applications need.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU University-Style Problem)

> **Given data** for a DC series motor under brake test:
> Supply voltage $V = 220$ V, speed $N = 1500$ rpm, spring balances $S_1 = 10$ kg and $S_2 = 2$ kg, effective drum radius $R = 10$ cm $= 0.1$ m, line current $I = 10$ A.

**Step 1 — Net force on drum:**

$$F_{net} = (S_1 - S_2) \times g = (10 - 2) \times 9.81 = 8 \times 9.81 = 78.48 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Shaft torque:**

$$T_{sh} = F_{net} \times R = 78.48 \times 0.1 = 7.848 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Output power:**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 7.848}{60} = \frac{73959.3}{60} = 1232.66 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Input power:**

$$P_{in} = V \times I = 220 \times 10 = 2200 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{1232.66}{2200} \times 100 = 56.03 \,\%$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Expected Behaviour Across Load Range

> [!NOTE]
> The table below gives the **qualitative trend** of each quantity as load on a DC series motor is increased (i.e., as spring-balance difference grows). This is a high-yield topic for KTU theory questions.

| Quantity | Trend as Load $\uparrow$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Current $I_a$ | Increases (steeply) | Motor slows $\Rightarrow E_b \downarrow \Rightarrow I_a \uparrow$ |
| Speed $N$ | Decreases (sharply) | Series motor has "inverse" speed–load curve |
| Torque $T$ | Increases ($\propto I_a^2$) | Square-law characteristic |
| Output power $P_{out}$ | Increases, then decreases | $P_{out} = T\omega$, $T \uparrow$ but $\omega \downarrow$ |
| Input power $P_{in}$ | Increases (linearly with $I$) | $P_{in} = VI$, $V$ is roughly constant |
| Efficiency $\eta$ | Rises to a maximum, then falls | Losses rise faster than output beyond a point |
| Power factor | Slightly improves | Armature reaction is partially offsetting |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often claim efficiency "keeps increasing" with load. It actually peaks at around **60–80 % of full load** for a typical DC series motor. Beyond the peak, copper losses ($I^2R$) rise quadratically and dominate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Experimental Setup (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mainsAC["AC MAINS 230V 50Hz"] --> rect["AUTOTRANSFORMER OR DIODE RECTIFIER"]
    rect --> dcmotor["DC SERIES MOTOR UNDER TEST"]
    dcmotor -. "SHAFT COUPLING" .-> brakedrum["BRAKE DRUM RADIUS R"]
    brakedrum --> ropeA["ROPE TIGHT SIDE"]
    brakedrum --> ropeB["ROPE SLACK SIDE"]
    ropeA --> spring1["SPRING BALANCE S1"]
    ropeB --> spring2["SPRING BALANCE S2"]
    dcmotor --> tach["TACHOMETER N rpm"]
    dcmotor --> vmet["VOLTMETER V"]
    dcmotor --> amet["AMMETER I line"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Circuit Schematic of the DC Series Motor with Measuring Instruments

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA([START]) --> dsply1["DC SUPPLY V"]
    dsply1 --> sw1["DOUBLE POLE SWITCH"]
    sw1 --> fuse1["FUSE"]
    fuse1 --> ameter["AMMETER A reads Iline"]
    ameter --> field["SERIES FIELD WINDING Rse"]
    field --> armature["ARMATURE Ra"]
    armature --> vmet2["VOLTMETER V across armature plus field"]
    vmet2 --> sw2["SWITCH S2"]
    sw2 --> stopA([END])
    ameter -. "I line equals Ia equals Ise" .-> field
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** In the schematic, note that the ammeter is placed **in series with the line** so it reads the same current flowing through both the field and the armature. The voltmeter is connected across the **motor terminals** to read the applied terminal voltage $V$.

## 4.3 Mermaid — Performance-Curve Set (Data Flow Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["V I N S1 S2 R constants"] --> calcT["Calculate T_sh eq 1"]
    inputA --> calcPout["Calculate P_out eq 2"]
    inputA --> calcPin["Calculate P_in VI"]
    calcT --> eff["Calculate efficiency percent"]
    calcPout --> eff
    calcPin --> eff
    calcT --> plotT["Plot T vs I curve"]
    calcPout --> plotP["Plot P_out vs I curve"]
    calcPin --> plotPin["Plot P_in vs I curve"]
    eff --> plotEff["Plot efficiency vs I curve"]
    inputA --> plotN["Plot N vs I curve"]
```

## 4.4 Physical Setup — What You See in the Lab

| Component | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **DC Series Motor** (on sturdy base) | Machine under test |
| **Brake Drum** (cast-iron) mounted on shaft | Provides friction surface |
| **Rope** (manila/hemp) — 1 to 2 turns | Transmits friction to spring balances |
| **Two Spring Balances** ($0$–$20$ kg typical) | Measure tight- and slack-side tensions |
| **Loading Screw** | Tightens the brake to increase load |
| **Tachometer** (digital or analog) | Measures $N$ in rpm |
| **Voltmeter + Ammeter** in DC panel | Measure $V$ and $I$ |
| **Rheostat** in series with armature | Starting current limiting |
| **DPST switch and fuse** | Safety and isolation |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`

> **"State the relation between torque and armature current for a DC series motor. Justify why a DC series motor cannot be started on no-load."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation shown):**

- **(1 mark)** The torque–current relation is $T \propto I_a^2$ (before magnetic saturation). This is because the field flux $\phi$ is produced by the armature current itself: $\phi \propto I_a$, and torque $T \propto \phi I_a$.
- **(1 mark)** On no-load, the only torque the motor must supply is the small friction and windage torque. Since $T \propto I_a^2$, the current required is extremely small.
- **(1 mark)** With negligible armature voltage drop ($I_a R_a$ is tiny) and $E_b = k\phi N$, the speed $N$ must rise to a very large value to generate the required back-EMF. The motor therefore **runs away to dangerously high speeds** that can destroy it mechanically. Hence a series motor must always be started with some mechanical load coupled to the shaft — exactly what the brake test ensures.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

> **"Define the term 'shaft torque' as used in a brake test. Write the expression for shaft torque using spring-balance readings."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation shown):**

- **(1 mark)** Shaft torque is the useful torque available at the motor coupling flange for doing mechanical work; it equals the net tangential frictional force on the brake drum multiplied by the effective drum radius.
- **(1 mark)** The two spring-balance readings are $S_1$ (tight side) and $S_2$ (slack side), both in kg.
- **(1 mark)** The expression is $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times g \times R$ Newton-metres, where $g = 9.81$ m/s² and $R$ is the effective drum radius in metres.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Either sub-part may be subdivided into finer steps. The two questions below are mutually exclusive alternatives — students answer any one of the two.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, describe the experimental procedure to conduct the **brake test on a DC series motor**. Mention the role of each instrument used.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a brake test on a DC series motor, the following readings were obtained:

| Sl. No. | $V$ (V) | $I$ (A) | $N$ (rpm) | $S_1$ (kg) | $S_2$ (kg) | Drum radius $R$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 220 | 5 | 1700 | 4.0 | 1.0 | 0.075 |
| 2 | 220 | 8 | 1550 | 7.0 | 1.5 | 0.075 |
| 3 | 220 | 12 | 1380 | 11.0 | 2.0 | 0.075 |
| 4 | 220 | 16 | 1220 | 15.0 | 2.5 | 0.075 |
| 5 | 220 | 20 | 1080 | 19.0 | 3.0 | 0.075 |

For each load condition, calculate **(i) shaft torque, (ii) output power, (iii) input power, and (iv) efficiency**. Plot the **efficiency vs current** and **speed vs current** characteristics. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

1. **(1 mark)** Apparatus listing: DC series motor, brake drum of known effective radius $R$, rope with two spring balances, loading screw, voltmeter, ammeter, tachometer, DPST switch, fuse, autotransformer or rectifier.
2. **(1.5 marks)** Make the connections as per the circuit diagram: the voltmeter is connected across the motor terminals; the ammeter is connected in series with the line so it reads $I_{line} = I_a = I_{se}$. The starting rheostat is kept at maximum-resistance position before switch-on.
3. **(1.5 marks)** Switch on the supply with the starting rheostat in the cut-in (max-resistance) position and the loading screw fully released. The motor must be given an initial small load by tightening the loading screw — **this is essential because a series motor must not be started on no-load**.
4. **(1 mark)** Adjust the loading screw step-by-step. At each step, after the readings stabilise, record $V$, $I$, $N$, $S_1$, $S_2$. Take 5–6 sets of readings.
5. **(1 mark)** Compute $T_{sh}$, $P_{out}$, $P_{in}$, $\eta$ for each set using the formulas in Section 2.3.
6. **(1 mark)** Plot the performance curves. Switch off the supply after the test and immediately release the brake to prevent the motor from being locked.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Formulae used (1 mark):**

$$T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81 \times 0.075$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60}, \quad P_{in} = 220 \times I, \quad \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$$

**Row 1 (I = 5 A):**

$$T_{sh} = (4.0 - 1.0) \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 3.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 2.207 \text{ N·m}$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1700 \times 2.207}{60} = \frac{23562.6}{60} = 392.71 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{in} = 220 \times 5 = 1100 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = \frac{392.71}{1100} \times 100 = 35.70 \,\%$$

**Row 2 (I = 8 A):**

$$T_{sh} = (7.0 - 1.5) \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 5.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 4.047 \text{ N·m}$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1550 \times 4.047}{60} = \frac{39417.6}{60} = 656.96 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{in} = 220 \times 8 = 1760 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = \frac{656.96}{1760} \times 100 = 37.33 \,\%$$

**Row 3 (I = 12 A):**

$$T_{sh} = (11.0 - 2.0) \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 9.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 6.622 \text{ N·m}$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1380 \times 6.622}{60} = \frac{57424.5}{60} = 957.07 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{in} = 220 \times 12 = 2640 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = \frac{957.07}{2640} \times 100 = 36.25 \,\%$$

**Row 4 (I = 16 A):**

$$T_{sh} = (15.0 - 2.5) \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 12.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 9.197 \text{ N·m}$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1220 \times 9.197}{60} = \frac{70500.4}{60} = 1175.01 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{in} = 220 \times 16 = 3520 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = \frac{1175.01}{3520} \times 100 = 33.38 \,\%$$

**Row 5 (I = 20 A):**

$$T_{sh} = (19.0 - 3.0) \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 16.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 11.772 \text{ N·m}$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1080 \times 11.772}{60} = \frac{79879.0}{60} = 1331.32 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{in} = 220 \times 20 = 4400 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = \frac{1331.32}{4400} \times 100 = 30.26 \,\%$$

**Consolidated Results Table (1 mark for table presentation):**

| $I$ (A) | $T_{sh}$ (N·m) | $P_{out}$ (W) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $\eta$ (%) | $N$ (rpm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 2.21 | 392.71 | 1100 | 35.70 | 1700 |
| 8 | 4.05 | 656.96 | 1760 | 37.33 | 1550 |
| 12 | 6.62 | 957.07 | 2640 | 36.25 | 1380 |
| 16 | 9.20 | 1175.01 | 3520 | 33.38 | 1220 |
| 20 | 11.77 | 1331.32 | 4400 | 30.26 | 1080 |

**Plotting (1 mark):** The efficiency curve rises from $35.7$ % at $I=5$ A to a peak near $I=8$ A (37.3 %) and then falls. The speed curve falls monotonically from 1700 rpm to 1080 rpm as current rises — confirming the **inverse speed–load characteristic** of a series motor.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a DC series motor** with a neat sketch. State the **two characteristic curves** (torque vs armature current and speed vs armature current) and justify their shape using the relevant equations.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a labelled block diagram, describe the **experimental setup of a brake test on a DC series motor**. Why is a brake test particularly suitable for a DC series motor, but not recommended for a DC shunt motor?  **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

1. **(1 mark)** Construction: yoke, pole cores and pole shoes, field winding wound in series with the armature (fewer turns of thick wire), commutator with brushes, armature winding on a slotted laminated core.
2. **(1.5 marks)** Working: when DC supply is given, current flows through the field and armature. The interaction between the field flux $\phi$ and armature conductors carrying $I_a$ produces a force $F = B I_a l$ per conductor, and a torque $T \propto \phi I_a$.
3. **(1.5 marks)** Since field winding is in series, $\phi \propto I_a$ (before saturation). Therefore $T \propto I_a^2$ — a **parabolic** rising curve, falling slightly at very high currents due to armature reaction and saturation.
4. **(1.5 marks)** The back-EMF equation $E_b = k\phi N$ and $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ give:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k\phi}$$

Since both $\phi$ and $I_a$ rise together, the numerator falls and denominator rises — the speed falls **sharply** as load (current) increases. This is the **inverse** speed–load curve.
5. **(1 mark)** Sketch requirements: labelled $T$–$I_a$ parabolic curve and $N$–$I_a$ drooping curve, axes labelled with units.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

1. **(2 marks)** Block diagram: DC supply $\rightarrow$ starter / rheostat $\rightarrow$ ammeter $\rightarrow$ series motor $\rightarrow$ shaft-coupled brake drum $\rightarrow$ rope with two spring balances $S_1, S_2$ $\rightarrow$ loading screw; tachometer on shaft; voltmeter across motor terminals.
2. **(2 marks)** Why suitable for series motor: the speed falls rapidly with load, so the brake can be loaded without the speed reaching destructive values. The starting torque is naturally high because $T \propto I_a^2$ — so a small increase in load torque is automatically matched by a small increase in current and hence a large increase in motor torque. The motor self-protects against overload stalling.
3. **(1.5 marks)** Why not preferred for shunt motor: a shunt motor has nearly **constant speed** — it is hard to vary the load on a brake over a wide range without slipping the rope. The shunt motor also has very high **no-load speed** which makes brake-loading near no-load impractical. For shunt motors, a **rope-brake test is rarely used** in modern practice; a Swinburne test (no-load) or a Hopkinson test (back-to-back) is preferred.
4. **(1.5 marks)** Advantages of the brake test: gives direct reading of $T_{sh}$ and $P_{out}$ under real load; reveals stray losses; does not require a calibrated DC machine. Disadvantages: brake dissipates all output as heat; cooling is necessary for high-power tests; readings fluctuate due to rope friction variation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap (–1 to –2 marks):** Spring-balance readings are in **kgf**, not Newtons. Forgetting to multiply by $g = 9.81$ is the single most common mistake in KTU answer sheets.
> 2. **Drum Radius Trap (–1 mark):** Sometimes the radius is given as the **diameter** in the question. Re-read carefully; convert to radius before substituting.
> 3. **Series-motor Start (–1 mark):** Examiners always check whether the student has mentioned the *no-load starting danger* and the need for an initial mechanical load. Skipping this costs a mark.
> 4. **Amps vs. Watts mix-up (–1 mark):** Input power is $VI$ in **watts**, not $I^2 R$. The $I^2R$ term is the copper loss, NOT the input.
> 5. **Plotting Marks:** Even if your calculation is correct, you lose marks if you do not **plot the curves with axes labelled, units shown, and a proper title**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **brake test** is a **direct load test** — it directly measures the mechanical output of a motor by absorbing it as friction.
- The **shaft torque** is $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times g \times R$ where $S_1, S_2$ are in kg, $R$ in metres, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- The **output power** is $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ watts, with $N$ in rpm.
- The **input power** is $P_{in} = V \times I$ watts.
- The **efficiency** is $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ %.
- For a **DC series motor**, $T \propto I_a^2$ and $N \propto 1/I_a$ (inverse).
- A series motor **must never be started on no-load** — always apply a small initial mechanical load.
- Performance curves plotted: $T$ vs $I$, $N$ vs $I$, $P_{in}$ vs $I$, $P_{out}$ vs $I$, $\eta$ vs $I$.
- In a series motor, **line current = armature current = field current** — there is only one ammeter reading.
- The brake test is **most suitable for series motors**; for shunt motors, Swinburne's test is preferred.
- After switching off, **release the brake immediately** to prevent the motor from being mechanically locked.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swinburne's Test on a DC Shunt Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Swinburne's Test on a DC Shunt Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Swinburne's Test** is an indirect, regenerative (near-zero power consumption) method of testing a **DC shunt machine** in which the machine is operated as a **motor at no-load (or light load)** at **rated voltage** and **rated speed**. The test is used to **predetermine the efficiency** of the machine at **any desired load** without actually loading the machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCEOL407):** Swinburne's test is a *no-load / constant-loss test*. It separates the **variable losses** (armature copper loss) from the **constant losses** (iron loss + friction + windage loss), so that efficiency at any fractional load $\rightarrow x \cdot P_{FL}$ can be computed analytically.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you want to estimate **how much fuel a heavy truck will consume on a long trip**, but you don't want to actually drive the truck with a full load attached (because that is expensive and risky). So instead, you **start the truck engine and let it idle at no-load for a few minutes**, measure the fuel used per hour, and assume the *idling losses* (engine friction, air drag on the engine, electrical accessories) will remain the same whether the truck is empty or fully loaded. You then add the *extra fuel needed to move the load* (which depends on the load and is calculated mathematically).

> **Swinburne's test is exactly this idea** — but applied to a DC motor:
> - The *idling losses* correspond to **constant losses** (iron + mechanical).
> - The *extra fuel for the load* corresponds to **armature copper loss** ($I_a^2 R_a$), which depends on load current.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Rated Voltage | $V$ | **220 V / 250 V / 440 V DC** |
| Rated Speed | $N$ | **1000 - 3000 rpm** |
| No-load current | $I_0$ | **3 % – 8 %** of full-load current |
| Armature resistance | $R_a$ | **0.1 – 1.0 Ω** |
| Shunt field resistance | $R_{sh}$ | **100 – 500 Ω** |

> [!NOTE]
> The no-load current of a DC shunt motor is typically **very small** (a few amperes), which is why the test consumes negligible input power despite being conducted at full rated voltage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Operating Principle

A DC shunt machine, when operated as a motor, has losses that can be classified into two broad categories:

1. **Constant Losses ($W_c$):** Independent of load current.
   - **Iron (Core) Loss** ($W_i$) — depends on flux density and frequency.
   - **Friction Loss** — brush friction + bearing friction.
   - **Windage Loss** — air resistance on rotating parts.
   
   *These are determined by the speed and flux — which remain essentially constant from no-load to full-load.*

2. **Variable Losses ($W_v$):** Depend on load.
   - **Armature Copper Loss** = $I_a^2 R_a$
   - **Shunt Field Copper Loss** = $V I_{sh}$ (this is *almost* constant since $V$ and $R_{sh}$ are fixed, but conventionally grouped with constant losses)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Assumption of Swinburne's Test:** The constant losses $W_c$ measured at no-load remain constant at *all loads* up to full-load. This is justified because speed $\approx$ constant and flux $\approx$ constant.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Test Procedure

1. The DC shunt machine is **mechanically uncoupled** from any load (belt removed or coupling detached).
2. Field rheostat is set to give **rated voltage** at the armature terminals (using a voltmeter across the armature).
3. The motor is started using a starter (3-point or 4-point starter).
4. Field rheostat is adjusted to obtain **rated speed** (speed is measured with a tachometer).
5. The **no-load input current $I_0$** is recorded using an ammeter.
6. The **no-load input power** = $V \cdot I_0$ is noted.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Shunt field current | $I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | A |
| 2 | No-load armature current | $I_{0a} = I_0 - I_{sh}$ | A |
| 3 | No-load armature Cu loss | $W_{cu,0} = I_{0a}^2 \cdot R_a$ | W |
| 4 | Shunt field Cu loss | $W_{sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = \dfrac{V^2}{R_{sh}}$ | W |
| 5 | Constant losses (iron + friction + windage) | $W_c = V \cdot I_0 - I_{0a}^2 R_a - V I_{sh}$ | W |
| 6 | Approximate constant losses | $W_c \approx V \cdot I_0$ | W (if $I_{0a}^2 R_a$ is neglected) |
| 7 | Armature current at load $I_L$ (motor) | $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ | A |
| 8 | Armature Cu loss at load | $I_a^2 \cdot R_a$ | W |
| 9 | Total losses at load | $W_{total} = I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c$ | W |
| 10 | Motor efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{V I_L - W_{total}}{V I_L} \times 100 \,\%$ | — |
| 11 | Armature current (generator at load) | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | A |
| 12 | Generator efficiency | $\eta_g = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + W_{total}} \times 100 \,\%$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notational Distinction:** In a motor, $I_L$ flows *into* the armature and field in parallel, so $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$. In a generator, $I_L$ flows *out* to the external load, while $I_{sh}$ is drawn from the terminal internally — so $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost-Efficiency in Manufacturing:** Manufacturers can rate each machine without burning enormous energy on load tests.
- **Field-Testing Convenience:** On-site testing of large motors (e.g., 100 kW industrial drives) becomes practical since the test draws only 3–8 % of full-load current.
- **Sub-Optimal Caveat:** It cannot predict performance at loads with significant *speed variation* (e.g., compound machines operating in series-mode) and cannot measure temperature rise (since machine runs unloaded).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Constant Losses

Starting from the **no-load power balance**:

$$P_{in,0} = V \cdot I_0$$

This input power must supply *all* the losses of the machine (since mechanical output at no-load is essentially zero, ignoring rotational friction counter-torque):

$$P_{in,0} = W_{cu,a} + W_{cu,sh} + W_c$$

where
- $W_{cu,a} = I_{0a}^2 \cdot R_a$ — armature copper loss at no-load
- $W_{cu,sh} = V \cdot I_{sh}$ — shunt field copper loss
- $W_c$ — constant (iron + friction + windage) losses

Solving for $W_c$:

$$W_c = P_{in,0} - I_{0a}^2 R_a - V I_{sh}$$

$$\boxed{W_c = V \cdot I_0 - I_{0a}^2 R_a - V \cdot I_{sh}}$$

> Since $I_{0a}$ is small (3–8 % of full load), $I_{0a}^2 R_a$ is *negligible*. So in **engineering approximation**:
> $$W_c \approx V \cdot I_0$$

### 3.2 Efficiency Derivation — Motor Mode

At a line current $I_L$ (load current drawn from supply):

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$$

The armature current is:

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$$

The total losses are:

$$W_{total} = I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c$$

The mechanical output developed is:

$$P_{out} = P_{in} - W_{total} = V I_L - \left[ (I_L - I_{sh})^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c \right]$$

The efficiency is:

$$\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V I_L - (I_L - I_{sh})^2 R_a - V I_{sh} - W_c}{V I_L}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_m = 1 - \dfrac{(I_L - I_{sh})^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c}{V I_L}}$$

### 3.3 Efficiency Derivation — Generator Mode

When the same machine acts as a generator delivering load current $I_L$ at terminal voltage $V$:

$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L$$

The armature current must supply *both* the load and the shunt field (drawn internally from armature):

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$$

The total losses are:

$$W_{total} = (I_L + I_{sh})^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c$$

The mechanical input is:

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + W_{total} = V I_L + (I_L + I_{sh})^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c$$

The efficiency is:

$$\eta_g = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + (I_L + I_{sh})^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_g = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + (I_L + I_{sh})^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A 200 V DC shunt machine has armature resistance of $0.5 \, \Omega$ and shunt field resistance of $200 \, \Omega$. When running as a motor on no-load, it draws $5 \,\text{A}$ from the 200 V supply. Calculate:
1. The constant losses of the machine.
2. The efficiency as a motor when the input current is $40 \,\text{A}$.
3. The efficiency as a generator when delivering a load current of $40 \,\text{A}$ at 200 V.

#### Step 1 — Compute Shunt Field Current & No-load Armature Current

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{200}{200} = 1 \, \text{A}$$

$$I_{0a} = I_0 - I_{sh} = 5 - 1 = 4 \, \text{A}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute No-load Losses

No-load input power:

$$P_{in,0} = V \cdot I_0 = 200 \times 5 = 1000 \, \text{W}$$

No-load armature copper loss:

$$W_{cu,a} = I_{0a}^2 \cdot R_a = 4^2 \times 0.5 = 16 \times 0.5 = 8 \, \text{W}$$

Shunt field copper loss:

$$W_{cu,sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = 200 \times 1 = 200 \, \text{W}$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate Constant Losses

$$W_c = P_{in,0} - W_{cu,a} - W_{cu,sh} = 1000 - 8 - 200 = 792 \, \text{W}$$

> **Answer to (1):** $W_c = \mathbf{792 \, W}$

#### Step 4 — Efficiency as a Motor at $I_L = 40 \, \text{A}$

Armature current:

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 40 - 1 = 39 \, \text{A}$$

Armature copper loss at load:

$$I_a^2 R_a = 39^2 \times 0.5 = 1521 \times 0.5 = 760.5 \, \text{W}$$

Total losses:

$$W_{total} = I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c = 760.5 + 200 + 792 = 1752.5 \, \text{W}$$

Input power:

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 200 \times 40 = 8000 \, \text{W}$$

Output power:

$$P_{out} = P_{in} - W_{total} = 8000 - 1752.5 = 6247.5 \, \text{W}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{6247.5}{8000} \times 100$$

> **Answer to (2):** $\eta_m = \mathbf{78.09 \, \%}$

#### Step 5 — Efficiency as a Generator at $I_L = 40 \, \text{A}$, $V = 200 \, \text{V}$

Armature current (note the sign change):

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 40 + 1 = 41 \, \text{A}$$

Armature copper loss:

$$I_a^2 R_a = 41^2 \times 0.5 = 1681 \times 0.5 = 840.5 \, \text{W}$$

Total losses:

$$W_{total} = 840.5 + 200 + 792 = 1832.5 \, \text{W}$$

Output power:

$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L = 200 \times 40 = 8000 \, \text{W}$$

Input power:

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + W_{total} = 8000 + 1832.5 = 9832.5 \, \text{W}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta_g = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{8000}{9832.5} \times 100$$

> **Answer to (3):** $\eta_g = \mathbf{81.37 \, \%}$

### 3.5 Analytical Loss-Separation Matrix

| Loss Component | Motor No-load | Motor Full-load | Generator Full-load |
|---|---|---|---|
| Iron Loss ($W_i$) | ✓ (included in $W_c$) | ✓ (assumed = $W_c$) | ✓ (assumed = $W_c$) |
| Friction + Windage | ✓ (included in $W_c$) | ✓ (assumed = $W_c$) | ✓ (assumed = $W_c$) |
| Armature $I_a^2 R_a$ | small ($I_{0a}^2 R_a$) | full ($I_a^2 R_a$) | full ($I_a^2 R_a$) |
| Shunt $V I_{sh}$ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Stray Load Loss | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Swinburne's Test Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the complete decision-flow of conducting Swinburne's test, from machine setup through efficiency computation in either mode.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Machine Setup] --> stepB[Couple Machine to No Load]
    stepB --> stepC[Connect Armature and Shunt Field to Rated DC Supply V]
    stepC --> stepD[Adjust Field Rheostat for Rated Speed]
    stepD --> stepE[Record No Load Voltage V]
    stepE --> stepF[Record No Load Current I0]
    stepF --> stepG[Compute Ish = V divided by Rsh]
    stepG --> stepH[Compute I0a = I0 minus Ish]
    stepH --> stepI[Compute No Load Armature Copper Loss]
    stepI --> stepJ[Compute Shunt Field Copper Loss V times Ish]
    stepJ --> stepK[Compute Constant Losses Wc]
    stepK --> stepL[Store Wc as Constant Loss Reference]
    stepL --> stepM{Choose Operating Mode}
    stepM --> stepN[Motor Mode at Load Current IL]
    stepM --> stepO[Generator Mode at Load Current IL]
    stepN --> stepP[Ia motor = IL minus Ish]
    stepO --> stepQ[Ia generator = IL plus Ish]
    stepP --> stepR[Total Losses = Ia squared Ra plus V Ish plus Wc]
    stepQ --> stepS[Total Losses = Ia squared Ra plus V Ish plus Wc]
    stepR --> stepT[Efficiency motor = Output divided by Input]
    stepS --> stepU[Efficiency generator = Output divided by Output plus Losses]
    stepT --> stepV[Report Final Efficiency Percentage]
    stepU --> stepV
```

### 4.2 No-Load Motor Circuit (Equivalent Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepV1[DC Supply Terminal V] --> stepV2[Ammeter A measures I0]
    stepV2 --> stepV3[Shunt Field Branch with Rsh]
    stepV2 --> stepV4[Armature Branch with Ra and Back EMF E]
    stepV3 --> stepV5[Common Neutral Return]
    stepV4 --> stepV5
    stepV5 --> stepV6[Back to Supply Negative Terminal]
    stepV2 -. no load measurement .- stepV7[No Load Input Power equals V times I0]
    stepV4 -. constant flux .- stepV8[Flux phi held constant by V across Rsh]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The circuit has **two parallel paths** across the supply: (a) the armature circuit (Ra + back-EMF E) and (b) the shunt field circuit (Rsh). The ammeter reads the *total* line current $I_0$, which is the sum of $I_{0a}$ and $I_{sh}$.

### 4.3 Cross-Mode Loss-Accounting Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepW1[Constant Losses Wc measured at No Load] --> stepW2[Apply Wc unchanged at all loads]
    stepW2 --> stepW3[Add Armature Cu Loss depending on Mode]
    stepW3 --> stepW4[Motor: Ia squared Ra with Ia = IL minus Ish]
    stepW3 --> stepW5[Generator: Ia squared Ra with Ia = IL plus Ish]
    stepW4 --> stepW6[Add Shunt Field Cu Loss V times Ish in both modes]
    stepW5 --> stepW6
    stepW6 --> stepW7[Compute Efficiency in Selected Mode]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]** What is Swinburne's test? Why is it classified as an *indirect* method of testing?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Swinburne's test is an indirect method of testing a **DC shunt machine** in which the machine is run as a motor at **no-load** at rated voltage and rated speed. The **constant losses** (iron loss + friction + windage loss) are determined from the no-load input power. Since these losses are assumed to remain constant at all loads, the **efficiency at any desired load** can be predetermined without actually loading the machine. It is called *indirect* because the actual loading is **never performed**; efficiency is *calculated* analytically using measured constant losses and assumed load-dependent copper losses. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]** State any **three assumptions** made in the analysis of Swinburne's test.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The constant losses (**iron loss + friction + windage loss**) remain **constant from no-load to full-load**. (1 mark)
2. The shunt field current $I_{sh}$ remains **constant** because the supply voltage $V$ and field resistance $R_{sh}$ are constant. (1 mark)
3. The armature reaction effect is **neglected** (or assumed constant), so flux and speed remain essentially unchanged. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Module Exam Convention:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice means *either* Question A *or* Question B must be answered.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain Swinburne's test on a DC shunt machine with the help of a neat circuit diagram. Discuss the procedure and the role of constant losses.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Aim:** To predetermine the efficiency of a DC shunt machine at any load without actually loading it.

**2. Circuit Diagram Description:**
The armature (A) and shunt field winding (F) are connected in parallel across a DC supply of rated voltage $V$. An ammeter ($A_1$) measures the total line current $I_0$ at no-load. A voltmeter ($V_1$) measures the supply voltage. A field rheostat ($R_{rh}}$) is connected in series with the shunt field to adjust the field current and hence the speed. The armature current $I_{0a}$ is computed indirectly.

**3. Procedure:**
- Disconnect the mechanical load from the machine shaft.
- Set the field rheostat to the maximum resistance position.
- Start the motor using a suitable starter (3-point or 4-point).
- Gradually reduce the field rheostat resistance to bring the motor to **rated speed** at **rated voltage**.
- Record the **no-load line current** $I_0$ and the **supply voltage** $V$.
- The constant losses are calculated.

**4. Role of Constant Losses:**
The constant losses ($W_c$) — comprising iron loss, friction loss, and windage loss — depend only on speed and flux. Since both speed and flux are essentially constant from no-load to full-load in a DC shunt motor, the *same* value of $W_c$ measured at no-load is used in the efficiency formula at *any* load. This is the central premise of Swinburne's test.

**5. Calculation (Standard Formulas):**

$$W_c = V \cdot I_0 - I_{0a}^2 R_a - V I_{sh}$$

where $I_{0a} = I_0 - I_{sh}$ and $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$. (7 marks)

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A **250 V** DC shunt motor has armature resistance of $0.25 \, \Omega$ and shunt field resistance of $125 \, \Omega$. On no-load, the motor draws $5 \, \text{A}$ from the supply. Calculate:
1. The constant losses of the machine.
2. The efficiency of the motor when the line current is **60 A**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $I_{sh}$ and $I_{0a}$:**

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{125} = 2 \, \text{A}$$

$$I_{0a} = I_0 - I_{sh} = 5 - 2 = 3 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — No-load Losses:**

$$P_{in,0} = V \cdot I_0 = 250 \times 5 = 1250 \, \text{W}$$

$$W_{cu,a} = I_{0a}^2 R_a = 3^2 \times 0.25 = 2.25 \, \text{W}$$

$$W_{cu,sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = 250 \times 2 = 500 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Constant Losses:**

$$W_c = 1250 - 2.25 - 500 = 747.75 \, \text{W}$$

> **Answer (1):** $W_c = \mathbf{747.75 \, W}$

**Step 4 — Efficiency at $I_L = 60 \, \text{A}$ (Motor Mode):**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 60 - 2 = 58 \, \text{A}$$

$$I_a^2 R_a = 58^2 \times 0.25 = 3364 \times 0.25 = 841 \, \text{W}$$

$$W_{total} = I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c = 841 + 500 + 747.75 = 2088.75 \, \text{W}$$

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 60 = 15000 \, \text{W}$$

$$P_{out} = P_{in} - W_{total} = 15000 - 2088.75 = 12911.25 \, \text{W}$$

$$\eta_m = \dfrac{12911.25}{15000} \times 100$$

> **Answer (2):** $\eta_m = \mathbf{86.08 \, \%}$ (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** What are the **advantages and disadvantages (limitations)** of Swinburne's test?

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Economical Power Consumption:** The machine is run at no-load, so the input power is only that required to overcome constant losses — typically 3–8 % of full-load rating. (2 marks)
2. **Convenient & Time-Saving:** The test setup is simple and the test can be completed in a few minutes. (1 mark)
3. **Efficiency at Any Load:** Once the constant losses are determined, the efficiency at any fractional load (say, $1/4, 1/2, 3/4,$ or full load) can be computed analytically. (2 marks)
4. **Suitable for Repeated Testing:** The same machine can be tested repeatedly (e.g., before and after rewinding) without affecting its life. (1 mark)
5. **Bidirectional Use:** The test data can be used to predict efficiency *both* as a motor and as a generator. (1 mark)

**Disadvantages / Limitations:**
1. **Applicable Only to Shunt Machines:** Cannot be used for series or short-shunt compound machines where field current varies with load. (2 marks)
2. **No Temperature-Rise Data:** Since the machine is never loaded, heating effects cannot be assessed — which is a major limitation for rating verification. (2 marks)
3. **No Commutation Assessment:** Heavy-load commutation behavior cannot be checked. (1 mark)
4. **Stray Load Losses Ignored:** The test neglects stray load losses, which become significant in large machines. (1 mark)
5. **Constant-Loss Assumption May Fail:** If the machine has a compound winding or wide speed variation, the constant-loss assumption breaks down. (1 mark)

(7 marks)

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A **400 V** DC shunt machine has armature resistance of $0.2 \, \Omega$ and shunt field resistance of $200 \, \Omega$. At no-load, it draws **6 A** as a motor. Calculate:
1. Constant losses of the machine.
2. Efficiency when delivering an output of **20 kW** as a **generator** at 400 V.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — No-load Calculations:**

$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{400}{200} = 2 \, \text{A}$$

$$I_{0a} = I_0 - I_{sh} = 6 - 2 = 4 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — No-load Losses:**

$$P_{in,0} = 400 \times 6 = 2400 \, \text{W}$$

$$W_{cu,a} = 4^2 \times 0.2 = 3.2 \, \text{W}$$

$$W_{cu,sh} = 400 \times 2 = 800 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Constant Losses:**

$$W_c = 2400 - 3.2 - 800 = 1596.8 \, \text{W}$$

> **Answer (1):** $W_c = \mathbf{1596.8 \, W}$

**Step 4 — Generator Mode at 400 V, Output = 20 kW:**

$$P_{out} = V \cdot I_L = 400 \cdot I_L = 20000 \, \text{W}$$

$$I_L = \dfrac{20000}{400} = 50 \, \text{A}$$

For a generator:

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 50 + 2 = 52 \, \text{A}$$

$$I_a^2 R_a = 52^2 \times 0.2 = 2704 \times 0.2 = 540.8 \, \text{W}$$

$$W_{total} = I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + W_c = 540.8 + 800 + 1596.8 = 2937.6 \, \text{W}$$

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + W_{total} = 20000 + 2937.6 = 22937.6 \, \text{W}$$

$$\eta_g = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{20000}{22937.6} \times 100$$

> **Answer (2):** $\eta_g = \mathbf{87.19 \, \%}$ (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Armature Current:** Many students incorrectly write $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ for a *motor* (it should be $I_L - I_{sh}$) and vice-versa. Examiner deducts **1–2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting Shunt Field Copper Loss:** Students often include only $I_a^2 R_a$ in the losses and forget $V I_{sh}$. Examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 3. **Neglecting No-load Armature Cu Loss:** In the constant-losses calculation, students sometimes write $W_c = V I_0$ directly without subtracting $I_{0a}^2 R_a$. While this is acceptable as an *approximation*, in numerical problems where $R_a$ is given, the **exact** formula must be used. Examiner deducts **1 mark** if not used.
> 4. **Confusing Output and Input in Generator Efficiency:** Efficiency = Output / Input = Output / (Output + Losses). Writing Output / (Output − Losses) loses **2 marks** (conceptual error).

---

### Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Definition:** Swinburne's test is a **no-load test** on a DC shunt machine to predetermine **efficiency at any load**.
- ✅ **Mode:** Machine runs as a **motor at no-load** at rated voltage and rated speed.
- ✅ **Central Premise:** Constant losses ($W_c$) measured at no-load are assumed **unchanged** at all loads.
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $W_c = V I_0 - I_{0a}^2 R_a - V I_{sh}$.
- ✅ **Motor Mode Armature Current:** $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$.
- ✅ **Generator Mode Armature Current:** $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$.
- ✅ **Motor Efficiency:** $\eta_m = \dfrac{V I_L - \text{Losses}}{V I_L}$.
- ✅ **Generator Efficiency:** $\eta_g = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + \text{Losses}}$.
- ✅ **Key Assumptions:** $W_c$, $I_{sh}$, speed, flux all remain constant from no-load to full-load.
- ✅ **Biggest Advantage:** Minimal power consumption (only 3–8 % of full-load input).
- ✅ **Biggest Limitation:** Cannot assess temperature rise or commutation under load.
- ✅ **Applicable Machines:** Only **DC shunt** and **level-compound** machines — *not* series machines.
- ✅ **Numerical Tip:** Always check whether the question asks for *motor* or *generator* mode — the armature current formula changes sign.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OC and SC Tests on Single-Phase Transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** OC and SC Tests on Single-Phase Transformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Open Circuit (OC) Test** and **Short Circuit (SC) Test** are the two indirect (non-loading) methods prescribed by KTU 2024 to determine the performance parameters of a single-phase transformer — namely its **equivalent circuit parameters, iron losses, copper losses, efficiency, and voltage regulation** — without actually connecting a physical load to the secondary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> *"OC test is performed on the LV side with HV open-circuited to obtain no-load power factor, core loss component current, magnetizing current, and the shunt branch parameters R₀ and X₀. SC test is performed on the HV side with LV short-circuited to obtain full-load copper loss and the equivalent series parameters R₀₁ and X₀₁ referred to the side of measurement."*

| Test | Energized Side | Other Side | Instrument Reading |
|---|---|---|---|
| OC Test | LV (Primary) | HV Open | V₀c, I₀c, P₀ (Wattmeter) |
| SC Test | HV (Primary) | LV Shorted | Vₛc, Iₛc, Pₛc (Wattmeter) |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you want to judge the health of a long water pipe system without actually opening the tap fully:

- **OC Test (Open Tap Analogy):** Turn on the pump, but leave the tap closed. The motor just hums — this hum represents the **iron/core loss** (magnetizing current doing no useful work, but overcoming hysteresis and eddy currents in the iron core). You measure the hum level (current I₀) and energy wasted as heat (wattmeter P₀).
- **SC Test (Blocked Tap Analogy):** Plug the tap almost completely, then turn on the pump. Water rushes but goes nowhere useful — the energy is entirely lost to friction inside the pipe. This friction is exactly like the **copper loss (I²R)** in the windings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just load the transformer directly?**
> In high-rating transformers (say 500 kVA), loading them would waste enormous energy and require bulky resistive load banks. Indirect OC-SC testing reproduces the losses accurately using a tiny fraction of rated power — typically **< 5% of full load power** — making it the industry-standard acceptance test.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Boundary Conditions

- The OC test is always conducted at **rated voltage and rated frequency**.
- The SC test is always conducted at **rated primary current** (achieved by applying a small percentage — usually **5% to 10%** — of rated voltage).
- All parameters are referred to the side on which the test is conducted, and then transferred to either side using the turns ratio **K = N₂/N₁ = V₂/V₁**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Logic of the OC Test (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

**Why is OC done on the LV side?**
- The LV winding has lower rated voltage → safer to handle → requires lower-rated instruments.
- Rated flux is established in the core at rated voltage; the test must be at rated voltage (since iron loss is voltage-dependent).

**Step-by-step logical flow:**

1. **Apply rated voltage V₀c** to the LV (primary) winding using a variac.
2. **Keep HV (secondary) open** — no load current flows on HV; the only current drawn is the small no-load current I₀c on the LV side.
3. **Read three quantities**: V₀c (voltmeter), I₀c (ammeter), P₀ (wattmeter).
4. Since the secondary is open, **all power P₀ consumed is the iron (core) loss** — because there is no copper loss on the secondary (zero current), and LV copper loss (I₀c²R₁) is negligibly small (I₀c is only 2–5% of full load current).

**Decomposition of No-Load Current:**

The no-load current I₀c has two orthogonal components:
- **Iron-loss component (I_w)** — in phase with V₀c, responsible for hysteresis + eddy current loss.
- **Magnetizing component (I_μ)** — 90° out of phase with V₀c, responsible for creating the working flux in the core.

### 2.2 Conceptual Logic of the SC Test (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

**Why is SC done on the HV side?**
- The HV winding has higher rated voltage but lower rated current.
- During SC test, applied voltage is only 5–10% of rated — applying this small voltage to the LV side (which has low voltage rating) would cause dangerous overcurrent. Applying it to the HV side is safer.

**Step-by-step logical flow:**

1. **Short-circuit the LV (secondary) winding** through a low-resistance link or ammeter.
2. **Apply reduced voltage** to the HV (primary) via variac, gradually increasing it until the **ammeter reads the rated primary current**.
3. **Read three quantities**: Vₛc (voltmeter), Iₛc = I_rated (ammeter), Pₛc (wattmeter).
4. Since voltage is very small (~5% rated), the flux in the core is negligible → **iron loss is negligible**. All power Pₛc is dissipated as **full-load copper loss** in both windings (I²R₁ + I²R₂ referred).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are referred to the side where the test is performed unless explicitly mentioned.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Source Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | No-load power factor | $\cos \phi_0 = \dfrac{P_0}{V_{0c} \times I_{0c}}$ | dimensionless | OC |
| 2 | Iron loss component | $I_w = I_{0c} \cos \phi_0$ | Ampere | OC |
| 3 | Magnetizing component | $I_\mu = I_{0c} \sin \phi_0$ | Ampere | OC |
| 4 | Core-loss resistance | $R_0 = \dfrac{V_{0c}}{I_w}$ | Ohm (Ω) | OC |
| 5 | Magnetizing reactance | $X_0 = \dfrac{V_{0c}}{I_\mu}$ | Ohm (Ω) | OC |
| 6 | SC power factor | $\cos \phi_{sc} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{V_{sc} \times I_{sc}}$ | dimensionless | SC |
| 7 | Equivalent impedance | $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$ | Ohm (Ω) | SC |
| 8 | Equivalent resistance | $R_{01} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^{2}}$ | Ohm (Ω) | SC |
| 9 | Equivalent reactance | $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^{2} - R_{01}^{2}}$ | Ohm (Ω) | SC |
| 10 | Efficiency at fraction x | $\eta = \dfrac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^{2} \cdot P_{cu}} \times 100$ | % | Both |
| 11 | Voltage regulation (lagging) | $VR = \dfrac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V} \times 100$ | % | SC |
| 12 | Voltage regulation (leading) | $VR = \dfrac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi - X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V} \times 100$ | % | SC |
| 13 | Iron loss | $P_i = P_0$ | Watt (W) | OC |
| 14 | Full-load Cu loss | $P_{cu} = P_{sc}$ | Watt (W) | SC |
| 15 | Condition for max efficiency | $x = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{cu}}}$ | fraction | Both |

> **Symbols used:** S = rated apparent power (VA), V = rated voltage, I = rated current, cos φ = load power factor, x = loading fraction (x = 1 for full load).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transformer Manufacturing Industry:** OC and SC tests are mandatory **routine factory tests** as per IS 2026 / IEC 60076 standards before dispatch. The test report is called the **"Losses and Impedance Certificate"**.
- **Condition Monitoring:** Periodic OC-SC tests in service help detect winding short circuits (R₀₁ decreases), inter-turn faults, or core degradation (P_i increases).
- **Substation Commissioning:** Used to verify nameplate data and to coordinate protection relays based on measured impedance.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of OC Test Equivalent Circuit Parameters

**Given:** V₀c, I₀c, P₀ from the OC test on the LV side.

**Step 1 — No-load power factor:**
The wattmeter reads the real power P₀ consumed by the transformer under no-load. Since this is the only power drawn:
$$P_0 = V_{0c} \cdot I_{0c} \cdot \cos \phi_0$$

Rearranging for the power factor angle φ₀:
$$\cos \phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{V_{0c} \cdot I_{0c}}$$

**Step 2 — Resolve I₀c into two perpendicular components:**
The no-load current I₀c can be split into a component in-phase with V₀c (I_w) and a component lagging V₀c by 90° (I_μ).

$$I_w = I_{0c} \cdot \cos \phi_0 \quad \text{(iron loss component, in-phase with voltage)}$$

$$I_\mu = I_{0c} \cdot \sin \phi_0 \quad \text{(magnetizing component, lagging by 90°)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's law to the shunt branch:**
The shunt branch consists of R₀ (representing iron loss) in parallel with X₀ (representing magnetizing reactance). The voltage across this branch is V₀c.

$$R_0 = \frac{V_{0c}}{I_w} = \frac{V_{0c}}{I_{0c} \cdot \cos \phi_0} = \frac{V_{0c}^{2}}{P_0}$$

$$X_0 = \frac{V_{0c}}{I_\mu} = \frac{V_{0c}}{I_{0c} \cdot \sin \phi_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic for Step 3:** Because I_w is in phase with V₀c, it must flow entirely through the resistive branch R₀ (which draws current in phase with voltage). Similarly, I_μ being purely reactive must flow entirely through the inductive branch X₀. Hence Ohm's law is applied separately to each branch.

### 3.2 Derivation of SC Test Equivalent Circuit Parameters

**Given:** Vₛc, Iₛc = I_rated, Pₛc from the SC test on the HV side.

**Step 1 — Equivalent impedance referred to the test side:**
Applying Ohm's law to the entire short-circuited transformer:
$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent resistance from power dissipated:**
The wattmeter reads Pₛc, which is entirely copper loss (I²R) since iron loss is negligible at reduced voltage. Therefore:
$$P_{sc} = I_{sc}^{2} \cdot R_{01}$$

Solving:
$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent reactance from impedance triangle:**
Using the right-angle impedance triangle:
$$Z_{01}^{2} = R_{01}^{2} + X_{01}^{2}$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^{2} - R_{01}^{2}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** R₀₁ and X₀₁ represent the total equivalent resistance and reactance of the entire transformer (both windings combined) referred to the side where the SC test was done.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

**Problem Statement (Model):**
A 5 kVA, 200 V / 400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer gave the following test results:
- **OC Test (on LV side):** V₀c = 200 V, I₀c = 1.0 A, P₀ = 80 W
- **SC Test (on HV side):** Vₛc = 20 V, Iₛc = 12.5 A, Pₛc = 100 W

**Calculate:**
(a) The equivalent circuit parameters referred to the HV side.
(b) Efficiency at full load, 0.8 power factor lagging.
(c) Voltage regulation at full load, 0.8 power factor lagging.

---

### Part (a): Equivalent Circuit Parameters

**OC Side — LV side, R₀ and X₀:**

$$\cos \phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{V_{0c} \cdot I_{0c}} = \frac{80}{200 \times 1.0} = 0.4$$

$$\sin \phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - 0.4^2} = 0.9165$$

$$I_w = 1.0 \times 0.4 = 0.4 \text{ A}, \quad I_\mu = 1.0 \times 0.9165 = 0.9165 \text{ A}$$

$$R_0 = \frac{V_{0c}^{2}}{P_0} = \frac{200^{2}}{80} = 500 \text{ Ω (on LV side)}$$

$$X_0 = \frac{V_{0c}}{I_\mu} = \frac{200}{0.9165} = 218.22 \text{ Ω (on LV side)}$$

**SC Side — HV side, R₀₁ and X₀₁:**

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{20}{12.5} = 1.6 \text{ Ω (on HV side)}$$

$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^{2}} = \frac{100}{12.5^{2}} = 0.64 \text{ Ω (on HV side)}$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^{2} - R_{01}^{2}} = \sqrt{1.6^{2} - 0.64^{2}} = \sqrt{2.56 - 0.4096} = \sqrt{2.1504} = 1.4664 \text{ Ω}$$

**Conversion to HV side using turns ratio K = V₂/V₁ = 400/200 = 2:**

Since OC was on LV, the shunt branch values on HV side:
$$R_0' = K^{2} \times R_0 = 4 \times 500 = 2000 \text{ Ω}$$
$$X_0' = K^{2} \times X_0 = 4 \times 218.22 = 872.88 \text{ Ω}$$

---

### Part (b): Efficiency at Full Load, 0.8 pf lagging

**Step 1 — Identify input/output quantities:**

Full-load secondary current (on HV side, since rating is 5 kVA at 400 V):
$$I_{fl} = \frac{S}{V_2} = \frac{5000}{400} = 12.5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Compute losses:**

Iron loss: $P_i = P_0 = 80 \text{ W}$
Full-load copper loss: $P_{cu} = P_{sc} = 100 \text{ W}$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency formula:**

$$\eta = \frac{V_2 \cdot I_{fl} \cdot \cos\phi}{V_2 \cdot I_{fl} \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + P_{cu}} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{400 \times 12.5 \times 0.8}{400 \times 12.5 \times 0.8 + 80 + 100} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{4000}{4000 + 80 + 100} \times 100 = \frac{4000}{4180} \times 100 = 95.69 \text{ \%}$$

---

### Part (c): Voltage Regulation at 0.8 pf lagging

Using lagging pf formula with values on HV side (I = 12.5 A, V = 400 V):

$$VR = \frac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V} \times 100$$

$$\cos\phi = 0.8, \quad \sin\phi = 0.6$$

$$VR = \frac{12.5 \times (0.64 \times 0.8 + 1.4664 \times 0.6)}{400} \times 100$$

$$VR = \frac{12.5 \times (0.512 + 0.8798)}{400} \times 100 = \frac{12.5 \times 1.3918}{400} \times 100$$

$$VR = \frac{17.3975}{400} \times 100 = 4.349 \text{ \%}$$
