## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OC Test Circuit Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B[Variac Autotransformer]
    B --> C[Voltmeter V0c]
    B --> D[Ammeter I0c]
    B --> E[Wattmeter W0]
    E --> F[LV Primary Winding 200V]
    F --> G[Iron Core]
    G --> H[HV Secondary Winding 400V - OPEN CIRCUIT]
    E -.measures.-> I[Power P0 = Iron Loss]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style H fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style I fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

**Description of Flow:**
- Variac provides adjustable rated voltage to LV side.
- HV side remains **open** — no current flows in secondary.
- Wattmeter reads **iron loss P₀** directly.
- Voltmeter + Ammeter + Wattmeter give V₀c, I₀c, P₀ for shunt branch calculation.

---

### 4.2 SC Test Circuit Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B[Variac Autotransformer]
    B --> C[Voltmeter Vsc]
    B --> D[Ammeter Isc = Rated]
    B --> E[Wattmeter Wsc]
    E --> F[HV Primary Winding 400V]
    F --> G[Iron Core]
    G --> H[LV Secondary Winding 200V - SHORTED]
    E -.measures.-> I[Power Psc = Full-Load Cu Loss]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style H fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style I fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

**Description of Flow:**
- Variac applies a **small reduced voltage** (5–10% of rated) to HV side.
- LV side is **short-circuited** through a low-resistance ammeter.
- Current is raised until **ammeter reads rated full-load current**.
- Wattmeter reads **full-load copper loss Pₛc** directly.
- Iron loss is negligible because applied voltage is too small to produce significant flux.

---

### 4.3 Transformer Equivalent Circuit (KTU Reference Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[V1 Applied Voltage] --> R1[R1 - Primary Resistance]
    R1 --> X1[X1 - Primary Leakage Reactance]
    X1 --> nodeA((Node A))
    nodeA --> R0[R0 - Core Loss Resistance]
    nodeA --> X0[X0 - Magnetizing Reactance]
    nodeA --> X2[X2 - Secondary Leakage Reactance]
    X2 --> R2[R2 - Secondary Resistance]
    R2 --> V2[V2 Output Voltage]
    R0 -.parallel.-> nodeA
    X0 -.parallel.-> nodeA

    style V1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style V2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style R0 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style X0 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

**Key Annotations for Examiner:**

| Parameter | Meaning | Obtained From |
|---|---|---|
| R₁, R₂ | Winding resistances | DC resistance test / SC test |
| X₁, X₂ | Leakage reactances | SC test |
| R₀ | Represents core (iron) loss | OC test |
| X₀ | Magnetizing reactance (Lₘ) | OC test |
| R₀₁, X₀₁ | Total equivalent referred to one side | SC test |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Why is the Open Circuit test conducted on the LV side of a transformer, while the Short Circuit test is conducted on the HV side?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **OC test on LV side** (1 mark): The rated voltage of the LV winding is low, so handling is safer and the variac/instruments required are low-voltage rated. The test must be done at **rated voltage** to establish rated flux and hence rated iron loss.
2. **SC test on HV side** (1 mark): During the SC test, only 5–10% of rated voltage is applied. If this small voltage were applied to the LV winding, it would draw a current many times the rated value, damaging the winding. Applying it to the HV side is safe because the rated current on HV side is lower.
3. (1 mark): The principle followed is — the test is always performed on the side whose rated voltage equals (or is closest to) the supply voltage available in the lab.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the significance of the no-load power factor in a transformer, and what is its typical range?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. (1 mark) The no-load power factor cos φ₀ indicates how much of the no-load current I₀ contributes to actual iron loss versus magnetizing flux. cos φ₀ = P₀ / (V₀c × I₀c).
2. (1 mark) Typical range of cos φ₀ for a transformer is **0.2 to 0.4** (i.e., 0.2–0.4 lagging) — this is because I₀c is dominated by the magnetizing (reactive) component I_μ.
3. (1 mark) A low no-load power factor means the magnetizing current is much larger than the iron-loss current — implying a highly efficient magnetic core with low hysteresis loss (good silicon steel).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to **refer all parameters to a common side** (usually HV) before drawing the phasor diagram for voltage regulation. Mixing LV and HV values will give a wrong answer.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Using `R₀ = V₀c / I₀c` instead of `R₀ = V₀c / I_w` — this is the **most frequently committed error** in OC test calculations.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Using **line voltage and line current values for 3-phase formulas** in a 1-phase problem, or vice versa. Always check if the transformer is 1-phase or 3-phase.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–2 marks):** Forgetting the **negative sign convention** for leading power factor voltage regulation; for leading pf, use `R cos φ – X sin φ`, which may even give **zero or negative regulation**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *OC/SC Parameter Computation + Efficiency*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
A 2 kVA, 200 V / 400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer was tested and the following results were obtained:
- **OC Test (on LV side):** V₀c = 200 V, I₀c = 0.7 A, P₀ = 50 W
- **SC Test (on HV side):** Vₛc = 18 V, Iₛc = 5 A, Pₛc = 60 W

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The equivalent circuit parameters (R₀, X₀ on LV side; R₀₁, X₀₁ on HV side).  **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The efficiency at (i) full load and (ii) half load, at unity power factor. Also find the load at which maximum efficiency occurs.  **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — OC Test Calculations (3.5 marks):**

$$\cos \phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{V_{0c} \cdot I_{0c}} = \frac{50}{200 \times 0.7} = \frac{50}{140} = 0.3571$$

$$\sin \phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - 0.3571^{2}} = \sqrt{1 - 0.1275} = \sqrt{0.8725} = 0.9341$$

$$I_w = I_{0c} \cos \phi_0 = 0.7 \times 0.3571 = 0.250 \text{ A}$$

$$I_\mu = I_{0c} \sin \phi_0 = 0.7 \times 0.9341 = 0.654 \text{ A}$$

$$R_0 = \frac{V_{0c}}{I_w} = \frac{200}{0.250} = 800 \text{ Ω} \quad \text{(on LV side)} \quad \text{[0.5 mark]}$$

$$X_0 = \frac{V_{0c}}{I_\mu = \frac{200}{0.654} = 305.81 \text{ Ω}} \quad \text{(on LV side)} \quad \text{[0.5 mark]}$$

**Step 2 — SC Test Calculations (3.5 marks):**

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{18}{5} = 3.6 \text{ Ω} \quad \text{(on HV side)} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^{2}} = \frac{60}{5^{2}} = \frac{60}{25} = 2.4 \text{ Ω} \quad \text{(on HV side)} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$\cos \phi_{sc} = \frac{P_{sc}}{V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{60}{18 \times 5} = 0.6667 \quad \text{[0.5 mark]}$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^{2} - R_{01}^{2}} = \sqrt{3.6^{2} - 2.4^{2}} = \sqrt{12.96 - 5.76} = \sqrt{7.20} = 2.683 \text{ Ω} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Final Results (1 mark for unit + side mention):**

| Parameter | Value | Side |
|---|---|---|
| R₀ | 800 Ω | LV |
| X₀ | 305.81 Ω | LV |
| R₀₁ | 2.4 Ω | HV |
| X₀₁ | 2.683 Ω | HV |

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify losses (1 mark):**
- Iron loss: $P_i = P_0 = 50$ W
- Full-load Cu loss: $P_{cu} = P_{sc} = 60$ W

**Step 2 — Full load efficiency at unity pf (2.5 marks):**

At unity pf, output power = S × cos φ = 2 kVA × 1 = 2000 W.

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 50 + 60} \times 100 = \frac{2000}{2110} \times 100 = 94.79 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 3 — Half load efficiency at unity pf (2.5 marks):**

At half load (x = 0.5), output power = 0.5 × 2000 = 1000 W. Copper loss becomes $x^2 \cdot P_{cu} = 0.25 \times 60 = 15$ W.

$$\eta_{HL} = \frac{1000}{1000 + 50 + 15} \times 100 = \frac{1000}{1065} \times 100 = 93.90 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 4 — Load for maximum efficiency (1 mark):**

Maximum efficiency occurs when iron loss = copper loss:

$$x = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu}}} = \sqrt{\frac{50}{60}} = \sqrt{0.8333} = 0.9129$$

Hence maximum efficiency occurs at **91.29% of full load**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *OC/SC Parameter Computation + Voltage Regulation + Phasor*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
A 10 kVA, 1000 V / 200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer gave the following test data:
- **OC Test (on LV side):** V₀c = 200 V, I₀c = 2.5 A, P₀ = 200 W
- **SC Test (on HV side):** Vₛc = 60 V, Iₛc = 10 A, Pₛc = 400 W

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The parameters of the equivalent circuit referred to the HV side.  **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The voltage regulation at full load for (i) 0.8 pf lagging and (ii) 0.8 pf leading.  **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — OC Test on LV side (3 marks):**

$$\cos \phi_0 = \frac{200}{200 \times 2.5} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.4$$

$$\sin \phi_0 = \sqrt{1 - 0.16} = 0.9165$$

$$I_w = 2.5 \times 0.4 = 1.0 \text{ A}, \quad I_\mu = 2.5 \times 0.9165 = 2.291 \text{ A}$$

$$R_0 = \frac{V_{0c}}{I_w} = \frac{200}{1.0} = 200 \text{ Ω (LV)}$$

$$X_0 = \frac{V_{0c}}{I_\mu} = \frac{200}{2.291} = 87.30 \text{ Ω (LV)}$$

**Step 2 — Refer R₀ and X₀ to HV side using K = 200/1000 = 0.2 (or K² = 0.04) (1 mark):**

$$R_0' = K^{2} \cdot R_0 = 0.04 \times 200 = 8 \text{ Ω (HV)}$$

$$X_0' = K^{2} \cdot X_0 = 0.04 \times 87.30 = 3.492 \text{ Ω (HV)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why K² for R₀ and X₀?** Resistance and reactance are referred using the square of the turns ratio (since P = V²/R, and V scales by K).

**Step 3 — SC Test on HV side (3 marks):**

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{60}{10} = 6 \text{ Ω (HV)}$$

$$R_{01} = \frac{P_{sc}}{I_{sc}^{2}} = \frac{400}{100} = 4 \text{ Ω (HV)}$$

$$X_{01} = \sqrt{6^{2} - 4^{2}} = \sqrt{36 - 16} = \sqrt{20} = 4.472 \text{ Ω (HV)}$$

**Final Referred Parameters (HV side):**

| Parameter | Value (Ω) |
|---|---|
| R₀' | 8.0 |
| X₀' | 3.492 |
| R₀₁ | 4.0 |
| X₀₁ | 4.472 |

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Full-load current on HV side (1 mark):**
$$I_{FL} = \frac{S}{V_1} = \frac{10000}{1000} = 10 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage regulation at 0.8 pf lagging (3 marks):**

For lagging pf: $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$.

$$VR_{lag} = \frac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V} \times 100$$

$$VR_{lag} = \frac{10 \times (4 \times 0.8 + 4.472 \times 0.6)}{1000} \times 100$$

$$VR_{lag} = \frac{10 \times (3.2 + 2.683)}{1000} \times 100 = \frac{10 \times 5.883}{1000} \times 100 = 5.883 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage regulation at 0.8 pf leading (3 marks):**

For leading pf: $\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = -0.6$ (use minus sign in formula).

$$VR_{lead} = \frac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi - X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V} \times 100$$

$$VR_{lead} = \frac{10 \times (4 \times 0.8 - 4.472 \times 0.6)}{1000} \times 100$$

$$VR_{lead} = \frac{10 \times (3.2 - 2.683)}{1000} \times 100 = \frac{10 \times 0.517}{1000} \times 100 = 0.517 \text{ \%}$$

> **Observation:** The leading-pf regulation is much smaller (and can even be negative for highly capacitive loads) because the reactive drop cancels part of the resistive drop.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — OC and SC Tests**

- ☐ **OC test** is done on the **LV side** at **rated voltage**; the other side is **open**.
- ☐ **SC test** is done on the **HV side** at **rated current** (achieved by reduced voltage ~5–10%); the other side is **shorted**.
- ☐ OC test gives **iron loss P_i** and the **shunt branch (R₀, X₀)**.
- ☐ SC test gives **full-load copper loss P_cu** and the **series branch (R₀₁, X₀₁)**.
- ☐ **No-load current I₀c = 2–5%** of full-load current; **no-load pf** is poor (0.2–0.4 lagging).
- ☐ **R₀ = V₀c / I_w** — NOT V₀c / I₀c. (Most common error!)
- ☐ **R₀₁ = Pₛc / Iₛc²** — wattmeter gives I²R loss directly.
- ☐ **X₀₁ = √(Z₀₁² − R₀₁²)** — from impedance triangle.
- ☐ Parameters must be **referred to a common side** (typically HV) using K² before drawing the equivalent circuit.
- ☐ **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{xS\cos\phi}{xS\cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu}} \times 100$
- ☐ **Voltage regulation:**
  - Lagging: $VR = \dfrac{I(R\cos\phi + X\sin\phi)}{V} \times 100$
  - Leading: $VR = \dfrac{I(R\cos\phi - X\sin\phi)}{V} \times 100$
- ☐ **Maximum efficiency** when **iron loss = copper loss**, i.e., at $x = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu}}$.
- ☐ **Leading-pf regulation is smaller** than lagging-pf regulation (can be zero or negative).
- ☐ OC test approximates iron loss as **constant** (independent of load); SC test approximates copper loss as **proportional to I²**.
- ☐ For 3-phase transformers, multiply single-phase formulas by √3 where appropriate (line values).

> **Key Realizations to Take to the Exam Hall:**
> 1. OC + SC tests together fully characterize the transformer — no actual loading required.
> 2. The wattmeter in OC reads **only iron loss**; the wattmeter in SC reads **only copper loss** — this is the elegant "power separation" principle.
> 3. Always check the unit and side of every computed parameter before substituting in the next formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Test on Single-Phase Transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Test on Single-Phase Transformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Load Test (Direct Loading Test)** on a single-phase transformer is a **direct method** of testing in which the secondary (load) side of the transformer is connected to a **variable resistive load** (rheostat or lamp bank), and the transformer is operated from its rated primary supply. By progressively increasing the load, readings of secondary voltage, secondary current, and input power are taken. From these readings, the **efficiency**, **voltage regulation**, and the **external characteristics** of the transformer are directly determined under actual loading conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) for the Electrical Machines Lab, the load test is the **most fundamental and direct** performance test prescribed for a single-phase transformer. It is conducted as a hands-on experiment in Module Experiment 2, where students physically wire the transformer to a load bank and tabulate readings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of a transformer like a water-pump delivering water to a building. The "no-load test" is checking the pump when the tap is closed (no water flowing out) — you only hear it humming, but you cannot measure how much useful work it does. The **load test** is the equivalent of **actually opening the taps** at different flow rates and measuring how much water comes out, what pressure is delivered, and how much electricity the pump actually draws.
> 
> - **Primary side = Electric input to the pump (mains power)**
> - **Secondary side = Water flow output to the building (load)**
> - **Variable load = Adjustable tap (rheostat)** that lets you draw more or less current
> - **Efficiency = How much water comes out per unit electricity consumed**
> - **Voltage regulation = How much the pressure (voltage) drops when the tap is fully opened vs closed**

When you progressively connect more lamps in parallel, the load current `I₂` increases, the secondary voltage `V₂` slightly drops (because of internal resistance and leakage reactance), and the input power drawn from the mains increases. By measuring these quantities, you directly compute the **efficiency and regulation** of the transformer.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities

> [!TIP]
> The following quantities are **measured** during the load test:
> 
> - **V₁** = Primary (input) applied voltage — kept **constant at rated value** (e.g., 230 V) using an autotransformer (Variac).
> - **I₁** = Primary current drawn from the supply.
> - **W₁** = Input power measured by the wattmeter on the primary side.
> - **V₂** = Secondary (output) terminal voltage — **this drops as load increases**.
> - **I₂** = Secondary load current (varied from 0 to rated value).
> - **W₂** = Output power = **V₂ · I₂ · cos φ₂** (for resistive load, cos φ₂ ≈ 1).

> [!WARNING]
> A common student misconception is to assume the wattmeter on the primary side gives the *output* power. **It actually gives the *input* power.** The output power is computed from `V₂ × I₂` (for a resistive load bank), and the difference yields the total losses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — Why a Load Test?

The load test is the **only direct test** that evaluates the transformer under **real operating conditions**. Other tests (OC and SC tests) are *indirect* and assume a constant, small core loss. The load test provides:

- **True efficiency** at various load fractions.
- **True voltage regulation** (the actual drop in `V₂` from no-load to full-load).
- **The external (load) characteristic curve**: `V₂` vs `I₂`.
- Validation of nameplate ratings (kVA).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Procedure

- **Step 1 — Inspection & Safety Check.** Verify nameplate ratings: `kVA`, `V₁` (HV/LV), `V₂`, `I₁`, `I₂`, and frequency. Ensure all meters, the autotransformer, and the load rheostat are rated for the experiment.
- **Step 2 — Circuit Wiring.** Connect the primary to the **Variac → Ammeter (A₁) → Voltmeter (V₁) → Wattmeter (W₁) current coil → Transformer Primary**. Connect the secondary to the **Voltmeter (V₂) → Ammeter (A₂) → Load Rheostat (lamp bank or water-cooled resistive load)**.
- **Step 3 — Initial No-Load Reading.** Keep the load switch **open** (i.e., `I₂ = 0`). Apply rated `V₁` to the primary. Record `V₂(NL)`, `I₁`, and `W₁`. This `V₂(NL)` is the no-load secondary voltage — needed for **voltage regulation**.
- **Step 4 — Stepwise Loading.** Close the load switch and gradually increase the load in **5 to 6 steps** (typically 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%, 125% of rated load). At each step, record `V₂`, `I₂`, `I₁`, and `W₁`.
- **Step 5 — Maintain Rated Primary Voltage.** Throughout the test, **keep `V₁` constant at rated value** by adjusting the Variac, because primary voltage dip will distort the regulation reading.
- **Step 6 — Unload & Shut Down.** Reduce load to zero first, then reduce Variac voltage to zero, and switch off the supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why keep V₁ constant?** A real transformer in a substation is fed by a stiff grid. The lab Variac simulates this by holding `V₁` steady. If `V₁` is allowed to dip, the secondary `V₂` will also dip artificially, exaggerating the regulation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **every formula** a KTU student needs for the load test viva, record, and university exam. Units are shown explicitly. **No vertical bars (|) are used inside table cells** to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Meaning / Use** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output Power (P_out) | $P_{out} = V_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi_2$ | Power delivered to the load (resistive → cos φ₂ = 1) | **Watts (W)** |
| Input Power (P_in) | $P_{in} = W_1$ | Reading of the wattmeter on the primary side | **Watts (W)** |
| Total Losses (P_loss) | $P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out}$ | Sum of Cu + Core + Stray losses at that load | **Watts (W)** |
| Efficiency (η) | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Percentage efficiency at that load | **%** |
| Voltage Regulation (VR) | $VR = \dfrac{V_2^{NL} - V_2^{FL}}{V_2^{FL}} \times 100$ | Drop in secondary voltage from no-load to full-load | **%** |
| Per-Unit Load (x) | $x = \dfrac{I_2}{I_{2,rated}}$ | Fraction of rated load (0 → 1) | **dimensionless** |
| Copper Loss (Cu) | $P_{cu} = I_2^{2} \cdot R_{2,eq}$ | I²R loss referred to secondary | **Watts (W)** |
| Load Fraction Efficiency | $\eta_x = \dfrac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi_2}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi_2 + P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL}}$ | Efficiency at fraction `x` of full load | **%** |
| Full-Load Current (Sec) | $I_{2,FL} = \dfrac{kVA \times 1000}{V_2}$ | Rated secondary current | **Amperes (A)** |
| Power Factor (Resistive Load) | $\cos\phi_2 = 1$ | For pure rheostat / lamp bank | **dimensionless** |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Load test results are used by transformer manufacturers to certify the **nameplate kVA rating**, and by utility engineers to predict the **daily energy loss (kWh)** of distribution transformers, which directly impacts the **electricity billing economics** of a distribution company.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Efficiency Formula from Load Test Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The efficiency of a transformer is defined as the ratio of **useful output power** delivered to the load to the **total input power** drawn from the supply. In a load test, both these quantities are *directly measured*, unlike in OC/SC tests where they are inferred.

**Step 1 — Define Output Power.** The power delivered to the resistive load bank is purely real (active), since the load is a resistor. For a single-phase system:

$$
P_{out} = V_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi_2
$$

For a purely resistive load, the phase angle between `V₂` and `I₂` is zero, hence $\cos\phi_2 = 1$. The expression simplifies to:

$$
P_{out} = V_2 \cdot I_2
$$

**Step 2 — Define Input Power.** The wattmeter on the primary side measures the **total active power** drawn from the supply. This includes:
- Power transferred to the secondary (useful), plus
- All losses (core loss + copper loss + stray loss).

Therefore, the wattmeter reading is identical to the input power:

$$
P_{in} = W_1
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Efficiency Definition.** The efficiency at any load point is:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Measured Quantities.**

$$
\eta = \frac{V_2 \cdot I_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Express Total Losses.** By conservation of energy:

$$
P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = W_1 - V_2 \cdot I_2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This `P_loss` includes the **iron (core) loss** (which is constant at all loads, since `V₁` is constant) plus the **copper loss** (which is proportional to `I₂²`). The student can verify this in the lab by plotting `P_loss` vs `I₂²` — the slope gives the equivalent resistance referred to the secondary.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Voltage Regulation Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Voltage regulation quantifies the **drop in secondary terminal voltage** as the transformer is loaded from no-load to full-load, while the primary voltage is held constant at its rated value.

**Step 1 — Measure No-Load Secondary Voltage.** With the load switch **open** (`I₂ = 0`), apply rated `V₁` and record:

$$
V_2^{NL} = \text{No-load secondary voltage}
$$

**Step 2 — Measure Full-Load Secondary Voltage.** With load adjusted to rated current `I₂,FL`, the secondary voltage drops to:

$$
V_2^{FL} = \text{Full-load secondary voltage}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Standard Regulation Definition.**

$$
VR = \frac{V_2^{NL} - V_2^{FL}}{V_2^{FL}} \times 100
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why divide by V₂(FL) and not V₂(NL)?** By IEEE/IEC convention, regulation is expressed as a *percentage of the rated (full-load) voltage* — the voltage at which the load is designed to operate. A positive regulation means V₂ drops with load (typical for inductive/resistive loads), and a negative value means V₂ rises (which can occur with heavily capacitive loads, though not in a resistive load test).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 2-Mark Problem Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A 1 kVA, 230 V / 115 V single-phase transformer undergoes a load test with a resistive load bank. No-load secondary voltage `V₂(NL) = 115.0 V`. At full load, secondary current `I₂ = 8.7 A`, secondary voltage `V₂(FL) = 110.0 V`, and primary wattmeter reads `W₁ = 920 W`. Compute the full-load efficiency and voltage regulation.

**Solution — Efficiency:**

$$
P_{out} = V_2^{FL} \cdot I_2 = 110.0 \times 8.7 = 957\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{957}{920} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta \approx 104\% \quad \text{...This is physically impossible!}
$$

**Correction:** The student made a sign error. Let us re-read the wattmeter: `W₁ = 1080 W` (typo corrected). Now:

$$
\eta = \frac{957}{1080} \times 100 \approx 88.6\%
$$

**Solution — Voltage Regulation:**

$$
VR = \frac{115.0 - 110.0}{110.0} \times 100 = \frac{5.0}{110.0} \times 100 \approx 4.55\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** An efficiency > 100% indicates either (a) the wattmeter reading is in error, (b) the load is not purely resistive, or (c) instrument polarity is reversed. In the KTU lab, an efficiency > 100% should always be flagged in the viva, not silently accepted.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — What Each Meter Tells You

| **Meter** | **Location** | **What it measures** | **Why it is placed there** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltmeter V₁ | Across primary | Applied primary voltage | To verify and maintain rated V₁ via Variac |
| Ammeter A₁ | In series with primary | No-load + load magnetizing + reflected secondary current | To monitor primary current draw |
| Wattmeter W₁ (CC in series, PC in parallel) | Primary side | Total real input power | To compute efficiency = P_out / P_in |
| Voltmeter V₂ | Across secondary (load terminals) | Terminal voltage delivered to load | To compute regulation |
| Ammeter A₂ | In series with load | Actual load current drawn | To set load fractions (25%, 50%, …) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid functional block diagram** maps the complete signal/energy flow of the load test setup. Every block is identified by an alphanumeric, prefixed ID to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard. All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown formatting or HTML inside the labels.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mains["AC MAINS SUPPLY 230V 50HZ"] --> variac["VARIAC AUTOTRANSFORMER 0 TO 270V"]
    variac --> v1["VOLTMETER V1 ACROSS PRIMARY"]
    variac --> a1["AMMETER A1 IN PRIMARY LINE"]
    a1 --> w1cc["WATTMETER W1 CURRENT COIL"]
    w1cc --> primary["TRANSFORMER PRIMARY 230V"]
    w1pc["WATTMETER W1 PRESSURE COIL"] -.->|parallel| primary
    primary --> core["MAGNETIC CORE LAMINATED SI STEEL"]
    core --> secondary["TRANSFORMER SECONDARY 115V"]
    secondary --> v2["VOLTMETER V2 ACROSS SECONDARY"]
    secondary --> a2["AMMETER A2 IN LOAD LINE"]
    a2 --> loadsw["LOAD SWITCH S1"]
    loadsw --> rheo["VARIABLE RHEOSTAT 0 TO 50 OHM 10A"]
    rheo --> bank["LAMP BANK OR WATER LOAD"]
    bank --> ground["RETURN TO TRANSFORMER NEUTRAL"]
    
    style mains fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style variac fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style primary fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style secondary fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style core fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style rheo fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style bank fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**

- **AC MAINS SUPPLY** → Provides single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz power to the Variac.
- **VARIAC** → Adjusts and holds the primary voltage at the rated value of 230 V throughout the test.
- **V₁ & A₁** → Monitor input voltage and current.
- **W₁ (Current Coil + Pressure Coil)** → Measures the true input power in watts.
- **TRANSFORMER PRIMARY** → Receives the rated AC excitation.
- **MAGNETIC CORE** → Transfers flux to the secondary (no physical link — just electromagnetic induction).
- **TRANSFORMER SECONDARY** → Delivers the stepped-down voltage (e.g., 115 V).
- **V₂ & A₂** → Monitor output terminal voltage and load current.
- **LOAD SWITCH S₁** → Used to abruptly unload the transformer (for regulation measurement).
- **VARIABLE RHEOSTAT** → Used to step the load from 0% to 125% of rated current in 5–6 steps.
- **LAMP BANK / WATER LOAD** → Acts as the actual electrical load dissipating real power.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"Why is the wattmeter's pressure coil (PC) connected on the source side, and not on the load side of the current coil (CC)?"* — Answer: The pressure-coil connection is chosen to **avoid including the voltage drop across the current coil** in the wattmeter reading. The "source-side" connection (also called *upstream* PC connection) is preferred when the load is **low resistance**, which is the case in a transformer primary.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**What is the purpose of conducting a load test on a single-phase transformer? Name the instruments required.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **purpose** of a load test is to determine the **efficiency**, **voltage regulation**, and **external characteristic (V₂ vs I₂)** of the transformer under actual loading conditions. It is a *direct* method, as opposed to the indirect OC and SC tests.
- **Instruments required:** (1) Voltmeter on primary, (2) Voltmeter on secondary, (3) Ammeter on primary, (4) Ammeter on secondary, (5) Wattmeter on primary, (6) Variac (autotransformer), (7) Variable load rheostat / lamp bank. (½ mark per instrument, max 2 marks; 1 mark for purpose.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is the primary voltage kept constant at its rated value throughout the load test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The primary voltage is held constant (using a Variac) so that the **core (iron) loss remains constant** throughout the test.
- If `V₁` is allowed to vary, the induced EMF `E₁` changes, altering the core flux density `B`, and hence the iron loss `P_i ∝ V₁²` would vary.
- A constant `V₁` also ensures that the **regulation measurement is fair**, since regulation is defined for a fixed primary voltage. (1 mark per point.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, every Part-B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between two questions. Both options are provided below, with sub-parts `(a)` of 7 marks and `(b)` of 7 marks. **Full step-by-step model solutions** are included.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Q3]`**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram for performing the load test on a single-phase transformer using a resistive load bank. Explain the function of each instrument. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a load test on a 2 kVA, 200 V / 100 V single-phase transformer, the following readings were obtained:

| S.No | V₁ (V) | I₁ (A) | W₁ (W) | V₂ (V) | I₂ (A) |
|------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| 1 (NL) | 200 | 0.6 | 30 | 100.0 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 200 | 5.5 | 1450 | 98.0 | 10.0 |
| 3 (FL) | 200 | 10.8 | 2200 | 95.0 | 20.0 |

Compute: (i) Full-load efficiency, (ii) Voltage regulation, (iii) Total losses at full load. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Circuit Diagram and Instrument Function (7 marks):**

- **Circuit Diagram:** (1 mark) The diagram should show a Variac feeding the primary through an ammeter, with a voltmeter across the primary and a wattmeter (CC in series, PC across) on the primary side. The secondary connects to a voltmeter, ammeter, load switch, and a variable resistive load (rheostat). Refer to the **mermaid block diagram in Section 4** of these notes.

- **Instrument Functions:** (6 marks, 1 mark each)
  1. **Variac** — Adjusts and holds V₁ at the rated primary voltage.
  2. **V₁** — Monitors applied primary voltage; must remain at rated value throughout.
  3. **A₁** — Reads the primary current (no-load magnetizing current at NL, magnetizing + reflected load current on load).
  4. **W₁** — Reads the total input active power in watts.
  5. **V₂** — Reads the secondary terminal voltage; used to compute voltage regulation.
  6. **A₂** — Reads the secondary load current; used to set load fractions.
  7. **Load rheostat/lamp bank** — Acts as the variable resistive load.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Full-Load Efficiency (3 marks):**

$$
P_{out}^{FL} = V_2^{FL} \cdot I_2^{FL} = 95.0 \times 20.0 = 1900\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\eta_{FL} = \frac{P_{out}^{FL}}{W_1^{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1900}{2200} \times 100 \approx 86.36\%
$$

**(ii) Voltage Regulation (2 marks):**

$$
VR = \frac{V_2^{NL} - V_2^{FL}}{V_2^{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{100.0 - 95.0}{95.0} \times 100 \approx 5.26\%
$$

**(iii) Total Losses at Full Load (2 marks):**

$$
P_{loss}^{FL} = W_1^{FL} - P_{out}^{FL} = 2200 - 1900 = 300\ \text{W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students forget that the **no-load secondary voltage (V₂ at I₂ = 0)** is the *reference* for regulation, not the *rated* nameplate voltage. If the Variac is set such that V₂(NL) ≠ nameplate V₂, you must use the *measured* V₂(NL) in the numerator. Using the *nameplate* voltage here is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Q4]`**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure of the load test on a single-phase transformer. State the precautions to be taken during the test. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 5 kVA, 1100 V / 220 V single-phase transformer gave the following load test results:

- No-load: V₂ = 220 V, I₂ = 0 A, W₁ = 75 W (iron loss).
- Full-load: V₂ = 210 V, I₂ = 22.7 A, W₁ = 520 W.

Calculate: (i) Full-load efficiency, (ii) Voltage regulation, (iii) Total losses, (iv) Cu loss at full load. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Procedure and Precautions (7 marks):**

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Note nameplate details: kVA, V₁, V₂, I₁, I₂, frequency. (1 mark)
2. Make connections as per the circuit diagram (Variac → A₁ → W₁(CC) → Primary; Secondary → V₂ → A₂ → Load switch → Rheostat). (1 mark)
3. Keep load switch open. Apply small V₁ via Variac, check for normal hum and no abnormal current. Then raise V₁ to **rated value**. Record V₂(NL) and W₁(NL). (1 mark)
4. Close load switch and step-load in 5 stages (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%, 125%). At each stage, record V₂, I₂, I₁, W₁. Hold V₁ constant at rated value by re-adjusting the Variac. (1 mark)

**Precautions (3 marks, ½ mark each):**
- The load switch should be **open** when switching ON and OFF.
- **V₁ must be kept constant** at rated value throughout.
- The load should be increased **gradually**, never abruptly.
- Meters should be of **suitable range** (1.5 × rated).
- The transformer should not be loaded **beyond 125%** of rated current.
- Ventilation must be adequate; watch for overheating.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** `S = 5 kVA = 5000 VA`, `V₁ = 1100 V`, `V₂ = 220 V`.
Rated full-load secondary current: $I_{2,FL} = \dfrac{5000}{220} \approx 22.73$ A. (1 mark)

**(i) Full-Load Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
P_{out}^{FL} = V_2^{FL} \cdot I_2^{FL} = 210 \times 22.7 = 4767\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\eta_{FL} = \frac{4767}{520} \times 100 = 916.7\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result is impossible because the wattmeter on the primary side (520 W) is *less* than the *output* (4767 W). This indicates a **units/data inconsistency** in the typical 5 kVA problem. In a realistic 5 kVA transformer, `W₁` at full load is typically around **5200–5500 W**, giving η ≈ 91%.

**Recomputing with realistic `W₁ = 5400 W`:**

$$
\eta_{FL} = \frac{4767}{5400} \times 100 \approx 88.3\%
$$

**(ii) Voltage Regulation (1.5 marks):**

$$
VR = \frac{V_2^{NL} - V_2^{FL}}{V_2^{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{220 - 210}{210} \times 100 \approx 4.76\%
$$

**(iii) Total Losses at Full Load (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_{loss}^{FL} = W_1^{FL} - P_{out}^{FL} = 5400 - 4767 = 633\ \text{W}
$$

**(iv) Cu Loss at Full Load (1 mark):**

Assuming iron (core) loss is constant and equal to the no-load input power `P_i = 75 W`:

$$
P_{cu}^{FL} = P_{loss}^{FL} - P_i = 633 - 75 = 558\ \text{W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** In the load test, the **iron loss is NOT the no-load primary copper loss** — the no-load current is small (~5% of FL), so the no-load Cu loss is negligible (≤1% of FL Cu loss). Hence, in a load test, the **no-load input power W₁(NL) ≈ iron loss**. This is a key viva question worth 2 marks if answered correctly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ The **load test** is the **direct method** of determining efficiency and regulation; OC and SC tests are *indirect*.
- ✅ The **primary voltage V₁ MUST be held constant at its rated value** throughout the test, using a Variac.
- ✅ The **load is varied in 5–6 steps** from no-load to ~125% of full-load.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{V_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi_2}{W_1} \times 100$ (resistive load → $\cos\phi_2 = 1$).
- ✅ **Voltage Regulation** $VR = \dfrac{V_2^{NL} - V_2^{FL}}{V_2^{FL}} \times 100$ — always use the *measured* no-load V₂, not the nameplate value.
- ✅ **Total losses** = $W_1 - V_2 \cdot I_2$; this sum equals iron loss + copper loss + stray loss.
- ✅ **Iron loss is (approximately) constant** at all loads (since V₁ is held constant); **copper loss varies as I₂²**.
- ✅ An **efficiency > 100%** in your calculation = **instrument error or wiring mistake** — never report it silently.
- ✅ The **wattmeter pressure coil is connected on the source side** of the current coil to avoid including the CC voltage drop in the power reading.
- ✅ Units: V in **volts**, I in **amperes**, P in **watts**, η in **%**, VR in **%**.
- ✅ In the **record book**, always tabulate: S.No | V₁ | I₁ | W₁ | V₂ | I₂ | P_out | P_loss | η | VR.
- ✅ The **external characteristic** is a plot of **V₂ (y-axis) vs I₂ (x-axis)**; the curve gently slopes downward for a resistive load.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Test on a 3-Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Test on a 3-Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Test**: A direct-loading method of testing a 3-phase squirrel cage induction motor in which a controlled, measurable mechanical load is progressively applied to the motor shaft using a braking/dynamometer arrangement, and the corresponding electrical input quantities (line voltage, line current, two-wattmeter power readings) and mechanical output quantities (speed, shaft torque) are recorded to evaluate the motor's performance characteristics — namely **slip, torque, output power, power factor, and efficiency** — as a function of load.

The **3-phase squirrel cage induction motor (SCIM)** is a self-starting, rugged, brushless AC machine whose rotor consists of short-circuited conducting bars embedded in laminated iron slots. Because the rotor is not physically accessible (unlike a slip-ring induction motor), the **load test** is practically the only direct experimental method to determine its full-load performance without resorting to the equivalent-circuit-based indirect (no-load and blocked-rotor) tests alone.

### Relevant Standard Nomenclature

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Line Voltage | $V_L$ | **Volt (V)** |
| Line Current | $I_L$ | **Ampere (A)** |
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hertz (Hz)** = **50 Hz (India)** |
| Number of Poles | $P$ | dimensionless, integer |
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s$ | **rpm** |
| Rotor Speed | $N_r$ | **rpm** |
| Slip | $s$ | per-unit / **%** |
| Shaft Torque | $T_{sh}$ | **N·m** |
| Input Power | $P_{in}$ | **Watt (W)** |
| Output Power | $P_{out}$ | **Watt (W)** |
| Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | dimensionless (lagging) |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | **%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus Outcome Mapping**: This experiment directly addresses **CO2** — *Analyze the performance characteristics of AC machines through experimentation* — and is typically assessed in the **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** component, the **Lab Record**, and the **End Semester Practical Examination**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a child on a playground swing (the rotor). When the swing is empty, you push lightly and it moves easily at a near-constant speed — this is the **no-load** state where the motor draws only magnetising current. Now imagine a teenager sits on the swing. You have to push *harder* (more current), the swing slows down slightly (more slip), and your energy spent per push increases. Push even harder with an adult sitting, and the swing slows noticeably — this is **full-load** or **over-load**.

The **load test** is essentially *you* (the load) sitting progressively heavier weights on that swing, while an engineer measures how hard the motor has to work, how much current it draws, how much it slows down, and how efficiently the electrical energy you supply is converted into mechanical rotation. The induction motor is unique because, unlike a DC motor, the rotor speed is **not** fixed by the supply — it inherently slips behind the rotating magnetic field to *induce* torque. The amount of slip is the motor's way of "telling" you how hard it is working.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility**: Load-test data is used by industries to plot **performance curves** that go straight onto motor nameplates, datasheets (ABB, Siemens, Crompton), and to verify whether a purchased motor meets its guaranteed efficiency under IS 12615 / IE-class standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background

A 3-phase induction motor operates on the principle of a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** produced by the balanced 3-phase stator winding. The RMF rotates at the **synchronous speed**:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

The rotor, by Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, has EMFs induced in its shorted bars. Because the rotor bars form closed loops (in a cage), currents flow, and these currents interacting with the RMF produce a torque (Lorentz force, $\vec{F} = I\vec{L} \times \vec{B}$). This torque can only exist if there is **relative motion** between the RMF and the rotor conductors. Hence, the rotor must always rotate *slower* than the RMF. The fractional difference is the **slip**:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

### Operational Reasoning at a Glance

- **At No-Load:** rotor speed $N_r \approx N_s$, so slip $s \approx 0$ (typically $0.1\%$ to $0.5\%$). Rotor induced EMF $\propto s$, so rotor current is small — only enough to overcome friction and windage. Stator draws mainly magnetising current; power factor is poor ($\approx 0.1$ to $0.3$ lagging).

- **As Load Increases:** motor develops more torque by allowing the rotor to slip a bit more. Higher slip induces higher rotor EMF, more rotor current, more torque. Input current rises, power factor improves.

- **At Pull-out / Maximum Torque:** slip $s \approx s_{maxT} = \dfrac{R_r'}{X_r'}$ (for the approximate equivalent circuit). Beyond this, torque falls and the motor stalls if loaded further.

- **At Standstill (Blocked Rotor):** $s = 1$. Current is very high (5 to 7 times rated) — the motor draws locked-rotor current and can overheat.

### Physical Insight: Why the Rotor Must Slip

If the rotor reached synchronous speed, there would be **no relative velocity** between RMF and rotor bars. No flux cutting ⇒ no induced EMF ⇒ no rotor current ⇒ no torque. The motor would decelerate back. Slip is therefore the **fundamental mechanism** by which torque is generated — *not* a parasitic loss.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are universally tested in KTU End-Semester Practical Examinations, CE viva-voce, and lab record evaluation. Memorise these before entering the lab.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | **rpm** | $f = 50$ Hz in India |
| 2 | Slip (fraction) | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | p.u. | Always $\geq 0$ for motoring |
| 3 | Slip (% form) | $s\,\% = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \times 100$ | **%** | Typical full-load $s \approx 3\%\text{–}5\%$ |
| 4 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | **Hz** | At full load of a 50 Hz motor, $f_r \approx 1.5$ to 2.5 Hz |
| 5 | Input power (2-wattmeter) | $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ | **W** | $W_1$ and $W_2$ are the two wattmeter readings |
| 6 | Power factor (2-wattmeter) | $\cos\phi = \cos\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right]$ | p.u. | Lagging if $W_1 > W_2$ for balanced load |
| 7 | Input power (3-wattmeter or $V$-$I$-$\cos\phi$) | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | **W** | Cross-check identity |
| 8 | Shaft torque (rope/band brake) | $T_{sh} = (W - S)\cdot g \cdot R$ | **N·m** | $W$ = gross load, $S$ = spring balance tare, $R$ = brake-drum radius, $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 9 | Shaft torque (DC dynamometer) | $T_{sh} = T_{dymo} \cdot \dfrac{N_{dymo}}{N_{motor}}\cdot \eta_{m\,coup}$ | **N·m** | Includes coupling/mechanical efficiency correction |
| 10 | Output power (mechanical) | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r\, T_{sh}}{60}$ | **W** | $= \omega_m T_{sh}$ with $\omega_m$ in rad/s |
| 11 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | **%** | Always $\leq 100\,\%$ |
| 12 | Percentage slip speed | $\Delta N = N_s - N_r$ | **rpm** | Used in plotting slip characteristics |
| 13 | Mechanical output from $T$-$\omega$ | $P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega_m$ where $\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N_r}{60}$ | **W** | Alternative path |
| 14 | Stator copper loss | $P_{cu1} = 3\,I_1^2\,R_1$ | **W** | From no-load test (stator copper loss at no-load is small) |
| 15 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | **W** | Where $P_{ag}$ = air-gap power |
| 16 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = \dfrac{P_{cu2}}{s} = \dfrac{T_{ag} \cdot \omega_s}{1}$ | **W** | Mechanical equivalent: $P_{ag} = T_{ag}\,\omega_s$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Examiner Note:** When using the **rope/band brake**, the net tangential force is $(W - S)$, NOT $(W + S)$. The two spring-balance readings $W$ (tight side) and $S$ (slack side) read on opposite sides of the drum; the *net* force creates the braking torque. Many students incorrectly add them, inflating torque by ~100%.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industry acceptance testing**: Manufacturers must guarantee efficiency per **IE2 / IE3 / IE4** classes (IS 12615, IEC 60034-30-1). Load test is the reference method.
- **Energy auditing**: In-plant motor surveys (e.g., at Hindalco, BHEL, NTPC) use load-test curves to detect *under-loaded* or *over-loaded* motors — every 10% voltage unbalance costs ~1–2% efficiency.
- **Renewable integration**: Wind turbine generator curves (PMSG / DFIG) are characterised using similar load-test principles on the generator side.
- **Electric vehicle drivetrains**: Traction induction motors (Tesla Model S/X legacy, Tata Tiago EV) are benchmarked by load-test maps of efficiency vs torque vs speed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Two-Wattmeter Method → Power Factor

When a balanced (or unbalanced) 3-phase, 3-wire load is supplied from lines R, Y, B, the **two-wattmeter method** places wattmeters $W_1$ (in line R) and $W_2$ (in line B), with their voltage coils connected to a common reference line Y.

Let phase sequence be R-Y-B and the per-phase impedance angle be $\phi$ (lagging). The line-to-neutral voltages are:

$$V_{RN} = V\angle 0°, \quad V_{YN} = V\angle\!-\!120°, \quad V_{BN} = V\angle\!+\!120°$$

The line currents (balanced) are:

$$I_R = I\angle\!-\!\phi, \quad I_Y = I\angle\!-\!120°\!-\!\phi, \quad I_B = I\angle\!+\!120°\!-\!\phi$$

The wattmeter $W_1$ reads the real power delivered into line R, with its voltage coil across $V_{RN} - V_{YN}$ (i.e., the line voltage $V_{RY}$):

$$W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$$

The wattmeter $W_2$ reads the real power into line B, voltage coil across $V_{BN} - V_{YN}$:

$$W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$$

> **Step 1** — Sum the two readings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 + W_2 &= V_L I_L\bigl[\cos(30°-\phi) + \cos(30°+\phi)\bigr] \\
&= V_L I_L \cdot 2\cos(30°)\cos(\phi) \\
&= \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi \\
&= P_{in}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the familiar 3-phase active power equation — **derived**, not assumed.

> **Step 2** — Subtract the two readings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 - W_2 &= V_L I_L\bigl[\cos(30°-\phi) - \cos(30°+\phi)\bigr] \\
&= V_L I_L \cdot 2\sin(30°)\sin(\phi) \\
&= V_L I_L \sin\phi \\
&= Q_{total}/\sqrt{3}\quad\text{(reactive power linkage)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3** — Form the ratio $\tan\phi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi &= \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2}\cdot\sqrt{3} \\
\therefore \cos\phi &= \cos\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **KTU board-standard** expression — memorise it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Output Power from Brake Torque

A band/rope brake wraps a rope (with coefficient of friction negligible for tight/slack friction assumed identical in KTU lab scenarios) over a drum of effective radius $R$ (metres) fixed to the motor shaft. The rope is held by two spring balances: the **tight side** reads $W$ kgf, the **slack side** reads $S$ kgf.

> **Step 1** — Net tangential braking force:

$$
F_{net} = (W - S) \cdot g \quad\text{[Newtons]}
$$

where $g = 9.81$ m/s² and the spring-balance readings are in kg-force.

> **Step 2** — Braking (shaft) torque:

$$
T_{sh} = F_{net} \cdot R = (W - S)\cdot g \cdot R \quad\text{[N·m]}
$$

> **Step 3** — Angular velocity in rad/s:

$$
\omega_m = \frac{2\pi N_r}{60} \quad\text{[rad/s]}
$$

> **Step 4** — Mechanical output power:

$$
P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega_m = \frac{2\pi N_r}{60}\,(W-S)\,g\,R \quad\text{[W]}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Slip–Torque Characteristic (Conceptual Map)

From the **approximate per-phase equivalent circuit** (magnetising branch shifted to the stator terminals for simplicity), the air-gap power transferred across the air gap is:

$$
P_{ag} = \frac{3\,V_{th}^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r'/s}{\left(\dfrac{R_r'}{s}\right)^2 + X_{total}^2}}
$$

The electromagnetic torque developed is:

$$
T_{em} = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s}\cdot\frac{V_{th}^2\,(R_r'/s)}{(R_r'/s)^2 + X_{total}^2}
$$

Differentiating $T_{em}$ with respect to $s$ and equating to zero yields the slip at maximum torque:

$$
\boxed{s_{maxT} = \frac{R_r'}{X_{total}}}
$$

and the corresponding **maximum torque**:

$$
\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3\,V_{th}^2}{2\,\omega_s\,X_{total}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> These derivations are **not** required to be reproduced in the KTU lab viva for this experiment, but examiners **frequently** ask students to "explain why slip increases with load" or "why torque-slip curve has the shape it does". Understanding the above is the key to scoring full marks in viva.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical** — Study this pattern; KTU viva questions often give raw readings and ask you to compute $\eta$, $\cos\phi$, and slip.

A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz squirrel cage induction motor running with a rope-brake loading gives the following readings at a particular load step:

- Line voltage $V_L = 415$ V
- Line current $I_L = 8.5$ A
- Wattmeter 1: $W_1 = 2860$ W
- Wattmeter 2: $W_2 = 760$ W
- Spring balance (tight): $W = 7.2$ kg
- Spring balance (slack): $S = 0.8$ kg
- Brake-drum radius $R = 0.08$ m
- Tachometer reading $N_r = 1430$ rpm

**Find:** Slip, power factor, shaft torque, output power, input power, and efficiency.

### Step 1: Synchronous Speed

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

### Step 2: Slip

$$
s = \frac{1500 - 1430}{1500} = \frac{70}{1500} = 0.0467 \text{ p.u.} = 4.67\,\%
$$

### Step 3: Input Power

$$
P_{in} = W_1 + W_2 = 2860 + 760 = 3620 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 4: Power Factor

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{\sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2} = \frac{1.732 \times (2860 - 760)}{3620} = \frac{1.732 \times 2100}{3620} = \frac{3637.2}{3620} \approx 1.0048
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(1.0048) \approx 45.13°
$$

$$
\cos\phi = \cos(45.13°) \approx 0.705 \text{ (lagging)}
$$

### Step 5: Net Brake Force

$$
F_{net} = (W - S) \cdot g = (7.2 - 0.8) \times 9.81 = 6.4 \times 9.81 = 62.784 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 6: Shaft Torque

$$
T_{sh} = F_{net} \cdot R = 62.784 \times 0.08 = 5.023 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 7: Output Power

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N_r\,T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1430 \times 5.023}{60} = \frac{45{,}127.5}{60} \approx 752.1 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 8: Efficiency

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{752.1}{3620} \times 100 \approx 20.78\,\%
$$

The efficiency is **low** because the motor is **lightly loaded** (only ~0.75 kW output vs ~3.62 kW input). At full load it would rise to 80–88% typical.

### Step 9: Verification by $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}V_LI_L\cos\phi$

$$
P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 8.5 \times 0.705 \approx 4311 \text{ W}
$$

The discrepancy (3620 vs 4311 W) is because at this **light load**, the wattmeters may have reading errors, or the load may have been slightly unbalanced. The **two-wattmeter value** is the **authoritative** one as it directly measures real power.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards in Effect
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB`) — never reserved words.
- No markdown bold/italic inside node labels — plain uppercase alphanumeric text only.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Complete Load Test Setup Architecture

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Experimental Procedure Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["START MOTOR DIRECT ON LINE OR STAR DELTA"]
    stepB["VERIFY NO LOAD RUN AND NULL CHECK INSTRUMENTS"]
    stepC["RECORD NO LOAD VALUES VL IL W1 W2 NR"]

    stepE["ALLOW SPEED TO STABILISE FOR 30 SECONDS"]

    stepK["SWITCH OFF MOTOR AFTER NO LOAD READING"]
    stepL["COMPUTE SLIP TORQUE OUTPUT PF EFFICIENCY"]
    stepM["PLOT OUTPUT VS SLIP PF EFFICIENCY SPEED"]
    stepN["TABULATE AND CONCLUDE"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL
```
