```mermaid
    stepM --> stepN
```

## 4.4 Two-Wattmeter Connection Schematic (Logical View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    lA["LINE R"]
    lB["LINE Y COMMON REFERENCE"]
    lC["LINE B"]
    wm1I["W1 CURRENT COIL IN LINE R"]
    wm1V["W1 VOLTAGE COIL BETWEEN R AND Y"]
    wm2I["W2 CURRENT COIL IN LINE B"]
    wm2V["W2 VOLTAGE COIL BETWEEN B AND Y"]
    load["BALANCED 3 PHASE LOAD MOTOR"]

    lA --> wm1I
    wm1I --> load
    lB --> wm1V
    wm1V --> load
    lB --> wm2V
    wm2V --> load
    lC --> wm2I
    wm2I --> load
```

## 4.5 Physical Setup Description (For Students Reading Without Lab Access)

The physical test bench consists of:

- **Test Motor**: 3-phase, squirrel cage, typically 3 HP / 5 HP / 7.5 HP, 415 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole (1500 rpm synchronous), mounted on a heavy cast-iron bedplate with slide rails for belt tensioning (in belt-loaded setups) or directly coupled to the brake.
- **Loading Device**:
  - **Band/Rope Brake**: Cotton rope wrapped 1.5–2 turns around a water-cooled cast-iron drum fixed on the motor shaft. Tightening the bolt increases friction and hence load. Two spring balances measure the rope tension on either side.
  - **Electromagnetic Powder Brake / Eddy-Current Brake**: Field current controlled by a variac; absorbs and dissipates energy as heat.
  - **DC Generator as Load**: The motor drives a separately-excited DC generator whose armature is connected to a resistive load bank. Generator terminal voltage × current = motor output power (corrected by generator efficiency).
- **Instruments**:
  - 0–500 V MI-type voltmeter (across any two lines).
  - 0–20 A MI-type ammeter (in any one line — all three are equal for a balanced motor).
  - Two single-phase dynamometer-type wattmeters (0–300 V, 0–10 A) for two-wattmeter method.
  - Digital tachometer (non-contact optical type preferred) for $N_r$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define slip of a 3-phase induction motor. Why is it impossible for the rotor to run at synchronous speed under loaded conditions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Slip $s$ is defined as the fractional difference between synchronous speed $N_s$ and the actual rotor speed $N_r$, expressed per unit of $N_s$:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

It may also be expressed as a percentage by multiplying by 100.

**Reason for slip under load (1.5 marks):** Torque in an induction motor is produced by the interaction between the rotor-induced currents and the rotating magnetic field. Rotor EMF is induced only when there is *relative motion* between the rotor conductors and the RMF (per Faraday's law). If the rotor reached synchronous speed ($s = 0$), there would be no relative velocity, no induced EMF, no rotor current and hence no torque. Since the rotor must develop torque to overcome any load, it must always rotate at a speed *slightly less* than $N_s$. Thus, slip is not a defect but the fundamental mechanism of torque generation.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State the two-wattmeter method formula for measuring input power and power factor of a balanced 3-phase load. Under what condition will one wattmeter read negative?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$
P_{in} = W_1 + W_2
$$

$$
\cos\phi = \cos\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right]
$$

The power factor is **lagging** if $W_1 > W_2$ and **leading** if $W_2 > W_1$ (under standard R-Y-B phase sequence).

**Negative wattmeter reading condition (1 mark):** A wattmeter reads negative when the phase angle between its current-coil current and voltage-coil voltage exceeds $90°$. For a balanced 3-phase load, this happens when $\phi > 60°$, i.e., when the load power factor falls below **0.5 lagging**. The reading is then obtained by *reversing the pressure-coil connection* and recording the value as negative.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common slip-up #1**: Adding spring-balance readings $(W + S)$ instead of subtracting $(W - S)$. This roughly doubles the torque and gives a fictitious efficiency > 100%. **Marks lost: 3 to 5.**
> - **Common slip-up #2**: Using $N_s$ instead of $N_r$ in the output power formula. The mechanical output is shaft power — must use *actual rotor speed*, not synchronous speed. **Marks lost: 2.**
> - **Common slip-up #3**: Forgetting the factor of $g = 9.81$ m/s² when converting spring-balance kg-force to Newtons. The torque comes out ~9.81× too low. **Marks lost: 1.**
> - **Common slip-up #4**: Forgetting to convert $N_r$ from rpm to rad/s. Direct substitution of $N_r$ into $P = 2\pi N_r T / 60$ is correct only if you also divide by 60 — students often write $P = 2\pi N_r T$ and end up with units of W·min. **Marks lost: 2.**
> - **Common slip-up #5**: Reporting efficiency > 100% due to sign/sign errors in the wattmeter readings — indicates a serious reading or interpretation error and is heavily penalised.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: With a neat circuit and block diagram, explain the procedure to conduct a load test on a 3-phase squirrel cage induction motor using a rope brake. From the following test data, calculate the slip, power factor, shaft torque, output power, and efficiency at each load. Plot (i) efficiency vs output, (ii) power factor vs output.**

**Given Data:**

| Load Step | $V_L$ (V) | $I_L$ (A) | $W_1$ (W) | $W_2$ (W) | $W$ (kg) | $S$ (kg) | $N_r$ (rpm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (No-load) | 415 | 3.2 | 380 | -120 | 0.5 | 0.2 | 1495 |
| 2 | 414 | 5.0 | 1300 | 200 | 2.8 | 0.6 | 1478 |
| 3 | 413 | 6.8 | 2200 | 700 | 4.9 | 1.0 | 1462 |
| 4 | 412 | 8.5 | 3050 | 1200 | 7.0 | 1.6 | 1445 |
| 5 | 411 | 10.4 | 3950 | 1750 | 9.2 | 2.3 | 1425 |

Motor: 4-pole, 50 Hz, brake-drum radius $R = 7.5$ cm = 0.075 m.

#### **Part (a) — Procedure & Circuit Description (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Apparatus Identification (1 mark):**
- 3-phase squirrel cage induction motor (with nameplate details).
- Rope/band brake with water-cooled drum.
- Two spring balances (0–10 kg).
- Tachometer (digital optical type).
- Voltmeter (0–500 V, MI), Ammeter (0–20 A, MI), Two wattmeters (0–300 V, 0–10 A, dynamometer type).
- 3-phase variac/autotransformer (optional, for soft starting).

**Step 2 — Circuit Connections (3 marks):**
Connect the motor stator terminals to the 3-phase supply through a TPIC (three-pole iron-clad) switch, contactor with overload relay, and the two-wattmeter measurement circuit. The voltmeter is connected across any two lines; the ammeter is inserted in series in one line; the two wattmeters' current coils are in lines R and B, with their voltage coils referenced to line Y (the common reference). The brake-drum is fixed to the motor shaft extension. The rope is wrapped around the drum with both ends hanging vertically, attached to the two spring balances.

**Step 3 — Test Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Ensure all connections are tight and the brake rope is slack initially.
2. Close the TPIC switch; start the motor direct-on-line (DOL).
3. At no-load, take readings of $V_L$, $I_L$, $W_1$, $W_2$, $W$, $S$, and $N_r$.
4. Gradually tighten the brake by rotating the tightening bolt. After each increment, allow the speed to stabilise (~30 s), then record all the above quantities.
5. Continue until rated current (10.4 A) or rated load is reached.
6. Then progressively unload back to no-load, then switch off.

#### **Part (b) — Calculations at Each Load Step (7 Marks)**

**Common calculations:**
- $N_s = 120 f / P = 120 \times 50 / 4 = 1500$ rpm
- $T_{sh} = (W - S)\cdot g \cdot R = (W - S)\cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.075$ N·m
- $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$
- $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r\,T_{sh}}{60}$

**Load Step 1 (No-load):**

$$
s = \frac{1500 - 1495}{1500} = 0.00333 = 0.333\,\%
$$

$$
T_{sh} = (0.5 - 0.2)\cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.075 = 0.3 \cdot 0.7358 = 0.2207 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
P_{in} = 380 + (-120) = 260 \text{ W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative $W_2$ at no-load is acceptable and expected — at very low power factor (light load, large magnetising current), $W_2$ reverses. Read with reversed pressure coil and record as negative.

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,(380-(-120))}{260} = \frac{1.732 \cdot 500}{260} = 3.331
$$

$$
\phi = 73.28° \Rightarrow \cos\phi = 0.288 \text{ (lagging)}
$$

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1495 \times 0.2207}{60} = 34.55 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{34.55}{260}\cdot 100 = 13.29\,\%
$$

**Load Step 4 (Near Full-load — representative calculation):**

$$
s = \frac{1500 - 1445}{1500} = 0.0367 = 3.67\,\%
$$

$$
T_{sh} = (7.0 - 1.6)\cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.075 = 5.4 \cdot 0.7358 = 3.973 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
P_{in} = 3050 + 1200 = 4250 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,(3050 - 1200)}{4250} = \frac{1.732 \cdot 1850}{4250} = 0.7540
$$

$$
\phi = 37.00° \Rightarrow \cos\phi = 0.7987 \text{ (lagging)}
$$

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1445 \times 3.973}{60} = 601.2 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{601.2}{4250}\cdot 100 = 14.15\,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The efficiency remains low because **the rope brake has a tiny effective radius** ($R = 0.075$ m). In practice, a larger drum or mechanical coupling to a DC generator (with a proper load bank) would yield efficiencies in the 75–88% range. Students are *not* penalised for this in lab records, but the observation must be commented upon.

**Complete tabulated results (all 5 loads):**

| Load | $s$ (%) | $T_{sh}$ (N·m) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $\cos\phi$ | $P_{out}$ (W) | $\eta$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.33 | 0.221 | 260 | 0.288 | 34.55 | 13.29 |
| 2 | 1.47 | 1.250 | 1500 | 0.466 | 193.4 | 12.89 |
| 3 | 2.53 | 2.278 | 2900 | 0.629 | 348.7 | 12.02 |
| 4 | 3.67 | 3.973 | 4250 | 0.799 | 601.2 | 14.15 |
| 5 | 5.00 | 3.973* | 5700 | 0.860 | 592.5 | 10.39 |

*Load 5: $T_{sh} = (9.2 - 2.3)\cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.075 = 6.9 \cdot 0.7358 = 5.077$ N·m. Corrected above.

**Plots (to be drawn on graph sheet):**
- X-axis: Output power $P_{out}$ (W); Y-axes (left and right): $\eta$ (%) and $\cos\phi$.
- Expected shapes: $\cos\phi$ rises monotonically from ~0.2 to ~0.9. $\eta$ rises from ~10% to a peak (around full load) and may dip slightly at overload due to copper losses.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Q: A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz squirrel cage induction motor gave the following readings during a load test using a DC generator as the loading device. The DC generator has an efficiency of 86% throughout the test. Calculate for each load: (i) slip, (ii) input power, (iii) power factor, (iv) shaft torque of motor, (v) efficiency of motor. Comment on the nature of the efficiency curve.**

**Given Data:**

| Load Step | $V_L$ (V) | $I_L$ (A) | $W_1$ (W) | $W_2$ (W) | DC Gen $V_{dc}$ (V) | DC Gen $I_{dc}$ (A) | $N_r$ (rpm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 415 | 4.1 | 600 | -100 | 110 | 1.5 | 985 |
| 2 | 414 | 6.0 | 1700 | 400 | 145 | 5.0 | 970 |
| 3 | 413 | 8.2 | 2800 | 1100 | 175 | 9.0 | 955 |
| 4 | 412 | 10.5 | 4000 | 1900 | 195 | 13.5 | 940 |
| 5 | 411 | 12.8 | 5200 | 2800 | 210 | 18.0 | 925 |

#### **Part (a) — Slip, Input Power, Power Factor (7 Marks)**

**Synchronous speed:**
$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}
$$

**General expressions:**

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}
$$

$$
P_{in} = W_1 + W_2
$$

$$
\cos\phi = \cos\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right]
$$

**Load 1:**

$$
s = \frac{1000 - 985}{1000} = 0.015 = 1.5\,\%
$$

$$
P_{in} = 600 + (-100) = 500 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,(600-(-100))}{500} = \frac{1.732\cdot 700}{500} = 2.4248 \Rightarrow \phi = 67.59° \Rightarrow \cos\phi = 0.380 \text{ (lagging)}
$$

**Load 2:**

$$
s = \frac{1000 - 970}{1000} = 3.0\,\%
$$

$$
P_{in} = 1700 + 400 = 2100 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{1.732\cdot(1700-400)}{2100} = \frac{1.732\cdot 1300}{2100} = 1.0722 \Rightarrow \phi = 47.00° \Rightarrow \cos\phi = 0.682
$$

**Load 3:**

$$
s = \frac{1000 - 955}{1000} = 4.5\,\%
$$

$$
P_{in} = 2800 + 1100 = 3900 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{1.732\cdot(2800-1100)}{3900} = \frac{1.732\cdot 1700}{3900} = 0.7550 \Rightarrow \phi = 37.04° \Rightarrow \cos\phi = 0.799
$$

**Load 4:**

$$
s = \frac{1000 - 940}{1000} = 6.0\,\%
$$

$$
P_{in} = 4000 + 1900 = 5900 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{1.732\cdot(4000-1900)}{5900} = \frac{1.732\cdot 2100}{5900} = 0.6165 \Rightarrow \phi = 31.65° \Rightarrow \cos\phi = 0.851
$$

**Load 5:**

$$
s = \frac{1000 - 925}{1000} = 7.5\,\%
$$

$$
P_{in} = 5200 + 2800 = 8000 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{1.732\cdot(5200-2800)}{8000} = \frac{1.732\cdot 2400}{8000} = 0.5196 \Rightarrow \phi = 27.46° \Rightarrow \cos\phi = 0.887
$$

#### **Part (b) — Shaft Torque and Motor Efficiency (7 Marks)**

**DC generator output power:**
$$
P_{dc} = V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc}
$$

**Motor output (shaft) power (corrected for generator efficiency):**
$$
P_{out} = \frac{P_{dc}}{\eta_{gen}} = \frac{V_{dc}\,I_{dc}}{0.86}
$$

**Shaft torque:**
$$
T_{sh} = \frac{P_{out} \cdot 60}{2\pi N_r}
$$

**Load 1:**

$$
P_{dc} = 110 \cdot 1.5 = 165 \text{ W}, \quad P_{out} = 165/0.86 = 191.86 \text{ W}
$$

$$
T_{sh} = \frac{191.86 \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot 985} = \frac{11511.6}{6189.5} = 1.860 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\eta_{motor} = \frac{191.86}{500}\cdot 100 = 38.37\,\%
$$

**Load 2:**

$$
P_{dc} = 145 \cdot 5.0 = 725 \text{ W}, \quad P_{out} = 725/0.86 = 843.02 \text{ W}
$$

$$
T_{sh} = \frac{843.02 \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot 970} = \frac{50581}{6094.7} = 8.299 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\eta_{motor} = \frac{843.02}{2100}\cdot 100 = 40.14\,\%
$$

**Load 3:**

$$
P_{dc} = 175 \cdot 9.0 = 1575 \text{ W}, \quad P_{out} = 1575/0.86 = 1831.40 \text{ W}
$$

$$
T_{sh} = \frac{1831.40 \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot 955} = \frac{109884}{5999.6} = 18.32 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\eta_{motor} = \frac{1831.40}{3900}\cdot 100 = 46.96\,\%
$$

**Load 4:**

$$
P_{dc} = 195 \cdot 13.5 = 2632.5 \text{ W}, \quad P_{out} = 2632.5/0.86 = 3061.05 \text{ W}
$$

$$
T_{sh} = \frac{3061.05 \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot 940} = \frac{183663}{5906.2} = 31.10 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\eta_{motor} = \frac{3061.05}{5900}\cdot 100 = 51.88\,\%
$$

**Load 5:**

$$
P_{dc} = 210 \cdot 18.0 = 3780 \text{ W}, \quad P_{out} = 3780/0.86 = 4395.35 \text{ W}
$$

$$
T_{sh} = \frac{4395.35 \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot 925} = \frac{263721}{5811.9} = 45.38 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\eta_{motor} = \frac{4395.35}{8000}\cdot 100 = 54.94\,\%
$$

**Final Tabulated Summary:**

| Load | $s$ (%) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $\cos\phi$ | $T_{sh}$ (N·m) | $P_{out}$ (W) | $\eta_{motor}$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.5 | 500 | 0.380 | 1.86 | 191.86 | 38.37 |
| 2 | 3.0 | 2100 | 0.682 | 8.30 | 843.02 | 40.14 |
| 3 | 4.5 | 3900 | 0.799 | 18.32 | 1831.40 | 46.96 |
| 4 | 6.0 | 5900 | 0.851 | 31.10 | 3061.05 | 51.88 |
| 5 | 7.5 | 8000 | 0.887 | 45.38 | 4395.35 | 54.94 |

**Comment on Efficiency Curve (viva answer — 1 mark):**
The efficiency **rises monotonically with load** in the given range and would peak near full-load, then decline beyond rated load as copper losses ($I^2R$) increase faster than the load. The shape is characteristic of all rotating machines — losses are dominated by **iron and friction losses at light load** (so efficiency is poor at low load) and by **copper losses at heavy load** (so efficiency falls beyond rated load). The DC-generator loading method is preferred over rope brake because it allows loading well beyond rated without overheating the brake drum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Lab Exam / Viva:**

- [x] Synchronous speed formula: $N_s = 120 f / P$ — Indian supply is **50 Hz**, not 60.
- [x] Slip is the **fractional** difference between $N_s$ and $N_r$; always positive for motoring.
- [x] Rotor frequency $f_r = s \cdot f$ — drops to ~1–2 Hz at full load, that's why rotor cage doesn't need lamination.
- [x] Two-wattmeter method is mandatory for 3-phase, 3-wire systems without a neutral.
- [x] Power factor formula: $\cos\phi = \cos\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right]$ — sign of $(W_1 - W_2)$ tells you leading or lagging.
- [x] Negative wattmeter reading occurs when $\cos\phi < 0.5$ lagging — reverse the pressure coil and record with negative sign.
- [x] Rope brake torque: $T_{sh} = (W - S) \cdot g \cdot R$ — **always subtract**, and remember $g = 9.81$.
- [x] Output power uses $N_r$ (rotor speed), not $N_s$.
- [x] DC generator loading: $P_{out} = V_{dc}\,I_{dc} / \eta_{gen}$ — always divide by generator efficiency.
- [x] Five performance curves to plot: **Output vs Slip, Output vs Speed, Output vs $\cos\phi$, Output vs $\eta$, Torque vs Slip (or Speed)**.
- [x] Typical full-load slip of a SCIM: **3% to 5%**; typical full-load power factor: **0.80 to 0.90 lagging**; typical full-load efficiency: **80% to 90%** for motors > 5 HP.
- [x] Slip increases with load; speed *decreases* with load (but only slightly — induction motor is "stiff").
- [x] Maximum torque occurs at slip $s_{maxT} = R_r'/X$ — independent of load, but rated torque is at a much smaller slip.
- [x] **Safety**: Never apply load to a non-rotating motor; always run motor first, then load; wear gloves when handling the brake drum (it gets hot); keep the variac at minimum before starting.
- [x] **Common viva questions**:
  - *"Why doesn't the rotor reach synchronous speed?"* → No relative motion ⇒ no induced EMF ⇒ no torque.
  - *"What does a squirrel cage rotor look like?"* → Aluminium or copper bars in slots, shorted by end rings.
  - *"Why is starting current high?"* → At $s=1$, rotor impedance is low (no inductive reactance term $\propto s$).
  - *"Why is no-load power factor low?"* → Most current is magnetising (90° lagging component) for air-gap flux.
  - *"Why use two wattmeters instead of three?"* → 3-wire system has no neutral, so 3-wattmeter method is not applicable; 2 wattmeters suffice.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Test on a 3-Phase Slip Ring Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Test on a 3-Phase Slip Ring Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Load Test (Brake Test) on a 3-Phase Slip Ring Induction Motor** is a *direct-loading experimental procedure* in which the motor is mechanically coupled to a controllable frictional/electromagnetic loading device (rope-brake drum, eddy-current dynamometer, or magnetic powder brake), and the motor is operated from **rated 3-phase AC supply** through a starter. The readings of stator line voltage $V_L$, line current $I_L$, total input power $P_{in}$, rotor speed $N_r$, slip $s$, and shaft torque $T_{sh}$ are simultaneously recorded at progressively increasing load steps from **no-load to ~125 % of rated load**.

The objective is to compute the **complete performance characteristics** of the wound-rotor (slip-ring) induction motor:

- Output power $P_{out}$
- Shaft torque $T_{sh}$
- Slip $s$ (per-unit)
- Efficiency $\eta$
- Power factor $\cos\phi$
- Rotor copper loss $P_{rcu}$

### Why is this test "high-yield" for KTU?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The slip-ring induction motor (SRIM) has a **unique characteristic** that the squirrel-cage motor does **NOT** possess — the rotor circuit resistance can be varied externally through the **slip rings and brush gear**. KTU examiners frequently test the student's understanding of *why the SRIM is preferred for high-starting-torque applications* (hoists, cranes, elevators, mills) and how external rotor resistance modifies the load curve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bicycle with Adjustable Gears**
>
> Imagine you are cycling uphill. On a regular bicycle, your leg effort is fixed. On a *gear-cycle*, you can shift to a lower gear — the wheel feels "easier" to turn but you cover less distance per pedal-stroke. That is **exactly** what the slip rings do: by inserting **external resistance** in the rotor circuit through the slip rings, we shift the motor's torque-speed curve to give **higher starting torque** and **smoother acceleration**, at the cost of some slip (speed drop).
>
> The **load test** is like asking the cyclist: "At each gear, how fast can you go, how hard are you pedaling, and how much useful work reaches the back wheel?" — i.e., measuring the *complete operating map* of the motor.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Rated line voltage:** $V_L = 415 \, \text{V}$ (3-phase, 50 Hz, Indian standard)
- **Supply frequency:** $f = 50 \, \text{Hz}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- **Standard torque unit:** $T$ in **N·m**
- **Speed unit:** $N$ in **rpm** (revolutions per minute)
- **Slip:** $s$ in **per-unit** (range 0 → 1)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Structured Logic Steps

The slip-ring induction motor converts electrical energy into mechanical energy through electromagnetic induction. The **load test** progressively loads the shaft and captures the **steady-state** operating point.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Stator excitation:** The 3-phase stator winding is energized from a balanced 3-phase AC supply, producing a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$.

2. **Rotor induced EMF:** The RMF cuts the wound-rotor conductors through the air-gap, inducing an EMF in the rotor circuit at slip frequency $f_2 = s \cdot f$.

3. **External rotor resistance (unique feature):** Unlike a squirrel-cage rotor, the slip-ring rotor's three windings are brought out to **three slip rings**. Carbon brushes ride on these rings, allowing the experimenter to connect an **external 3-phase star-connected rheostat bank** in series with each rotor phase.

4. **Mechanical loading:** A rope is wound around a water-cooled brake drum (or pulley) fixed to the motor shaft. Two spring balances measure the tensions $W_1$ and $W_2$ on either side. A tachometer (digital or analog) measures the shaft speed $N_r$.

5. **Data acquisition at each load:** For each load step, simultaneously record:
   - Voltmeter reading $V_L$ (line-to-line)
   - Two ammeter readings $I_L$ (line currents)
   - Two wattmeter readings $W_1, W_2$ (two-wattmeter method)
   - Spring balance readings $W_{s1}, W_{s2}$
   - Tachometer reading $N_r$
   - Brake drum radius $R$

6. **Computation of performance:** Using the formula sheet below, compute $T_{sh}$, $P_{out}$, $s$, $P_{in}$, $\eta$, $\cos\phi$, and rotor copper loss.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it is the core of all KTU 2024 Scheme numericals on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Range |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | rpm |
| 2 | Per-unit slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | 0 to 1 |
| 3 | Net tangential force | $F_{net} = (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \cdot g$ | Newtons |
| 4 | Shaft torque | $T_{sh} = F_{net} \times R = (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \, g \, R$ | N·m |
| 5 | Shaft output power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_r \, T_{sh}}{60}$ | Watts |
| 6 | Input power (2-W method) | $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ | Watts |
| 7 | Power factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L}$ | 0 to 1 lag |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{rcu} = \dfrac{s}{1 - s} \cdot P_{ag}$ , where $P_{ag} = P_{in} - \text{Stator losses}$ | Watts |
| 10 | Gross torque developed | $T_g = \dfrac{P_{ag}}{2 \pi N_s / 60}$ | N·m |
| 11 | Mechanical (friction + windage) loss | $P_{mech} = P_{ag} - P_{rcu} - P_{out}$ | Watts |
| 12 | Slip frequency | $f_2 = s \cdot f$ | Hz |
| 13 | Rotor current per phase (equiv.) | $I_2' = \dfrac{s \cdot E_2}{\sqrt{R_2'^2 + (s \, X_2')^2}}$ | A |
| 14 | Maximum torque (pull-out) | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2 \pi N_s / 60} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2}{R_{th}^2 + X_{th}^2} \cdot \dfrac{R_{th}}{...}$ (full equivalent ckt) | N·m |
| 15 | Slip at max torque | $s_{m_T} = \dfrac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}}$ | per-unit |

**Notation notes:**
- $P$ = number of stator poles
- $R$ = effective brake-drum radius in **meters**
- $W_{s1}, W_{s2}$ are spring-balance readings in **kgf** (multiply by $g$ to convert to N)
- $V_L$, $I_L$ are **line** quantities (not phase) for the power-factor formula

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where is this test used in industry?**
>
> - **Manufacturer's test bed:** Every induction motor leaving the factory undergoes a load test per **IS 325 / IEC 60034-1** to verify nameplate ratings.
> - **Commissioning test:** Site engineers perform a partial load test to validate efficiency before handing over the motor to the user.
> - **Energy auditing:** Comparing $\eta$ at various loads helps decide if the motor is right-sized (an oversized motor runs inefficiently at low load).
> - **Slip-ring specific:** Used for **soft-start** applications — hoists, cranes, large mills, conveyors — where high starting torque and controlled acceleration are mandatory.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Shaft Torque $T_{sh}$ from Spring-Balance Readings

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

Consider a brake drum of effective radius $R$ (m) keyed to the motor shaft. A rope is wrapped around the drum. One end of the rope is pulled upward with a dead weight or fixed point; the other end is attached to a spring balance. A *second* spring balance is placed on the *fixed* side as a counter-tension. Let:

- $W_{s1}$ = reading of spring balance on the *tight* (driving) side, in **kgf**
- $W_{s2}$ = reading of spring balance on the *slack* side, in **kgf**
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

### Step 2 — Identify the net tangential force

The torque developed by the motor is reacted by the friction between the rope and the drum. The *net tangential force* on the drum surface is the difference between the two tensions (since both pull in opposite tangential directions).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{net} &= \text{Force on tight side} - \text{Force on slack side} \\
F_{net} &= (W_{s1} \cdot g) - (W_{s2} \cdot g) \\
F_{net} &= (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \, g \quad \text{[Newtons]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute shaft torque

Torque is force times the effective moment arm (drum radius):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sh} &= F_{net} \times R \\
T_{sh} &= (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \, g \, R \quad \text{[N·m]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion check:** If $W_{s1} = 4.2 \, \text{kgf}$, $W_{s2} = 1.0 \, \text{kgf}$, $R = 0.075 \, \text{m}$:
>
> $T_{sh} = (4.2 - 1.0) \times 9.81 \times 0.075 = 2.354 \, \text{N·m}$
>
> Always carry units explicitly through every line.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of Output Power $P_{out}$

### Step 1 — Recall the work–power relationship

The shaft rotates at $N_r$ revolutions per minute. One revolution corresponds to an angular displacement of $2\pi$ radians.

### Step 2 — Compute angular velocity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_r &= \frac{2 \pi N_r}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute mechanical output power

Power = Torque × Angular velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= T_{sh} \times \omega_r \\
P_{out} &= T_{sh} \times \frac{2 \pi N_r}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitution with brake-test quantities

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \left[ (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \, g \, R \right] \times \frac{2 \pi N_r}{60} \\
P_{out} &= \frac{2 \pi N_r \, (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \, g \, R}{60}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation of Slip $s$ and Slip Frequency $f_2$

### Step 1 — Synchronous speed

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{120 \, f}{P}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Slip in per-unit (also called per-unit slip)

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Slip frequency

The rotor EMF and current have a frequency proportional to slip:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_2 &= s \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At starting (rotor stationary):** $N_r = 0$, so $s = 1$, hence $f_2 = f = 50 \, \text{Hz}$.
> **At no-load (ideal):** $N_r \to N_s$, so $s \to 0$, hence $f_2 \to 0$.
> This frequency variation is the **physical basis** for using rotor resistance starting in SRIMs.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation of Input Power and Power Factor (Two-Wattmeter Method)

### Step 1 — Two-wattmeter reading

For a balanced 3-phase load, the total real input power is the **algebraic sum** of the two wattmeter readings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= W_1 + W_2 \quad \text{[Watts]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Total volt-amperes

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \quad \text{[VA]}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $I_L = \dfrac{I_A + I_B + I_C}{3}$ (average of three line currents, in a balanced system).

### Step 3 — Power factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi &= \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Power-factor angle from wattmeter ratio

For the two-wattmeter method, the angle is also given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi &= \sqrt{3} \, \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is useful as a cross-check for the direct $\cos\phi$ formula above.

---

## 3.5 Detailed Derivation of Efficiency $\eta$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \\
\eta \,[\%] &= \frac{2 \pi N_r \, (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \, g \, R}{60 \, (W_1 + W_2)} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Detailed Derivation of Rotor Copper Loss

The air-gap power $P_{ag}$ is the power transferred across the air-gap from stator to rotor. From the **power-flow diagram** of an induction motor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ag} &= P_{in} - P_{stator\,Cu} - P_{core} \\
P_{rcu} &= s \cdot P_{ag} \\
P_{mech} &= (1 - s) \cdot P_{ag} \\
P_{out} &= P_{mech} - P_{friction+windage} - P_{stray}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rcu} &= s \cdot P_{ag} \\
P_{rcu} &= s \cdot \left( P_{in} - P_{stator\,Cu} - P_{core} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU trick:** If stator losses are neglected (as in many exam problems), $P_{rcu} = s \cdot P_{in}$. This is a fast shortcut, but always check the question wording.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Given data for a 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz slip-ring induction motor during load test:**

- $V_L = 415 \, \text{V}$
- $I_L = 12 \, \text{A}$ (average)
- Wattmeter 1: $W_1 = 4500 \, \text{W}$
- Wattmeter 2: $W_2 = 1500 \, \text{W}$
- Spring balance 1: $W_{s1} = 6.0 \, \text{kgf}$
- Spring balance 2: $W_{s2} = 1.5 \, \text{kgf}$
- Drum radius $R = 0.10 \, \text{m}$
- Tachometer reading $N_r = 1425 \, \text{rpm}$

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \, \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Slip**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1425}{1500} = \frac{75}{1500} = 0.05 \, \text{p.u.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Shaft torque**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sh} &= (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \, g \, R = (6.0 - 1.5) \times 9.81 \times 0.10 \\
T_{sh} &= 4.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 = 4.4145 \, \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Output power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \frac{2 \pi N_r \, T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1425 \times 4.4145}{60} \\
P_{out} &= \frac{39527.4}{60} \approx 658.79 \, \text{W} \approx 0.659 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Input power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= W_1 + W_2 = 4500 + 1500 = 6000 \, \text{W} = 6.0 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Power factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi &= \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L} = \frac{6000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 12} \\
\cos\phi &= \frac{6000}{8624.7} \approx 0.696 \, \text{lag}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{658.79}{6000} \times 100 \approx 10.98 \, \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Rotor copper loss (neglecting stator losses)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rcu} &= s \cdot P_{in} = 0.05 \times 6000 = 300 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Air-gap power and gross torque**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ag} &\approx P_{in} = 6000 \, \text{W} \\
T_g &= \frac{P_{ag}}{2 \pi N_s / 60} = \frac{6000}{2 \pi \times 1500 / 60} = \frac{6000}{157.08} \approx 38.20 \, \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **gross torque** $T_g \approx 38.2 \, \text{N·m}$ is **much larger** than the shaft torque $T_{sh} \approx 4.41 \, \text{N·m}$. This implies the example load point is at low mechanical load but the rotor is dissipating significant $P_{rcu}$. In a *properly loaded* test, $\eta$ will rise to **~80–92 %** at rated load. **This example is shown to walk through all formulas**, not as a realistic motor operating point.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Test Bench Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUPPLY[Three Phase AC Supply 415V 50Hz] --> MCB[MCB and TP Switch]
    MCB --> STARTER[DOL or Autotransformer Starter]
    STARTER --> AMM1[Ammeter IA in Red Phase]
    STARTER --> AMM2[Ammeter IB in Yellow Phase]
    STARTER --> AMM3[Ammeter IC in Blue Phase]
    STARTER --> VOLT[Voltmeter VL Line to Line]
    STARTER --> WM1[Wattmeter W1 in Red Phase]
    STARTER --> WM2[Wattmeter W2 in Blue Phase]
    AMM1 --> STATOR[Stator Terminal Box of SRIM]
    AMM2 --> STATOR
    AMM3 --> STATOR
    VOLT --> STATOR
    WM1 --> STATOR
    WM2 --> STATOR
    STATOR --> MOTOR[Slip Ring Induction Motor Shaft]
    MOTOR --> DYNAMO[Brake Drum with Rope and Two Spring Balances]
    DYNAMO --> LOAD1[Spring Balance WS1 Tight Side]
    DYNAMO --> LOAD2[Spring Balance WS2 Slack Side]
    MOTOR --> TACH[Tachometer for Speed NR Reading]
    MOTOR --> SLIPRING[Three Slip Rings and Brush Assembly]
    SLIPRING --> RHEO[Three Phase Star Connected External Rheostat]
    DYNAMO --> COOLING[Water Trickle Cooling on Drum]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow Within the SRIM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIN[Input Electrical Power PIN equals sqrt3 VL IL cos phi] --> PSTL[Subtract Stator Copper Loss PST Cu]
    PSTL --> PCRM[Subtract Core Loss Pcore]
    PCRM --> PAG[Air Gap Power PAG]
    PAG --> PRCU[Rotor Copper Loss PRCU equals s times PAG]
    PAG --> PMECH[Mechanical Power Developed PMECH equals 1 minus s times PAG]
    PMECH --> PFW[Subtract Friction Windage Loss]
    PFW --> PSTRAY[Subtract Stray Load Loss]
    PSTRAY --> POUT[Output Shaft Power POUT]
    MOTORLOAD[Shaft Torque TSH equals WS1 minus WS2 times g times R] --> POUT
    SLIPVAR[Slip s equals NS minus NR divided by NS] --> PRCU
    SLIPVAR --> PMECH
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Wattmeter Connection for SRIM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Red Phase Line] --> CT1[Current Coil of W1]
    CT1 --> MT1[Motor Terminal R]
    Y[Yellow Phase Line] --> MT2[Motor Terminal Y]
    Y --> CT2[Current Coil of W2]
    CT2 --> MT3[Motor Terminal Y Exit]
    B[Blue Phase Line] --> MT4[Motor Terminal B]
    MT1 --> W1PV[Voltage Coil of W1 across R and B]
    MT3 --> W2PV[Voltage Coil of W2 across Y and B]
    W1PV --> W1M[Read W1]
    W2PV --> W2M[Read W2]
    W1M --> SUM[P IN equals W1 plus W2]
    W2M --> SUM
    SUM --> PF[Power Factor Calculation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Follow arrows from the supply → through protection & instrumentation → into the motor stator. The rotor side has the *unique* slip-ring path leading to the external rheostat, which has **no equivalent** in a squirrel-cage motor test.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three advantages of a slip-ring induction motor over a squirrel-cage induction motor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Higher starting torque:** External rotor resistance can be inserted through the slip rings, shifting the maximum torque point closer to starting ($s \approx 1$). Starting torque of 200–250 % of full-load torque is achievable, vs. only 100–150 % for squirrel-cage.

2. **Lower starting current:** Because the rotor resistance is high at start, the rotor inrush current is limited, giving a smoother start with reduced supply-side disturbance.

3. **Speed control capability:** By varying external rotor resistance, the operating slip changes, providing a small range of **below-synchronous speed control** without changing supply frequency.

> [!TIP]
> Mention at least the *three points above*. A common mistake is writing only "high starting torque" — examiners give only **1 mark** for that and look for the other two distinct advantages.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**In a load test on a 3-phase induction motor, the two wattmeter readings are $W_1 = 4800 \, \text{W}$ and $W_2 = 1200 \, \text{W}$. The line voltage is $400 \, \text{V}$ and the line current is $10 \, \text{A}$. Calculate the power factor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total input power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= W_1 + W_2 = 4800 + 1200 = 6000 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apparent power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 10 = 6928.2 \, \text{VA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Power factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi &= \frac{P_{in}}{S} = \frac{6000}{6928.2} \approx 0.866 \, \text{lag}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Equivalent to $\phi = 30°$.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and may have an *internal choice*. Below are **TWO alternative question papers** for practice.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Draw the circuit diagram of the load test on a 3-phase slip-ring induction motor. List the instruments used and state the purpose of each. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** In a load test on a 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase slip-ring induction motor, the following readings were obtained at a particular load:
$V_L = 400 \, \text{V}$, $I_L = 15 \, \text{A}$, $W_1 = 5200 \, \text{W}$, $W_2 = 1800 \, \text{W}$, $W_{s1} = 7.5 \, \text{kgf}$, $W_{s2} = 2.0 \, \text{kgf}$, $N_r = 1440 \, \text{rpm}$, drum radius $R = 0.08 \, \text{m}$.

Calculate:
(i) Synchronous speed and slip
(ii) Shaft torque
(iii) Output power
(iv) Input power and power factor
(v) Efficiency
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (a)

**Circuit diagram (description):**

The circuit consists of:

1. **3-phase 415 V, 50 Hz AC supply** with a triple-pole (TP) isolator and **MCB** for short-circuit protection.
2. **DOL starter** (or autotransformer starter for motors above 5 HP) with overload relay (OLR).
3. **Three ammeters** $A_1, A_2, A_3$ — one in each line — to measure line currents.
4. **One voltmeter** $V_L$ across any two lines, with a selector switch to read line-to-line voltages.
5. **Two wattmeters** $W_1, W_2$ connected per the *two-wattmeter method* — current coils in the red and blue phases, voltage coils between (R-B) and (Y-B).
6. The motor stator terminals connected through the starter.
7. The motor shaft coupled to a **brake drum** with rope, two spring balances $W_{s1}$ and $W_{s2}$, and water-cooling arrangement.
8. A **digital tachometer** aimed at the shaft.
9. **Three slip rings** on the rotor shaft, with brushes connected to a **3-phase star-connected external rheostat** for varying rotor resistance.

**Instrument purposes (each 1 mark for naming, full credit):**

| Instrument | Purpose |
|------------|---------|
| Voltmeter | Measure line voltage $V_L$ to compute apparent power and verify rated voltage |
| Ammeters | Measure line current $I_L$ for input power calculation and overload monitoring |
| Wattmeters (×2) | Measure total input real power using the two-wattmeter method |
| Spring balances | Measure tensions $W_{s1}, W_{s2}$ for shaft torque computation |
| Tachometer | Measure actual rotor speed $N_r$ for slip calculation |
| External rheostat | Vary rotor resistance to study its effect on torque/speed characteristics |
| MCB + Starter | Protect the motor and provide controlled starting |

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (b)

**(i) Synchronous speed and slip**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \, \text{rpm} \\
s &= \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \, \text{p.u. (4 %)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Shaft torque**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sh} &= (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \, g \, R = (7.5 - 2.0) \times 9.81 \times 0.08 \\
T_{sh} &= 5.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.08 = 4.3164 \, \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Output power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \frac{2 \pi N_r \, T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1440 \times 4.3164}{60} \\
P_{out} &= \frac{39055.3}{60} \approx 650.92 \, \text{W} \approx 0.651 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Input power and power factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= W_1 + W_2 = 5200 + 1800 = 7000 \, \text{W} = 7.0 \, \text{kW} \\
\cos\phi &= \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L} = \frac{7000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 15} \\
\cos\phi &= \frac{7000}{10392.3} \approx 0.6736 \, \text{lag}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{650.92}{7000} \times 100 \\
\eta &\approx 9.30 \, \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **low efficiency** in this question is intentional — it is a *part-load point*. In a real load-test graph, $\eta$ peaks near rated load and falls at low load. KTU examiners often ask students to **comment on this**.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**Q. (a)** Explain the **rotor resistance starting method** for slip-ring induction motors. With the help of torque-slip curves, show how varying external rotor resistance improves starting torque. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A 6-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase slip-ring induction motor gave the following readings during a load test:
$V_L = 415 \, \text{V}$, $I_L = 18 \, \text{A}$, $W_1 = 7000 \, \text{W}$, $W_2 = 2000 \, \text{W}$, $W_{s1} = 9.0 \, \text{kgf}$, $W_{s2} = 3.0 \, \text{kgf}$, $N_r = 940 \, \text{rpm}$, drum radius $R = 0.10 \, \text{m}$.

Determine:
(i) Synchronous speed and slip
(ii) Shaft torque
(iii) Output power
(iv) Power factor
(v) Efficiency
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (a)

**Rotor Resistance Starting — Explanation (7 marks):**

In a slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motor, the three rotor phase windings are brought out through **slip rings and brush gear**. A **3-phase star-connected external rheostat** is connected to these brushes.

**Starting Procedure:**

1. With the rotor initially at standstill, the **full external resistance** is inserted in each rotor phase through the rheostat.
2. The stator is energized. The high rotor resistance limits the rotor inrush current to a safe value (~1.5–2 × rated).
3. As the motor accelerates, the **slip decreases**. The external resistance is *gradually cut out in steps*.
4. Once full speed is reached, the slip rings are **short-circuited** and the brushes are usually *lifted* to reduce wear.

**Effect on torque-slip curve:**

The slip at which maximum torque occurs is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{m_T} = \frac{R_2' + R_{ext}'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_2')^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $R_2'$ = rotor winding resistance referred to stator
- $R_{ext}'$ = external rotor resistance referred to stator (this is what we vary)
- $R_{th}, X_{th}$ = Thevenin equivalent resistance and reactance

By increasing $R_{ext}'$, $s_{m_T}$ moves closer to **1.0** (starting). The starting torque can be made equal to or greater than the full-load torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** Although starting torque increases, the **slip at full load also increases**, which means the motor runs slower (more speed drop). This is acceptable for short-time-rated applications like crane hoists, but **not for constant-speed drives**.

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (b)

**(i) Synchronous speed and slip**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \, \text{rpm} \\
s &= \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1000 - 940}{1000} = 0.06 \, \text{p.u. (6 %)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Shaft torque**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sh} &= (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \, g \, R = (9.0 - 3.0) \times 9.81 \times 0.10 \\
T_{sh} &= 6.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 = 5.886 \, \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Output power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \frac{2 \pi N_r \, T_{sh}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 940 \times 5.886}{60} \\
P_{out} &= \frac{34762.8}{60} \approx 579.38 \, \text{W} \approx 0.579 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Power factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= W_1 + W_2 = 7000 + 2000 = 9000 \, \text{W} \\
\cos\phi &= \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L} = \frac{9000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 18} \\
\cos\phi &= \frac{9000}{12937.0} \approx 0.6957 \, \text{lag}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{579.38}{9000} \times 100 \approx 6.44 \, \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $g$ factor:** Spring balance readings are in **kgf**, but torque needs **Newtons**. Always multiply the difference $(W_{s1} - W_{s2})$ by $g = 9.81$. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Using phase voltage instead of line voltage in $\sqrt{3} V_L I_L$:** The two-wattmeter formula uses **line** quantities. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing units in $R$:** Drum radius must be in **meters**, not cm. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the slip calculation:** Examiners expect $s$ to be calculated *first* and *shown explicitly*. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Reporting efficiency $> 100$ %:** If your answer crosses 100, you have either used $P_{out}$ from gross torque (not shaft torque) or confused $P_{in}$ and $P_{out}$. Recheck.
> 6. **Not commenting on why efficiency is low:** Add a concluding remark such as: *"The motor is operating significantly below rated load, hence the efficiency is low; near rated load the efficiency would approach 85–92 %."* Marks lost: **0.5 mark** for lack of discussion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember for KTU 2024 Scheme Exams (Module Experiment 2):**

- [x] The **synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ depends *only* on supply frequency and number of poles — **NOT** on load.
- [x] The **rotor speed** $N_r < N_s$ always, and $N_r \to N_s$ only at no-load.
- [x] **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$ is the fundamental performance indicator.
- [x] **Shaft torque** $T_{sh} = (W_{s1} - W_{s2}) \, g \, R$ — units must be **N·m**, so multiply kgf readings by $g = 9.81$ and use $R$ in **meters**.
- [x] **Output power** $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_r T_{sh}}{60}$ in Watts when $N_r$ is in rpm.
- [x] **Input power** $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ from the two-wattmeter method.
- [x] **Power factor** $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_L}$, where $V_L$ and $I_L$ are **line** values.
- [x] **Efficiency** $\eta = (P_{out} / P_{in}) \times 100$ — always report in **%**.
- [x] **Rotor copper loss** $P_{rcu} = s \cdot P_{ag}$, and $P_{ag} = P_{in} - \text{Stator losses}$.
- [x] The **unique feature** of SRIM is the **external rotor resistance** through slip rings — enables high starting torque and limited speed control.
- [x] **Slip-ring motor vs. squirrel-cage motor:** SRIM gives higher starting torque and lower starting current; squirrel-cage is cheaper and more robust.
- [x] **Two-wattmeter method** works only for balanced loads (or near-balanced); one wattmeter reads high and the other low at low power factors.
- [x] **Practical caveat:** Rope-brake cooling is critical. Without water cooling, the drum overheats and readings drift.
- [x] **Test procedure order:** No-load run first to verify connections → record no-load readings → apply load in 4–5 steps → record full set of readings at each step.
- [x] **Graph to be plotted:** $T_{sh}$ vs $N_r$, $\eta$ vs $P_{out}$, $\cos\phi$ vs $P_{out}$, slip $s$ vs $P_{out}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: No Load and Blocked Rotor Tests on a Three-Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** No Load and Blocked Rotor Tests on a Three-Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **No-Load Test** is a no-load performance test performed on a three-phase squirrel cage induction motor running at its rated voltage and rated frequency, with the shaft mechanically uncoupled from any load. The objective is to determine the **rotational losses** (core loss + friction and windage loss) and the parameters of the **magnetizing branch** of the per-phase equivalent circuit. **Blocked Rotor Test** (also called the **Locked Rotor Test** or **Short-Circuit Test**) is performed with the rotor mechanically locked, by applying a **reduced balanced polyphase voltage** to the stator terminals, just sufficient to circulate the **rated stator current**. The objective is to determine the **rotor copper loss at standstill** and the parameters of the **leakage impedance branch** ($R_1 + R_2'$ and $X_1 + X_2'$) of the per-phase equivalent circuit. > [!IMPORTANT] > These two tests together are the **indirect (non-loading) methods** used to obtain the complete equivalent circuit parameters of a squirrel cage induction motor — the rotor being inaccessible makes the **direct measurement of rotor resistance and reactance physically impossible**, hence this dual-test methodology is mandatory. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you bought a sealed black box labeled "Induction Motor" and you are not allowed to open it. The two tests are like performing two cleverly designed "non-invasive medical scans" to deduce the internal organs of the motor: - **No-Load Test (The "Idle Scan")**: Think of a ceiling fan running on "low speed with no load" (just the propeller spinning in air). You listen to the hum, measure the small current it draws, and the small power it consumes. This tiny power is mostly being "wasted" as iron heating (hysteresis + eddy current) and as friction/windage drag of the rotor. From this, you deduce how "magnetic" the iron core is — its magnetizing inductance and core loss resistance. - **Blocked Rotor Test (The "Stuck Scan")**: Now imagine you jam the ceiling fan's blades with your hand and re-energize it briefly. The motor draws a HUGE current (just like a transformer with its secondary shorted). By measuring the modest voltage needed to push the rated current through this "stuck" motor, you can deduce the "internal resistance and leakage of the copper windings" — because almost all voltage is being dropped across the series impedances, with the magnetic iron core (magnetizing branch) being essentially "short-circuited" by the dominant current flow. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics (Memorize These):** > - **Rated Frequency:** $f = 50$ **Hz** (India) > - **No-Load Current:** $I_0 \approx 30\text{–}40\%$ of rated full-load current > - **Blocked Rotor Test Voltage:** $V_{sc} \approx 10\text{–}25\%$ of rated voltage to circulate rated current > - **Power Factor at No-Load:** $\cos\phi_0 \approx 0.1\text{–}0.3$ (highly lagging) > - **Power Factor at Blocked Rotor:** $\cos\phi_{sc} \approx 0.3\text{–}0.5$ (lagging) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit — The Foundation The per-phase equivalent circuit of a three-phase induction motor (referred to stator) consists of: - **Stator branch:** $R_1$ (stator winding resistance) in series with $jX_1$ (stator leakage reactance) - **Magnetizing branch (no-load branch):** $R_c$ (core-loss resistance) in parallel with $jX_m$ (magnetizing reactance) - **Rotor branch (referred):** $\dfrac{R_2'}{s}$ in series with $jX_2'$ (rotor leakage reactance referred) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the no-load test gives magnetizing parameters:** At no-load, slip $s \approx 0$, so $\dfrac{R_2'}{s} \rightarrow \infty$. Hence almost all stator current flows through the magnetizing branch — the rotor branch is effectively "open." The test directly yields $R_c$ and $X_m$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the blocked rotor test gives leakage parameters:** At blocked rotor, $s = 1$, and the applied voltage is very low. The magnetizing reactance $X_m$ is large compared to the leakage reactances, so the magnetizing branch can be **neglected** (moved to the input terminals with negligible error). The circuit reduces to a simple series combination of $(R_1 + R_2') + j(X_1 + X_2')$. ### 2.2 No-Load Test — Measurement Procedure **Step 1:** Connect the stator in **star** (or delta, as per nameplate) to a three-phase variac (auto-transformer) with appropriate metering. **Step 2:** Apply **rated voltage** and **rated frequency** to the stator terminals. The rotor is uncoupled — it runs at near-synchronous speed. **Step 3:** Measure using **two-wattmeter method** (for 3-phase, 3-wire system): - Line voltage $V_L$ (volts) - Line current $I_0$ (amperes) — equal in all three phases - Total no-load power input $P_0$ (watts) = $W_1 + W_2$ **Step 4:** Also measure the **DC stator resistance** between any two stator terminals using an ammeter-voltmeter method or a Kelvin bridge, immediately after the test (to capture the hot resistance, or with temperature correction). ### 2.3 Blocked Rotor Test — Measurement Procedure **Step 1:** Mechanically lock the rotor using a strong metal bracket/bolt arrangement. **Safety first — the rotor must NOT move, even slightly, when energized.** **Step 2:** Apply **reduced balanced three-phase voltage** through the variac. Gradually increase the voltage from zero until the ammeter reads the **rated full-load current** of the motor. **Step 3:** Measure: - Line voltage $V_{sc}$ (volts) - Line current $I_{sc}$ (amperes) = rated current - Total blocked-rotor power $P_{sc}$ (watts) = $W_1 + W_2$ > [!WARNING] > **Examination Pitfall:** The blocked rotor test is conducted with **reduced voltage**, NOT rated voltage. Applying rated voltage to a blocked rotor will cause **catastrophic overheating** within seconds and may burn out the windings. This is a very common mistake in lab vivas. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula (Star Connection) | Units | |---|----------|---------------------------|-------| | 1 | No-load power factor | $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{P_0}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_0}$ | dimensionless | | 2 | No-load reactive power | $Q_0 = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_0 \cdot \sin\phi_0$ | VAR | | 3 | Stator Cu loss at no-load | $P_{scu,0} = 3 \cdot I_0^2 \cdot R_1$ | W | | 4 | Rotational losses (constant) | $P_{rot} = P_0 - 3 I_0^2 R_1$ | W | | 5 | No-load impedance per phase | $Z_0 = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot I_0}$ | $\Omega$ | | 6 | Core loss resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{(V_L/\sqrt{3})^2}{P_{rot}/3}$ | $\Omega$ | | 7 | Magnetizing reactance | $X_m = \dfrac{(V_L/\sqrt{3})^2}{Q_0/3}$ | $\Omega$ | | 8 | Blocked rotor power factor | $\cos\phi_{sc} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Equivalent impedance | $Z_{eq} = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | $\Omega$ | | 10 | Equivalent resistance | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{3 \cdot I_{sc}^2}$ | $\Omega$ | | 11 | Equivalent reactance | $X_{eq} = \sqrt{Z_{eq}^2 - R_{eq}^2}$ | $\Omega$ | | 12 | Stator resistance (DC) | $R_1 = \dfrac{R_{dc}}{2}$ (star), $\dfrac{3 R_{dc}}{2}$ (delta) | $\Omega$ | | 13 | Splitting leakage | $R_1 = R_2' = \dfrac{R_{eq} - R_1}{1} = \dfrac{R_{eq}}{2}$ (approx) | $\Omega$ | | 14 | Splitting leakage | $X_1 = X_2' = \dfrac{X_{eq}}{2}$ (approx) | $\Omega$ | | 15 | Short-circuit current at rated V | $I_{sc,\text{rated}} = I_{sc} \cdot \dfrac{V_{\text{rated}}}{V_{sc}}$ | A | | 16 | Slip at full load | $s = \dfrac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}$ | dimensionless | | 17 | Rotor Cu loss at slip $s$ | $P_{rcu} = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot R_2'$ | W | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These two tests are the **universal, low-cost, indirect method** of characterizing any 3-phase induction motor in the field. They eliminate the need for an expensive dynamometer (loading test) and are the basis for constructing the **Circle Diagram** — the single most important graphical tool used by design and application engineers to predict motor performance (efficiency, slip, torque, power factor) at any load. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of No-Load Test Parameters **Starting Point:** Per-phase approximate equivalent circuit at no-load ($s \approx 0$, so $R_2'/s \to \infty$). All current $I_0$ flows through the magnetizing branch $(R_c \parallel jX_m)$. The total complex power input per phase is: $$S_{0,\text{ph}} = V_{\text{ph}} \cdot I_0^*$$ The total three-phase apparent power at no-load: $$S_0 = 3 \cdot V_{\text{ph}} \cdot I_0 = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_0$$ The real power (measured by wattmeters): $$P_0 = S_0 \cdot \cos\phi_0 = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_0 \cdot \cos\phi_0$$ The reactive power (not directly measured, but derived): $$Q_0 = S_0 \cdot \sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_0 \cdot \sin\phi_0$$ The no-load power factor angle: $$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_0} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi_0 = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_0}\right)$$ **Now, separating the losses:** The total no-load power $P_0$ consists of (a) stator copper loss at no-load current, and (b) the rotational losses (core + friction & windage), which are essentially constant from no-load to full-load (for a given $V$ and $f$): $$P_0 = 3 \cdot I_0^2 \cdot R_1 + P_{\text{rotational}}$$ Solving for the rotational losses: $$P_{\text{rotational}} = P_{\text{core}} + P_{F\&W} = P_0 - 3 I_0^2 R_1$$ > This is the most critical separation in the entire experiment — it isolates the **constant loss component** of the motor, which is assumed invariant with load. **Deriving $R_c$:** Since the core loss per phase is $P_{\text{core}}/3$ dissipated in $R_c$ at phase voltage $V_{\text{ph}} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$: $$R_c = \frac{V_{\text{ph}}^2}{P_{\text{core}}/3} = \frac{3 \cdot (V_L/\sqrt{3})^2}{P_{\text{core}}} = \frac{V_L^2}{P_{\text{core}}}$$ **Deriving $X_m$:** The reactive power per phase flows through $X_m$: $$X_m = \frac{V_{\text{ph}}^2}{Q_0/3} = \frac{3 \cdot (V_L/\sqrt{3})^2}{Q_0} = \frac{V_L^2}{Q_0}$$ where $Q_0 = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_0 \cdot \sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{S_0^2 - P_0^2}$. ### 3.2 Derivation of Blocked Rotor Test Parameters **Starting Point:** Per-phase equivalent circuit at blocked rotor ($s = 1$, with magnetizing branch neglected). The circuit reduces to a simple series combination of $R_1 + jX_1 + R_2' + jX_2'$. Define the per-phase equivalent impedance: $$Z_{eq} = (R_1 + R_2') + j(X_1 + X_2') = R_{eq} + jX_{eq}$$ Magnitude: $$\vert Z_{eq} \vert = \frac{V_{\text{ph,sc}}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{sc}}$$ The total real power input is consumed entirely in the copper losses (core loss is negligible at low voltage): $$P_{sc} = 3 \cdot I_{sc}^2 \cdot R_{eq}$$ Solving for $R_{eq}$: $$R_{eq} = \frac{P_{sc}}{3 \cdot I_{sc}^2} = \frac{R_1 + R_2'}{1}$$ Computing the reactive part: $$X_{eq} = \sqrt{\vert Z_{eq} \vert^2 - R_{eq}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} I_{sc}}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{P_{sc}}{3 I_{sc}^2}\right)^2}$$ ### 3.3 Separation of $R_1$ from $R_2'$ and $X_1$ from $X_2'$ The two tests give us the **sums** $(R_1 + R_2')$ and $(X_1 + X_2')$ — not the individual components. To separate them, we use the **approximate assumption** (valid for KTU-level problems): > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Assumption (Memorize):** $R_1 = R_2'$ and $X_1 = X_2'$ This gives: $$R_1 = R_2' = \frac{R_{eq}}{2}, \quad X_1 = X_2' = \frac{X_{eq}}{2}$$ A more accurate separation uses the slip at maximum torque $s_{mT} = R_2' / X_{eq}$ (when the rotor branch is shifted past the magnetizing reactance) — but this is reserved for advanced problems. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem) **Given Data** (typical KTU past year): - Motor: 3-phase, star-connected, 5 HP, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole - **No-load test:** $V_L = 400$ V, $I_0 = 4.0$ A, $P_0 = 350$ W - **Blocked rotor test:** $V_{sc} = 100$ V, $I_{sc} = 10.0$ A, $P_{sc} = 600$ W - **DC resistance** between two stator terminals: $R_{dc} = 0.8\ \Omega$ (hot) **Step 1: Stator resistance per phase (star-connected)** $$R_1 = \frac{R_{dc}}{2} = \frac{0.8}{2} = 0.4\ \Omega$$ **Step 2: No-load calculations** No-load power factor: $$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_0} = \frac{350}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 4.0} = \frac{350}{2771.28} = 0.1263$$ $$\phi_0 = \cos^{-1}(0.1263) = 82.74^\circ$$ No-load impedance per phase: $$Z_0 = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3} \cdot I_0} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3} \times 4.0} = 57.735\ \Omega$$ Stator Cu loss at no-load: $$P_{scu,0} = 3 \cdot I_0^2 \cdot R_1 = 3 \times (4.0)^2 \times 0.4 = 19.2\ \text{W}$$ Rotational (constant) losses: $$P_{\text{rot}} = P_0 - P_{scu,0} = 350 - 19.2 = 330.8\ \text{W}$$ Core-loss resistance: $$R_c = \frac{V_L^2}{P_{\text{rot}}} = \frac{(400)^2}{330.8} = 483.79\ \Omega$$ Magnetizing reactance: $$Q_0 = \sqrt{(\sqrt{3} V_L I_0)^2 - P_0^2} = \sqrt{(2771.28)^2 - (350)^2} = \sqrt{7679994 - 122500} = 2749.03\ \text{VAR}$$ $$X_m = \frac{V_L^2}{Q_0} = \frac{160000}{2749.03} = 58.20\ \Omega$$ **Step 3: Blocked rotor calculations** Blocked rotor power factor: $$\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{P_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{600}{\sqrt{3} \times 100 \times 10.0} = \frac{600}{1732.05} = 0.3464$$ $$\phi_{sc} = \cos^{-1}(0.3464) = 69.74^\circ$$ Equivalent impedance per phase: $$Z_{eq} = \frac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{3} \times 10.0} = 5.774\ \Omega$$ Equivalent resistance: $$R_{eq} = \frac{P_{sc}}{3 \cdot I_{sc}^2} = \frac{600}{3 \times 100} = 2.0\ \Omega$$ Equivalent reactance: $$X_{eq} = \sqrt{Z_{eq}^2 - R_{eq}^2} = \sqrt{(5.774)^2 - (2.0)^2} = \sqrt{33.34 - 4.0} = \sqrt{29.34} = 5.417\ \Omega$$ **Step 4: Splitting the leakage parameters** $$R_1 = R_2' = \frac{R_{eq}}{2} = \frac{2.0}{2} = 1.0\ \Omega$$ $$X_1 = X_2' = \frac{X_{eq}}{2} = \frac{5.417}{2} = 2.709\ \Omega$$ **Final Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit Parameters:** $$\boxed{R_1 = 1.0\ \Omega,\ R_2' = 1.0\ \Omega,\ X_1 = 2.709\ \Omega,\ X_2' = 2.709\ \Omega,\ R_c = 483.79\ \Omega,\ X_m = 58.20\ \Omega}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check (Important for KTU):** $R_c \gg X_m$? Here $R_c = 483.79\ \Omega$, $X_m = 58.20\ \Omega$ — wait, this is reversed. Actually, the correct relationship is $R_c \gg X_m$ for a typical motor, meaning **core loss is much smaller than magnetizing VAR**. Our result shows $R_c$ large = small current through $R_c$ = small core loss — which is correct. The numerical ratio depends on the motor design. Our values are consistent. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Tests to Parameters | Equivalent Circuit Parameter | Test That Yields It | Formula Used | |------------------------------|---------------------|--------------| | $R_1$ (stator Cu resistance) | DC resistance test | $R_1 = R_{dc}/2$ (star) | | $R_c$ (core loss resistance) | No-load test | $R_c = V_L^2 / P_{\text{rot}}$ | | $X_m$ (magnetizing reactance) | No-load test | $X_m = V_L^2 / Q_0$ | | $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2'$ | Blocked rotor test | $R_{eq} = P_{sc} / (3 I_{sc}^2)$ | | $X_{eq} = X_1 + X_2'$ | Blocked rotor test | $X_{eq} = \sqrt{Z_{eq}^2 - R_{eq}^2}$ | | $R_2'$ (rotor Cu resistance ref.) | Both tests | $R_2' = R_{eq} - R_1$ (exact) or $R_{eq}/2$ (approx) | | $X_1$ (stator leakage) | Both tests | $X_1 = X_{eq}/2$ (approx) | | $X_2'$ (rotor leakage ref.) | Both tests | $X_2' = X_{eq}/2$ (approx) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Test Setup Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the test setup, including the test instruments, motor connections, and the logical flow of data acquisition. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[3Phase Variac
Auto Transformer] --> B[Voltmeter V_L] A --> C[Ammeter I_line] A --> D[Wattmeter W1] A --> E[Wattmeter W2] B --> F[3Phase Induction Motor
Stator Terminals] C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G{Rotor Status} G -->|No Load Test| H[Free Rotor
Near Sync Speed] G -->|Blocked Rotor Test| I[Mechanically Locked
s = 1] H --> J[Readings V_L0 I_0 P_0] I --> K[Readings V_sc I_sc P_sc] J --> L[Compute R_c and X_m] K --> M[Compute R_eq and X_eq] L --> N[Equivalent Circuit
Per Phase Model] M --> N N --> O[Final Motor Parameters
R1 R2 X1 X2 Rc Xm] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Equivalent Circuit Model The next diagram represents the **per-phase equivalent circuit** of the induction motor after the parameters have been extracted from the two tests. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[V_ph Stator Terminal] --> B[R1 Stator Resistance] B --> C[jX1 Stator Leakage Reactance] C --> D[Node A Air Gap] D --> E[jXm Magnetizing Reactance] D --> F[jX2 Prime Rotor Leakage Reactance] D --> G[Rc Core Loss Resistance] E --> H[Node B Neutral] G --> H F --> I[R2 prime over s Rotor Resistance] I --> H ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Flow Chart — Calculation Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Test Data] --> B[Measure R_dc between 2 terminals] B --> C{Rotor Star or Delta} C -->|Star| D[R1 = R_dc divided by 2] C -->|Delta| E[R1 = 1.5 times R_dc] D --> F[No Load Test Rated V and f] E --> F F --> G[Record V_L I_0 P_0] G --> H[Compute cos phi_0] H --> I[Compute Q_0] I --> J[Compute P_rot = P_0 minus 3 I0^2 R1] J --> K[Compute R_c and X_m] K --> L[Blocked Rotor Test Reduced V] L --> M[Record V_sc I_sc P_sc] M --> N[Compute Z_eq R_eq X_eq] N --> O[Split R1 = R2 prime and X1 = X2 prime] O --> P[Equivalent Circuit Ready] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Mermaid Diagrams:** The block diagrams are simplified to functional abstractions. In the actual lab, the wattmeters $W_1$ and $W_2$ are connected with their **current coils** in series with two of the three lines and their **voltage coils** connected between the respective line and the third line (two-wattmeter method). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Why is the no-load test on a three-phase induction motor conducted at rated voltage and rated frequency?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The no-load test is conducted at rated voltage and rated frequency because: 1. The core loss ($P_{\text{core}}$) and friction-windage losses ($P_{F\&W}$) depend on the **operating flux density** and **shaft speed**, both of which are functions of the applied voltage and supply frequency. 2. The **magnetizing branch parameters** ($R_c$ and $X_m$) are non-linear functions of flux; hence they must be evaluated at the actual working point. 3. If performed at reduced voltage, the measured no-load current and losses will not represent the motor's true in-service behavior, and the equivalent circuit derived will give erroneous performance predictions. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the purpose of conducting the blocked rotor test at reduced voltage? List the quantities measured.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The blocked rotor test is conducted at **reduced voltage** (typically 10–25% of rated) to circulate the **rated full-load current** through the stator. If rated voltage were applied, the short-circuit current would be 5 to 7 times the rated current, causing **catastrophic overheating** of the windings within seconds, potentially burning out the motor. By reducing the voltage, the current is safely limited to its rated value, and the test can be completed in the few seconds needed to record the readings. **Quantities Measured:** - Line voltage $V_{sc}$ - Line current $I_{sc}$ - Total input power $P_{sc}$ (using two-wattmeter method) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) #### **Question A: 14 Marks** **(a) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *Explain with neat circuit diagrams the procedure to conduct the no-load and blocked rotor tests on a three-phase squirrel cage induction motor. What are the precautions to be taken?* **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **No-Load Test Procedure:** 1. **Connections:** The three stator terminals of the motor (star or delta connected as per nameplate) are connected to a three-phase variac through an ammeter, voltmeter, and two wattmeters (two-wattmeter method). The rotor is left free, with its shaft uncoupled from any mechanical load. 2. **Energization:** The variac is gradually increased from zero to the rated voltage of the motor. The rated frequency is maintained. 3. **Readings:** At rated voltage, the no-load line current $I_0$, the line voltage $V_L$, and the two wattmeter readings $W_1$ and $W_2$ are noted. 4. **Power Calculation:** Total no-load power $P_0 = W_1 + W_2$ (when both wattmeters read positive). 5. **DC Resistance:** Immediately after the test (or at a known temperature), the DC resistance between any two stator terminals is measured using an ammeter-voltmeter method. **Precautions during no-load test:** - The motor must run at rated voltage and frequency. - The shaft must be completely free of any load or friction (except the inherent bearing friction, which is included in $P_{F\&W}$). - Wait for the motor to reach steady-state (a few minutes) before recording readings. **Blocked Rotor Test Procedure:** 1. **Mechanical Locking:** The rotor is firmly locked using a strong metal bracket/bolt such that it cannot rotate even under electromagnetic torque. This corresponds to slip $s = 1$. 2. **Reduced Voltage:** The stator is connected to a three-phase variac. Voltage is increased **gradually from zero** until the ammeter reads the rated full-load current of the motor. 3. **Readings:** The reduced line voltage $V_{sc}$, the rated line current $I_{sc}$, and the wattmeter readings are noted. The total power $P_{sc} = W_1 + W_2$ is calculated. 4. The test is completed quickly (within 10–15 seconds) to avoid overheating. **Precautions during blocked rotor test:** - The rotor must be **rigidly locked** — no slip. - The voltage must be increased gradually to avoid current surges. - Readings must be taken **quickly** to prevent insulation damage. - One wattmeter may read negative (reverse the connection and subtract). **(b) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *The following data are obtained from tests on a 3-phase, 5 HP, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole, star-connected squirrel cage induction motor:* (7 Marks) - **No-load test:** 400 V, 3.5 A, 280 W - **Blocked rotor test:** 90 V, 10.0 A, 540 W - **DC resistance** between two stator terminals: $0.7\ \Omega$ *Calculate the per-phase equivalent circuit parameters referred to the stator.* **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Stator resistance per phase (star-connected)** $$R_1 = \frac{R_{dc}}{2} = \frac{0.7}{2} = 0.35\ \Omega$$ **Step 2: No-load calculations** $$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_0} = \frac{280}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 3.5} = \frac{280}{2424.87} = 0.1155$$ $$\phi_0 = \cos^{-1}(0.1155) = 83.37^\circ$$ Stator Cu loss at no-load: $$P_{scu,0} = 3 \cdot I_0^2 \cdot R_1 = 3 \times (3.5)^2 \times 0.35 = 12.86\ \text{W}$$ Rotational losses: $$P_{\text{rot}} = P_0 - P_{scu,0} = 280 - 12.86 = 267.14\ \text{W}$$ Core loss resistance: $$R_c = \frac{V_L^2}{P_{\text{rot}}} = \frac{(400)^2}{267.14} = \frac{160000}{267.14} = 598.96\ \Omega$$ Magnetizing reactance: $$Q_0 = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_0 \cdot \sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 3.5 \times \sin(83.37^\circ) = 2424.87 \times 0.9933 = 2408.6\ \text{VAR}$$ $$X_m = \frac{V_L^2}{Q_0} = \frac{160000}{2408.6} = 66.43\ \Omega$$ **Step 3: Blocked rotor calculations** $$\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{P_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{540}{\sqrt{3} \times 90 \times 10.0} = \frac{540}{1558.85} = 0.3464$$ $$Z_{eq} = \frac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{90}{\sqrt{3} \times 10.0} = 5.196\ \Omega$$ $$R_{eq} = \frac{P_{sc}}{3 \cdot I_{sc}^2} = \frac{540}{3 \times 100} = 1.80\ \Omega$$ $$X_{eq} = \sqrt{Z_{eq}^2 - R_{eq}^2} = \sqrt{(5.196)^2 - (1.80)^2} = \sqrt{27.00 - 3.24} = \sqrt{23.76} = 4.874\ \Omega$$ **Step 4: Splitting the leakage parameters** $$R_2' = R_{eq} - R_1 = 1.80 - 0.35 = 1.45\ \Omega$$ $$R_1 = 0.35\ \Omega \quad \text{(from DC test)}$$ $$X_1 = X_2' = \frac{X_{eq}}{2} = \frac{4.874}{2} = 2.437\ \Omega$$ **Final Per-Phase Parameters:** $$\boxed{R_1 = 0.35\ \Omega,\ R_2' = 1.45\ \Omega,\ X_1 = X_2' = 2.437\ \Omega,\ R_c = 598.96\ \Omega,\ X_m = 66.43\ \Omega}$$ --- #### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Derive the expressions for the no-load branch parameters ($R_c$ and $X_m$) and the leakage branch parameters ($R_1 + R_2'$ and $X_1 + X_2'$) of the equivalent circuit of a three-phase induction motor using the no-load and blocked rotor test data.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Derivation of $R_c$ and $X_m$ (from no-load test):** At no-load, slip $s \approx 0$ and rotor branch impedance $\dfrac{R_2'}{s} \to \infty$. The per-phase equivalent circuit reduces to the magnetizing branch $(R_c \parallel jX_m)$ in series with $R_1 + jX_1$. The total complex power per phase at no-load is: $$S_{0,\text{ph}} = V_{\text{ph}} \cdot I_0^*$$ The real power dissipated in $R_c$ per phase is $V_{\text{ph}}^2 / R_c$, and the reactive power stored in $X_m$ per phase is $V_{\text{ph}}^2 / X_m$. The three-phase no-load input is: $$P_0 = \sqrt{3} V_L I_0 \cos\phi_0, \quad Q_0 = \sqrt{3} V_L I_0 \sin\phi_0$$ Subtracting the stator Cu loss at no-load: $$P_{\text{core}} = P_0 - 3 I_0^2 R_1$$ For a typical motor, $R_1 \ll \vert R_c + jX_m \vert$, so the voltage drop across $R_1 + jX_1$ is negligible, and $V_{\text{ph}}$ is approximately across the magnetizing branch. Hence: $$R_c = \frac{V_{\text{ph}}^2}{P_{\text{core}}/3} = \frac{V_L^2}{P_{\text{core}}}$$ $$X_m = \frac{V_{\text{ph}}^2}{Q_0/3} = \frac{V_L^2}{Q_0}$$ **Derivation of $R_{eq}$ and $X_{eq}$ (from blocked rotor test):** At blocked rotor ($s = 1$), with reduced voltage applied, the magnetizing branch is neglected (because $X_m \gg X_1 + X_2'$, so most current flows through the low-impedance series path). The circuit reduces to: $$Z_{eq} = (R_1 + R_2') + j(X_1 + X_2')$$ The total input power is dissipated entirely in the series resistances (core loss at low voltage is negligible): $$P_{sc} = 3 I_{sc}^2 (R_1 + R_2') = 3 I_{sc}^2 R_{eq}$$ Therefore: $$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2' = \frac{P_{sc}}{3 I_{sc}^2}$$ The magnitude of the equivalent impedance: $$\vert Z_{eq} \vert = \frac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} I_{sc}}$$ Therefore: $$X_{eq} = X_1 + X_2' = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{sc}}{\sqrt{3} I_{sc}}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{P_{sc}}{3 I_{sc}^2}\right)^2}$$ **Separation into individual parameters:** Using the approximation $R_1 = R_2'$ and $X_1 = X_2'$: $$R_1 = R_2' = \frac{R_{eq}}{2}, \quad X_1 = X_2' = \frac{X_{eq}}{2}$$ A more accurate separation uses $R_2' = X_{eq} \cdot s_{mT}$ where $s_{mT}$ is the slip at maximum torque (obtainable from the load test or from manufacturer's data). **(b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Explain how the circle diagram of a three-phase induction motor is constructed using the data obtained from the no-load and blocked rotor tests. List the information that can be obtained from the circle diagram.* **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Construction Steps of the Circle Diagram:** The **circle diagram** is a graphical representation of the locus of the stator current phasor as the load (slip) varies, plotted on a $P$-$Q$ (or $X$-$Y$) coordinate plane. It is constructed as follows: 1. **Choose the scale:** Select a convenient scale for power (e.g., 1 cm = 200 W) and mark the $P$-axis (horizontal) and $Q$-axis (vertical). 2. **Mark the no-load point (NOL):** Compute no-load $P_0$ and $Q_0$ from the no-load test, and plot point **NL** at $(P_0, Q_0)$. This represents the motor running at no-load. 3. **Mark the blocked rotor point (BR or SC):** Compute $P_{sc}$ (at rated voltage — extrapolate from test data: $P_{sc,\text{rated}} = P_{sc} \times (V_{\text{rated}}/V_{sc})^2$ approximately) and corresponding $Q_{sc}$, and plot point **SC** at $(P_{sc,\text{rated}}, Q_{sc,\text{rated}})$. This represents the motor at standstill with rated voltage. 4. **Draw the circle:** The locus of the stator current phasor is a circle. Draw a circle passing through the points **NL** and **SC** such that the line joining them is a **chord** of the circle. The center of the circle lies on the perpendicular bisector of the chord NL-SC, and the diameter is determined by the equation of the induction motor circle (the circle is tangent to the horizontal line drawn at $Q = $ magnetizing VAR, and its diameter equals $V_{\text{ph}}/X_1$ in terms of current). 5. **Draw the output line:** From the no-load point **NL**, draw the **output power line** at an angle $\theta$ below the $P$-axis, where $\tan\theta = (P_{F\&W} + P_{\text{core}}) / (\text{Rotational losses portion})$. This line is parallel to the line connecting origin to the point representing the rotational losses. 6. **Draw the torque line:** The air-gap torque line passes through the origin (or through the blocked rotor point with proper offset). It is parallel to the output line but shifted. 7. **Operate at any load:** To find the operating point at slip $s$, draw a line from the no-load point NL to the point of intersection of the torque line and the circle. The point on the circle represents the stator current phasor; dropping a perpendicular gives $P_{\text{in}}$, and the horizontal distance from the output line gives $P_{\text{out}}$, etc. **Information Obtainable from the Circle Diagram:** | # | Quantity | How Obtained | |---|----------|--------------| | 1 | Stator current at any load | Distance from origin to the operating point on the circle | | 2 | Power factor at any load | $\cos\phi = P_{\text{in}} / (\sqrt{3} V_L I)$ | | 3 | Slip at any load | Ratio of rotor Cu loss to air-gap power | | 4 | Rotor Cu loss | Distance between the operating point and the output line (horizontally) | | 5 | Output power | Distance from output line to the operating point | | 6 | Input power | $P$-coordinate of the operating point | | 7 | Efficiency | $P_{\text{out}} / P_{\text{in}}$ | | 8 | Maximum (pull-out) torque | Length of the vertical tangent from origin to the circle | | 9 | Full-load operating point | Point on the circle corresponding to rated output | | 10 | Starting current | Phasor from origin to the blocked rotor point | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in DC resistance conversion** — students often write $R_1 = R_{dc}$ instead of $R_1 = R_{dc}/2$ for star-connected motors. **Always** remember the DC test measures resistance between **two terminals**, which is **twice** the per-phase resistance for star. > 2. **Using total power $P_0$ directly for $P_{\text{core}}$** — the no-load input power includes the stator Cu loss.
