You **must** subtract $3 I_0^2 R_1$ to get the rotational losses. > 3. **Using $V_L$ instead of $V_{\text{ph}} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$** in impedance calculations for star-connected motors. > 4. **Not converting blocked rotor test data to rated voltage** when asked for the circle diagram or full-load parameters — the test is done at reduced voltage, but the circle diagram requires data at rated voltage. > 5. **Confusing the two wattmeters' sign conventions** — if one wattmeter reads negative, its contribution must be subtracted: $P_{\text{total}} = W_1 - W_2$ (or $W_2 - W_1$), not always added. > 6. **Forgetting the $1/s$ in rotor Cu loss** — at slip $s$, the rotor branch impedance is $R_2'/s + jX_2'$, NOT $R_2' + jX_2'$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **No-load test** = rated $V$, rated $f$, free rotor → yields **magnetizing branch** ($R_c$, $X_m$) and **rotational losses**. - [ ] **Blocked rotor test** = reduced $V$, rotor locked ($s = 1$) → yields **leakage branch** ($R_1 + R_2'$, $X_1 + X_2'$) and **short-circuit power factor**. - [ ] **Slip conditions:** No-load → $s \approx 0$; Blocked rotor → $s = 1$; Full load → $s = 0.02$ to $0.05$. - [ ] **Two-wattmeter method** is used for both tests in 3-phase, 3-wire systems. One wattmeter may show **negative** deflection — reverse its connection and subtract. - [ ] **Stator resistance per phase (star):** $R_1 = R_{dc}/2$. **Stator resistance per phase (delta):** $R_1 = 3R_{dc}/2$. - [ ] **No-load power factor** is very low (0.1–0.3) because magnetizing current is dominant. - [ ] **Blocked rotor power factor** is moderate (0.3–0.5) because leakage reactances dominate. - [ ] **Core-loss resistance** $R_c$ is **much larger** than $X_m$ in p.u. terms, meaning core loss is small compared to magnetizing VAR. - [ ] **Leakage reactance** $X_1 + X_2'$ is **shared equally** between stator and rotor in the KTU approximate method. - [ ] **Safety rule:** Apply voltage **gradually from zero**; never apply rated voltage to a locked rotor. - [ ] **Take readings quickly** during blocked rotor test to avoid overheating (test should last < 15 seconds). - [ ] **Power stages** of an induction motor: $P_{\text{in}} \to P_{\text{scu}} + P_{\text{core}} \to P_{\text{air gap}} \to P_{\text{rcu}} \to P_{\text{mech}} \to P_{\text{out}}$ (subtracting losses at each stage). - [ ] **Circle diagram** is the **graphical output** of these two tests and is used to predict all performance quantities. - [ ] **Synchronous speed** $n_s = 120 f / P$ (in RPM, with $P$ = number of poles). - [ ] **Mechanical power developed** $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_{\text{air gap}}$; **Rotor Cu loss** $P_{\text{rcu}} = s \cdot P_{\text{air gap}}$. - [ ] **Rated current extrapolation for blocked rotor:** $I_{sc,\text{rated}} = I_{sc,\text{test}} \times (V_{\text{rated}}/V_{sc})$. - [ ] **Most common KTU 14-mark format:** Data → Calculate parameters → Plot or use to find performance.

topics.title: Load Test on a Single-Phase Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Test on a Single-Phase Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Load Test on a Single-Phase Induction Motor** is a direct experimental procedure in which the motor is mechanically coupled to a controllable load, supplied with its rated single-phase AC voltage at rated frequency, and progressively loaded from no-load to full-load (or overload up to safe mechanical limits). The test yields the **performance characteristics**, namely **slip**, **shaft torque**, **output power**, **input power**, **power factor**, and **efficiency** of the motor as a function of mechanical loading.

The single-phase induction motor under test is invariably a **self-starting** type — typically a **Capacitor-Start**, **Capacitor-Run**, or **Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run** motor — because a plain single-phase induction motor without an auxiliary winding is **not self-starting**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a person trying to pedal a bicycle on flat ground — they can keep it moving, but they cannot start from rest without a push or an initial push-off. A single-phase AC supply, on its own, produces a magnetic field that **does not rotate** — it merely pulses in magnitude (alternates) along a fixed axis. Such a "pulsating" field cannot drag the rotor from standstill because the torque it produces in one half-cycle is exactly cancelled in the next half-cycle.

The auxiliary (start) winding with a series capacitor creates a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** that pushes the rotor into motion (the "initial push"). Once the rotor picks up speed, it continues to rotate due to the cross-field (double-revolving-field) effect. The **load test** is the equivalent of asking: *"After the bicycle is moving, how much weight can the rider pull, at what speed, and how much energy is being wasted as heat?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in This Experiment**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Synchronous speed of motor: **Nₛ = 120f / P** (rpm), where **f** is supply frequency (**50 Hz** in India) and **P** is the number of poles.
> - The rated electrical and mechanical limits of the motor (printed on the nameplate) must be respected throughout the test.

### 1.3 Types of Single-Phase Induction Motors Tested in the Lab

| Motor Type | Starting Principle | Auxiliary Winding | Capacitor Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| Split-Phase (Resistance Start) | Resistance difference creates phase shift | High-resistance auxiliary winding | No capacitor |
| Capacitor-Start | Capacitive phase shift of ~90° | Auxiliary winding | In series with auxiliary winding; disconnected by centrifugal switch at ~75% of synchronous speed |
| Capacitor-Run (Permanent Capacitor) | Continuous capacitive phase shift | Auxiliary winding | Permanently connected (small value) |
| Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run | Dual capacitor optimisation | Auxiliary winding | Start capacitor (electrolytic, large) + Run capacitor (oil-filled, small) |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle Under Load

When rated voltage is applied to the main winding, a **pulsating** alternating flux is produced. By double-revolving-field theory (Leblanc's theorem), this pulsating field can be mathematically decomposed into two counter-rotating flux waves of equal magnitude, each of half the maximum value. The rotor, once given an initial direction of rotation by the auxiliary winding, sees an **asymmetric** response to the forward and backward fields:

- The **forward field** component is reinforced by rotor reaction (acts like a normal induction motor at slip *s*).
- The **backward field** component is heavily damped and contributes only small braking torque and double-frequency losses.

Hence, the rotor accelerates to a slip just below unity, settles at a steady speed slightly less than synchronous, and can then deliver mechanical torque to the load.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Bullet Logic

- **Step 1 — Energisation**: Single-phase 230 V AC is applied across the main winding **M** and auxiliary winding **A** (with series capacitor **C**).
- **Step 2 — Production of RMF**: The capacitor creates an approximately 90° phase difference between currents in **M** and **A**, producing a rotating magnetic field.
- **Step 3 — Rotor Acceleration**: The RMF induces current in the short-circuited rotor bars (squirrel cage); electromagnetic torque accelerates the rotor.
- **Step 4 — Centrifugal Switch Action**: At about **70–80% of synchronous speed**, the centrifugal switch opens and disconnects the start winding + start capacitor from the circuit.
- **Step 5 — Steady-State Operation**: The motor continues to run on the main winding alone, with the rotating rotor sustaining its own torque via cross-field action.
- **Step 6 — Loading**: Mechanical load is gradually applied through a brake-drum / rope arrangement. Increased load demands more torque, which causes a slight drop in speed (increase in slip).
- **Step 7 — Measurement**: Voltmeter, Ammeter, Wattmeter, Tachometer, and Spring Balances are read at each loading step.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation** — Subscript **sh** denotes *shaft* (mechanical output). All readings are in SI units before substitution. Use `\vert` in LaTeX (instead of the vertical pipe `\vert` typed as ASCII) when writing absolute-value expressions inline within Markdown tables. Here, all quantities are positive by physical construction.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s$ | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm |
| 2 | Actual (Rotor) Speed | $N$ | Measured by tachometer | rpm |
| 3 | Slip | $s$ | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ | per unit (p.u.) |
| 4 | Slip Percentage | $s\%$ | $s\% = s \times 100$ | percent (%) |
| 5 | Net Tangential Force at Brake Drum | $F$ | $F = (S_1 - S_2) \times g$ where $g = 9.81$ | Newtons (N) |
| 6 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh}$ | $T_{sh} = F \times R$ where $R$ = effective pulley radius | N·m |
| 7 | Shaft Output Power | $P_{out}$ | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Input Power | $P_{in}$ | $P_{in} = W$ (Wattmeter reading) | Watts (W) |
| 9 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{V \times I}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | percent (%) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **Torque–Speed characteristic** obtained from the load test allows engineers to verify whether the motor can start and run the connected mechanical load (fan, pump, compressor, washing-machine drum, refrigerator compressor).
- The **efficiency curve** identifies the **operating point of maximum efficiency**, which is the design target for energy-saving applications (BEE star-rated appliances).
- The **power factor curve** guides whether **capacitor banks** must be installed at the installation point to avoid KSEB penalties for low PF (typically below 0.9 lag).
- Slip characteristics are critical in applications requiring **soft starting** and in **servo / precision drives** where speed variation with load is unacceptable.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Synchronous Speed

The supply frequency is $f = 50$ Hz. For a motor with $P$ poles, the alternating flux completes one electrical cycle in $\dfrac{1}{f}$ seconds. In one complete electrical cycle, the flux rotates through $\dfrac{2}{P}$ of a mechanical revolution (because each pole pair corresponds to one electrical cycle).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of mechanical revolutions per second} &= \frac{2f}{P} \\
\text{Number of mechanical revolutions per minute} &= \frac{2f}{P} \times 60 = \frac{120f}{P}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \quad \text{[rpm]}
$$

For a 4-pole, 50 Hz single-phase induction motor:

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \;\text{rpm}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Slip

Synchronous speed is the speed of the rotating magnetic field. Rotor speed $N$ is always less than $N_s$ because the rotor must "slip" with respect to the field for induced current to flow (Lenz's law). Slip is the fractional difference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{\text{Relative speed}}{\text{Synchronous speed}} \\
s &= \frac{N_s - N}{N_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Per cent slip:

$$
s\% = s \times 100
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Net Tangential Force (Rope-Brake Setup)

A rope is wrapped around the motor pulley (effective radius $R$ in metres). One end of the rope is attached to a fixed support via spring balance $S_1$; the other end is pulled via spring balance $S_2$. Both spring balances read the tension in their respective sides of the rope.

The **net tangential force** on the pulley rim due to friction is the difference of tensions:

$$
F_{net} = (S_1 - S_2) \;\; \text{[kgf]}
$$

To convert kilogram-force to Newtons, multiply by $g$:

$$
F = (S_1 - S_2) \times g = (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81 \;\; \text{[N]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If both spring balances are on the **tight side and slack side** of the rope (loose-end arrangement), the formula above is correct. If both balances are on the tight side (no fixed end), use $F = (S_1 + S_2) \times g$ — **always confirm your lab setup**.

### 3.4 Derivation of Shaft Torque

Force × radius = moment. The torque transmitted to the pulley by friction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sh} &= F \times R \\
&= (S_1 - S_2) \times g \times R \\
&= (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81 \times R \;\; \text{[N·m]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation of Shaft Output Power

In one revolution, the rope travels a linear distance $2\pi R$ at the pulley rim. The work done per revolution is $F \times 2\pi R$. At $N$ revolutions per minute, work per minute is $F \times 2\pi R \times N$. Dividing by 60 gives work per second = power:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= \frac{\text{Work per minute}}{60} \\
&= \frac{F \times 2\pi R \times N}{60} \\
&= \frac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60} \;\; \text{[W]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Derivation of Efficiency

Efficiency is the ratio of useful mechanical output to electrical input, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100
$$

> All losses (stator copper, rotor copper, core, friction & windage, stray load) are absorbed into the difference $P_{in} - P_{out}$.

### 3.7 Derivation of Power Factor

For a single-phase AC circuit:

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{P_{in}}{V \times I}
$$

Where $V$ is the voltmeter reading (RMS), $I$ is the ammeter reading (RMS), and $P_{in}$ is the wattmeter reading.

### 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** A 230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole, single-phase capacitor-start induction motor is subjected to a load test. Effective pulley radius $R = 0.075$ m. At one loading step:

- Voltmeter reading $V = 230$ V  
- Ammeter reading $I = 4.2$ A  
- Wattmeter reading $P_{in} = 720$ W  
- Spring balance 1 reading $S_1 = 5.8$ kg  
- Spring balance 2 reading $S_2 = 1.6$ kg  
- Tachometer reading $N = 1420$ rpm

**Calculate:** Synchronous speed, slip, shaft torque, output power, input power, power factor, and efficiency.

**Solution:**

Synchronous speed:

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \;\text{rpm}
$$

Slip:

$$
s = \frac{1500 - 1420}{1500} = \frac{80}{1500} = 0.0533 \;\;\text{p.u.} \;(\text{i.e., } 5.33\%)
$$

Net tangential force:

$$
F = (5.8 - 1.6) \times 9.81 = 4.2 \times 9.81 = 41.202 \;\text{N}
$$

Shaft torque:

$$
T_{sh} = F \times R = 41.202 \times 0.075 = 3.090 \;\text{N·m}
$$

Shaft output power:

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1420 \times 3.090}{60} = 459.6 \;\text{W}
$$

Power factor:

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{720}{230 \times 4.2} = \frac{720}{966} = 0.745 \;\;\text{lagging}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \frac{459.6}{720} \times 100 = 63.83\%
$$

### 3.9 Observation Table Template

| Load Step | $S_1$ (kg) | $S_2$ (kg) | $V$ (V) | $I$ (A) | $W$ (W) | $N$ (rpm) | $s$ (p.u.) | $T_{sh}$ (N·m) | $P_{out}$ (W) | $\eta$ (%) | $\cos\phi$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No-load |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| ¼ load |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| ½ load |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| ¾ load |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Full load |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Load Test Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interaction** between the motor under test, the loading device, the measuring instruments, and the AC supply. This is the KTU-recommended schematic representation when physical circuit drawings are not feasible.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACSupply[AC Single Phase 230 V 50 Hz Supply] --> MCB[MCB + Fuse Protection]
    MCB --> Voltmeter[Voltmeter V Parallel Across Motor]
    MCB --> Ammeter[Ammeter A in Series]
    MCB --> Wattmeter[Wattmeter W Current Coil in Series]
    Wattmeter --> Motor[Single Phase Induction Motor M with Aux Winding A and Capacitor C and Centrifugal Switch CS]
    Motor --> Pulley[Motor Pulley Effective Radius R]
    Pulley --> Rope[Brake Rope Wrapped on Pulley]
    Rope --> Spring1[Spring Balance S1 Fixed End]
    Rope --> Spring2[Spring Balance S2 Operator End]
    Motor --> Tacho[Tachometer N rpm]
    Motor -.Shaft Coupling.-> Generator[Optional DC Generator as Calibrated Load]
    Generator --> LoadBank[Variable Resistive Load Bank]

    classDef power fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b30000,color:#000;
    classDef measure fill:#e2eafd,stroke:#0033b3,color:#000;
    classDef mech fill:#e2fde2,stroke:#0a7a0a,color:#000;
    class ACSupply,MCB,Motor,Pulley,Rope,Generator,LoadBank power;
    class Voltmeter,Ammeter,Wattmeter,Tacho,Spring1,Spring2 measure;
```

### 4.2 Flowchart of the Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Experiment]) --> Step1[Step 1: Inspect Motor Nameplate and Record Rated V f P rpm]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Identify Main Winding M Auxiliary Winding A and Capacitor C Terminals]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Make Circuit Connections with Voltmeter Ammeter Wattmeter in Proper Polarity]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Set Loading Arrangement Rope Loose and Brake Drum Free]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Switch ON Supply and Verify Motor Accelerates to Rated Speed]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Record No Load Readings V I W N and Spring Balances]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Gradually Tighten Rope to Apply Mechanical Load in Steps]
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 8: Record All Meter Readings at Each Load Step]
    Step8 --> Step9{Load Step Reached Rated Full Load?}
    Step9 --> No[No] --> Step7
    Step9 --> Yes[Yes] --> Step10[Step 9: Loosen Rope Completely to Release Load]
    Step10 --> Step11[Step 10: Switch OFF Supply After Cooling Interval]
    Step11 --> Step12[Step 11: Tabulate Readings and Compute s Tsh Pout cos phi eta]
    Step12 --> Step13[Step 12: Plot Characteristics N vs T eta vs Pout cos phi vs Pout]
    Step13 --> End([Experiment Concluded])

    classDef proc fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000;
    classDef term fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#0a7a0a,color:#000;
    classDef dec fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000;
    class Start,End,Step9 term;
    class Step1,Step2,Step3,Step4,Step5,Step6,Step7,Step8,Step10,Step11,Step12,Step13 proc;
    class Yes,No dec;
```

### 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Reminder (Double Revolving Field Theory)

For deeper theoretical analysis, the **forward** and **backward** impedances seen by the rotor at slip $s$ are:

$$
Z_f = \frac{R_2}{s} + jX_2 + jX_m \parallel (R_1 + jX_1)
$$

$$
Z_b = \frac{R_2}{2-s} + jX_2 + jX_m \parallel (R_1 + jX_1)
$$

Forward-field torque is positive (motoring); backward-field torque is negative (braking). Net air-gap torque is their difference. While this is not the focus of the load test per se, KTU viva questions frequently probe this concept.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 2 Mark / 3 Mark Short Answer Questions

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam – 2024]**
*Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting? How is starting torque produced in a capacitor-start motor?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A single-phase AC supply produces a pulsating magnetic field along a fixed axis. This field can be mathematically resolved (Leblanc's theorem) into two equal counter-rotating fields. The torques produced by these two fields are equal and opposite at standstill, giving **zero net starting torque**.

In a **capacitor-start motor**, the auxiliary winding is connected in series with a capacitor. This causes the current in the auxiliary winding to lead the main-winding current by approximately **90 electrical degrees**, producing a true **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** that drags the rotor into motion. Once the rotor reaches ~75 % of synchronous speed, a **centrifugal switch** disconnects the auxiliary winding, and the motor continues to run on the main winding alone.

**(1 mark)** for stating the no-RMF-at-start condition, **(1 mark)** for the capacitor phase-shift explanation, **(1 mark)** for centrifugal switch action.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*What is the purpose of the load test on a single-phase induction motor? List any four quantities obtained from this test.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The purpose of the load test is to determine the **steady-state performance** of the motor under controlled loading conditions, from which the operating characteristics are plotted. The four quantities obtained are:

1. **Slip** $s = (N_s - N)/N_s$
2. **Shaft torque** $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot g \cdot R$
3. **Shaft output power** $P_{out} = 2\pi N T_{sh}/60$
4. **Efficiency** $\eta = (P_{out}/P_{in}) \times 100$

(Power factor and speed are also commonly tabulated.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often write $\cos\phi = W/(VI)$ for three-phase by mistake. For single-phase, this formula is **correct** only if $V$ and $I$ are line values (which they are in single-phase). No $\sqrt{3}$ factor is needed.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: The spring-balance formula sign. If the rope is **pulled from both sides** with a fixed wall on the other end, **subtract** ($S_1 - S_2$); if pulled only from one side with a fixed wall on the other, the tension is read **once** as the entire tangential force.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Forgetting to **convert kgf to N** by multiplying by 9.81. Marks are specifically allotted for unit conversions.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Computing $N_s$ using wrong frequency (e.g., 60 Hz for India). Always use **50 Hz** unless the question explicitly says otherwise.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Confusing shaft torque with electromagnetic torque. The load test gives **shaft torque only**; the difference between the two is the **friction and windage torque**, which is not measured in this test.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper – 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the procedure for conducting the load test on a single-phase capacitor-start induction motor. List the instruments used and the precautions to be observed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Instruments Used (2 Marks):**
- Voltmeter (0–300 V AC, MI type)
- Ammeter (0–10 A AC, MI type)
- Wattmeter (Single-phase, Dynamometer, LPF type)
- Tachometer (Digital, non-contact, 0–10000 rpm)
- Two spring balances (0–10 kg range)
- Brake drum + rope arrangement or electromagnetic dynamometer
- Connecting wires, MCB, fuse

**Procedure (4 Marks):**
1. Record the nameplate details of the motor: rated $V$, $f$, $P$ (number of poles), rated current, rated speed, and rated power.
2. Identify the **main winding** and **auxiliary winding** terminals. Ensure the **centrifugal switch** is in working condition.
3. Make the circuit connections as per the standard wiring diagram. Voltmeter is connected in **parallel**; Ammeter and Wattmeter current coil are connected in **series**.
4. Set the loading arrangement such that the rope is loose and not applying any braking torque.
5. Switch on the supply. Verify that the motor accelerates to rated speed smoothly without abnormal noise or vibration.
6. Record **no-load** readings: $V_0$, $I_0$, $W_0$, $N_0$, and both spring balance readings ($S_1 = S_2$ nominally, since no load).
7. Gradually tighten the rope to apply a small mechanical load. Allow 1–2 minutes for the system to reach thermal and mechanical steady state.
8. Record all meter readings and tachometer reading at each loading step.
9. Continue increasing the load in equal steps up to the **rated full-load** or until any instrument reaches its maximum safe value.
10. Loosen the rope fully, allow the motor to run at no-load for a few minutes, then switch off the supply.

**Precautions (1 Mark):**
- Do not exceed rated current or rated torque.
- Keep the voltage constant at rated value throughout the test.
- Ensure the spring balances are calibrated and vertically aligned.
- Do not touch rotating parts; keep loose clothing / hair tied.
- Ensure the centrifugal switch operates correctly (audible click during run-up).

**(b)** During a load test on a 230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole single-phase induction motor, the following readings were noted at one loading step. Effective pulley radius $R = 0.08$ m; Spring balance readings $S_1 = 6.0$ kg and $S_2 = 0.8$ kg; Voltmeter = 230 V; Ammeter = 5.2 A; Wattmeter = 920 W; Tachometer = 1430 rpm.

Calculate:
- (i) Synchronous speed and slip  
- (ii) Shaft torque  
- (iii) Output power  
- (iv) Power factor  
- (v) Efficiency  

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Synchronous speed and slip (2 Marks):**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \;\text{rpm}
$$

$$
s = \frac{1500 - 1430}{1500} = \frac{70}{1500} = 0.0467 \;\text{p.u.} = 4.67\%
$$

**(ii) Shaft torque (2 Marks):**

$$
F = (6.0 - 0.8) \times 9.81 = 5.2 \times 9.81 = 51.012 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
T_{sh} = F \times R = 51.012 \times 0.08 = 4.081 \;\text{N·m}
$$

**(iii) Output power (1 Mark):**

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1430 \times 4.081}{60} = 611.5 \;\text{W}
$$

**(iv) Power factor (1 Mark):**

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{920}{230 \times 5.2} = \frac{920}{1196} = 0.7693 \;\;\text{lag}
$$

**(v) Efficiency (1 Mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{611.5}{920} \times 100 = 66.46\%
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2022, Retest – 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a single-phase capacitor-start induction motor with a neat block diagram. Compare its starting torque and running performance with that of a split-phase motor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
A single-phase capacitor-start induction motor has the following components:
- **Stator**: A laminated silicon-steel core with uniformly distributed slots. It carries two windings:
  - **Main winding (Running winding)**: Distributed winding occupying about 2/3 of the stator slots, with low resistance and high reactance.
  - **Auxiliary winding (Starting winding)**: Placed in the remaining ~1/3 of the stator slots, with high resistance and low reactance. For capacitor-start motors, this winding has low DC resistance and is connected in series with a **large AC electrolytic start capacitor** (typically 80–300 µF for fractional-horsepower motors).
- **Centrifugal switch**: Mounted on the rotor shaft; opens at ~70–80 % of synchronous speed to disconnect the auxiliary winding + capacitor.
- **Rotor**: Squirrel-cage type, identical to three-phase induction motor rotors — die-cast aluminum bars in laminated slots.
- **End-shields, bearings, frame, terminal box**.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
When single-phase AC is applied, the currents in the main and auxiliary windings are phase-shifted by approximately **90°** due to the series capacitor. This produces a strong, nearly circular, **rotating magnetic field**. The RMF induces currents in the rotor and produces strong **starting torque** (typically 200–400 % of full-load torque). The rotor accelerates, and at about 75 % of synchronous speed, the centrifugal switch opens. The motor then continues to run on the main winding alone, sustained by the rotor's own rotation (cross-field torque).

**Comparison with Split-Phase Motor (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Split-Phase | Capacitor-Start |
|---|---|---|
| Phase-shift method | High resistance in aux winding | Series capacitor |
| Phase difference | ~25–30° | ~80–90° |
| Starting torque | Low (~150 % of FLT) | Very High (~300 % of FLT) |
| Starting current | Moderate | High (capacitor charging) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate (extra capacitor + switch) |
| Typical applications | Fans, blowers, small pumps | Compressors, conveyors, large pumps |

**(b)** A 230 V, 50 Hz, 2-pole single-phase induction motor draws 3.5 A and 540 W at no-load. At full-load, it draws 6.0 A and 980 W at a speed of 2820 rpm. The rotational losses (friction + windage) are 30 W. Determine:
- (i) The no-load power factor
- (ii) The full-load shaft torque if pulley radius is 0.05 m (assume spring-balance-based loading gives $S_1 - S_2 = 7.5$ kg)
- (iii) The full-load efficiency and full-load power factor
- (iv) The slip at full load

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) No-load power factor (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\cos\phi_{NL} = \frac{540}{230 \times 3.5} = \frac{540}{805} = 0.6708 \;\text{lag}
$$

**(ii) Full-load shaft torque (2 Marks):**

$$
F = 7.5 \times 9.81 = 73.575 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
T_{sh} = 73.575 \times 0.05 = 3.679 \;\text{N·m}
$$

**(iii) Full-load efficiency and power factor (2 Marks):**

First compute shaft output power at full load:

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 2820 \times 3.679}{60} = 1086.5 \;\text{W}
$$

Full-load efficiency:

$$
\eta_{FL} = \frac{1086.5}{980} \times 100 = 110.87\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note:** Efficiency greater than 100 % indicates a problem — either the spring-balance reading is inconsistent with the wattmeter reading, or the load in the problem is over-specified. In a real exam, you would **flag this anomaly** in your answer, since it is physically impossible. For academic credit, you would still report the calculated values and note the discrepancy.

Full-load power factor:

$$
\cos\phi_{FL} = \frac{980}{230 \times 6.0} = \frac{980}{1380} = 0.7101 \;\text{lag}
$$

**(iv) Slip at full load (1.5 Marks):**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{2} = 3000 \;\text{rpm}
$$

$$
s = \frac{3000 - 2820}{3000} = \frac{180}{3000} = 0.06 \;\text{p.u.} = 6\%
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **synchronous speed** of a single-phase induction motor is **$N_s = 120f/P$** — not the actual running speed.
- ✅ Single-phase induction motors are **not self-starting**; auxiliary winding + capacitor is mandatory for starting.
- ✅ **Centrifugal switch** disconnects the start winding at ~75 % of $N_s$.
- ✅ **Spring-balance formula**: $F = (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81$ N — **always convert kgf → N**.
- ✅ **Shaft torque** $T_{sh} = F \times R$, in N·m.
- ✅ **Output power** $P_{out} = 2\pi N T_{sh}/60$, in Watts.
- ✅ **Power factor** for single-phase: $\cos\phi = P_{in}/(VI)$ — **no $\sqrt{3}$**.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = (P_{out}/P_{in}) \times 100$ — must always be < 100 %.
- ✅ Slip $s$ of a healthy single-phase induction motor at full load is **3–8 %**.
- ✅ Speed at no-load is slightly less than $N_s$; speed **decreases** as load increases.
- ✅ The **double-revolving-field theory** (Leblanc) explains why the motor produces torque only after rotation begins.
- ✅ **Always keep the supply voltage at the rated value** throughout the load test.
- ✅ **Allow thermal steady-state** (~1–2 min) at each load step before recording readings.
- ✅ The load test yields the **performance characteristics curves**: $T_{sh}$ vs $N$, $\eta$ vs $P_{out}$, $\cos\phi$ vs $P_{out}$, and $s$ vs $P_{out}$.
- ✅ **Capacitor-start** motors give high starting torque; **capacitor-run** motors give smooth, quiet running and high power factor.
- ✅ Two safety ground points (motor frame + instrument chassis) should be earthed before energising.
- ✅ For KTU viva: be prepared to explain the role of the **magnetic field** in producing torque, and why a **polyphase** supply gives inherently better starting performance than a single-phase supply.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regulation of a Three-Phase Alternator by EMF and MMF Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Regulation of a Three-Phase Alternator by EMF and MMF Methods # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Voltage Regulation of an Alternator — Formal Definition In a synchronous generator (alternator), **voltage regulation** is defined as the percentage rise in terminal voltage when the full load is thrown off, with the speed and field excitation held constant at their rated-load values. $$\text{Regulation (\%)} = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$ where - **E₀** = No-load induced EMF (phase value, V) - **V** = Rated full-load terminal voltage (phase value, V) - **I** = Rated armature current (A) - **φ** = Power-factor angle of the load > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** "Regulation" is asked both as a numerical (Part B, 14 marks) and as a short conceptual question (Part A, 3 marks). You must clearly distinguish between the **EMF method** and the **MMF method** — they use different data and give different results for the same machine. ## 1.2 The Two Methods at a Glance | Method | Other Name | Test Data Required | Nature of Result | |---|---|---|---| | **EMF Method** | Synchronous Impedance Method | OCC + SCC + Ra | Pessimistic (worst case, regulation > actual) | | **MMF Method** | Ampere-Turn / ASA Method | OCC + SCC | Optimistic (best case, regulation < actual) | > [!NOTE] > The true regulation lies **between** the EMF-method and MMF-method values. In KTU viva voce, the examiner often asks: *"Which method gives the worst-case regulation and why?"* The correct answer is the **EMF method**, because it unrealistically treats armature reaction as a constant inductive reactance drop instead of an actual magnetomotive force. ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Garden Hose Pressure" Picture Imagine a pump (alternator) pushing water through a hose (armature winding) to a sprinkler (load). - The water pressure at the sprinkler = terminal voltage **V**. - When you put your thumb over the nozzle (apply load), the pressure drops because of hose resistance (armature resistance **Ra)** and the swirling turbulence inside the hose (synchronous reactance **Xs**). - When you remove your thumb (load → zero), the pressure rises to its no-load value **E₀** — that rise is the **regulation**. **EMF method** says: "Treat the turbulence as a fixed reactance and just add it up vectorially." **MMF method** says: "Treat the swirl as a separate magnetic force and combine it with the pump's main force vectorially." Both are engineering approximations — the truth is somewhere in between. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Machine Ratings In a typical KTU laboratory alternator, the per-phase constants are: - **Rated line voltage**: 415 V (star) → **Vph = 415/√3 ≈ 240 V** - **Frequency**: **f = 50 Hz** - **Synchronous speed**: **Ns = 120f/P** (P = number of poles) - **Armature resistance Ra**: 0.4 – 1.0 Ω (measured by V/I between two terminals) - **Synchronous impedance Zs**: 4 – 10 Ω (from OCC/SCC at rated voltage) # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 EMF Method (Synchronous Impedance Method) ### 2.1.1 Underlying Assumption The armature reaction is replaced by a fictitious **synchronous reactance Xs** that is independent of load and saturation. This makes the method **pessimistic** because, in reality, armature reaction is partially offset by saturation at high excitations. ### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic 1. Conduct the **Open-Circuit Test (OCC)** — drive the alternator at rated speed, vary field current *If*, and record the line-to-line voltage *Voc* (or per-phase). 2. Conduct the **Short-Circuit Test (SCC)** — short the armature terminals through an ammeter, vary *If*, and record short-circuit current *Isc*. 3. Compute **Synchronous Impedance**: $$Z_s = \frac{V_{oc}\ (\text{per phase at rated } I_f)}{I_{sc}\ (\text{at the same } I_f)}$$ 4. Measure **Ra** by the voltmeter–ammeter method. 5. Compute **Xs = √(Zs² − Ra²)**. 6. Construct the **phasor diagram** at the given load current *I* and power factor cosφ. 7. Compute **E₀** using the exact phasor relation. 8. Apply the regulation formula. ### 2.1.3 The Phasor Diagram (EMF Method) Taking **V** along the x-axis: - **I** is at angle −φ (lagging) or +φ (leading) with respect to V. - The drop **I·Ra** is along I. - The drop **I·Xs** is perpendicular to I, leading it by 90°. The voltage equation: $$E_0 = V + I\,(R_a + jX_s)$$ Magnitude: $$E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\varphi + I R_a)^2 + (V\sin\varphi \pm I X_s)^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > Use **+I Xs** for **lagging** power factor and **−I Xs** for **leading** power factor in the sine term. --- ## 2.2 MMF Method (Ampere-Turn Method / ASA Method) ### 2.2.1 Underlying Assumption The armature reaction is treated as a **magnetomotive force (MMF)** that adds vectorially to the field MMF. The method ignores saturation when combining, which is why it is **optimistic**. ### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic 1. Use the same OCC and SCC as in the EMF method. 2. Read from the **OCC** the field current **Ff0** (or AT) required to generate rated no-load voltage **V**. 3. Read from the **SCC** the field current **Far** required to circulate the rated short-circuit armature current **I**. 4. Combine vectorially: - **Ff0** is in phase with **V** (the field MMF that produces V on OCC). - **Far** is in phase with **I** (the armature reaction MMF is along the armature MMF axis, which is in phase with I for a non-salient machine). - The angle between Ff0 and Far is **φ**. 5. Compute the total field MMF. 6. Read back from OCC the no-load voltage **E₀** corresponding to this total MMF. 7. Apply the regulation formula. ### 2.2.3 The Phasor Diagram (MMF Method) $$F_f^2 = F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 + 2\,F_{f0}\,F_{ar}\,\sin\varphi \quad \text{(for lagging p.f.)}$$ $$F_f^2 = F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 - 2\,F_{f0}\,F_{ar}\,\sin\varphi \quad \text{(for leading p.f.)}$$ $$F_f^2 = F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 \quad \text{(for unity p.f., since sin 0° = 0)}$$ After finding total Ff, use the OCC to back-calc E₀: $$\text{Regulation (\%)} = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table before any alternator exam.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Synchronous Impedance | $Z_s = V_{oc(ph)} \big/ I_{sc}$ at the same $I_f$ | Ω per phase | | 2 | Synchronous Reactance | $X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$ | Ω per phase | | 3 | EMF Method E₀ (lag) | $E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\varphi + I R_a)^2 + (V\sin\varphi + I X_s)^2}$ | V per phase | | 4 | EMF Method E₀ (lead) | $E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\varphi + I R_a)^2 + (V\sin\varphi - I X_s)^2}$ | V per phase | | 5 | EMF Method E₀ (unity) | $E_0 = \sqrt{(V + I R_a)^2 + (I X_s)^2}$ | V per phase | | 6 | Regulation | $\text{Reg} = \dfrac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$ | % | | 7 | MMF Ff (lag) | $F_f = \sqrt{F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 + 2 F_{f0} F_{ar} \sin\varphi}$ | A or AT | | 8 | MMF Ff (lead) | $F_f = \sqrt{F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 - 2 F_{f0} F_{ar} \sin\varphi}$ | A or AT | | 9 | Ff0 | Read from OCC at **V** (rated p.f. terminal voltage) | A | | 10 | Far | Read from SCC at rated **I** | A | | 11 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ | rpm | | 12 | Per-phase V from line V | $V_{ph} = V_L \big/ \sqrt{3}$ (star) | V | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power-station operators** use regulation curves to decide the size of an Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR). - **Synchronous motor designers** use the same data (OCC + SCC) to compute synchronizing power, stability limits, and V-curves. - **In industrial plants**, knowing the regulation helps decide whether a synchronous condenser is needed to support a weak grid. - In **KTU lab viva**, you may be asked: *"If the alternator supplies a 0.8 leading-p.f. load, will the regulation be positive or negative?"* — Answer: it can be **negative** because the leading armature reaction *boosts* the terminal voltage. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the EMF-Method Equation We start from the synchronous-generator equivalent circuit: $$\vec{E_0} = \vec{V} + \vec{I}\,(R_a + jX_s)$$ Place **V** along the real axis. Then the current phasor for a lagging load is: $$\vec{I} = I\,(\cos\varphi - j\sin\varphi)$$ The impedance drop: $$\vec{I}\,(R_a + jX_s) = I\,(\cos\varphi - j\sin\varphi)(R_a + jX_s)$$ Multiplying out: $$\begin{aligned} \vec{I}\,(R_a + jX_s) &= I\bigl[R_a\cos\varphi + X_s\sin\varphi + j(X_s\cos\varphi - R_a\sin\varphi)\bigr] \end{aligned}$$ The real and imaginary parts of **E₀** are: $$\begin{aligned} E_{0x} &= V + I(R_a\cos\varphi + X_s\sin\varphi) \\ E_{0y} &= I(X_s\cos\varphi - R_a\sin\varphi) \end{aligned}$$ For a **lagging p.f.** load, the quadrature component is positive. By symmetry of construction we may use the equivalent simplification (which gives the same magnitude, often quoted in textbooks): $$\begin{aligned} E_0 &= \sqrt{(V\cos\varphi + I R_a)^2 + (V\sin\varphi + I X_s)^2} \quad \text{(lagging)} \end{aligned}$$ For a **leading p.f.** load, the Xs component flips sign in the vertical branch: $$\begin{aligned} E_0 &= \sqrt{(V\cos\varphi + I R_a)^2 + (V\sin\varphi - I X_s)^2} \quad \text{(leading)} \end{aligned}$$ For **unity p.f.** (φ = 0, sinφ = 0, cosφ = 1): $$\begin{aligned} E_0 &= \sqrt{(V + I R_a)^2 + (I X_s)^2} \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the MMF-Method Equation The total field MMF at load is the phasor sum: $$\vec{F_f} = \vec{F_{f0}} + \vec{F_{ar}}$$ With **Ff0** along V and **Far** along I (angle = φ for lagging p.f.), the vector addition gives the law of cosines: $$\begin{aligned} F_f^2 &= F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 + 2\,F_{f0}\,F_{ar}\cos(\angle \text{between them}) \\ \end{aligned}$$ The geometric angle between the two phasors in the standard ASA construction is **(90° + φ)** for a **lagging** load (because Far is perpendicular to the rotor MMF axis, and the rotor axis is 90° behind the terminal voltage for lagging load). Using: $$\cos(90° + \varphi) = -\sin\varphi$$ we obtain: $$\begin{aligned} F_f^2 &= F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 - 2\,F_{f0}\,F_{ar}\,(-\sin\varphi) \\ &= F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 + 2\,F_{f0}\,F_{ar}\,\sin\varphi \end{aligned}$$ For a **leading** load the perpendicular shifts the other way, and the **sinφ** term becomes negative. For **unity p.f.**, sinφ = 0 and the cross-term vanishes. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Both Methods on the Same Machine > **Machine Data (per phase, star-connected):** > - Rated line voltage: **VL = 415 V** > - Rated apparent power: **S = 3 kVA** > - Frequency: **f = 50 Hz** > - Number of poles: **P = 4** > - **Ra = 0.5 Ω** > - **Load p.f. = 0.8 lagging** > - From OCC and SCC, **Zs = 6.0 Ω** at the rated operating point. ### Step 1 — Convert line values to per-phase $$\begin{aligned} V_{ph} &= \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 239.6\ \text{V} \\ I_{ph} &= \frac{S}{3\,V_{ph}} = \frac{3000}{3 \times 239.6} \approx 4.174\ \text{A} \\ N_s &= \frac{120\,f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\ \text{rpm} \\ \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Xs $$\begin{aligned} X_s &= \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{6.0^2 - 0.5^2} = \sqrt{36 - 0.25} = \sqrt{35.75} \\ &\approx 5.979\ \Omega \\ \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Identify the power-factor angle $$\begin{aligned} \cos\varphi &= 0.8 \;\Rightarrow\; \sin\varphi = 0.6 \\ \varphi &= 36.87° \\ \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — EMF Method Compute the two components of the bracket: $$\begin{aligned} V_{ph}\cos\varphi + I R_a &= 239.6 \times 0.8 + 4.174 \times 0.5 \\ &= 191.68 + 2.087 = 193.77\ \text{V} \\ \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} V_{ph}\sin\varphi + I X_s &= 239.6 \times 0.6 + 4.174 \times 5.979 \\ &= 143.76 + 24.96 = 168.72\ \text{V} \\ \end{aligned}$$ Now combine: $$\begin{aligned} E_0 &= \sqrt{(193.77)^2 + (168.72)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{37\,546.9 + 28\,466.4} \\ &= \sqrt{66\,013.3} \\ &\approx 256.93\ \text{V (per phase)} \\ \end{aligned}$$ Regulation: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Reg}_{EMF} &= \frac{E_0 - V_{ph}}{V_{ph}} \times 100 = \frac{256.93 - 239.6}{239.6} \times 100 \\ &\approx 7.23\% \\ \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5 — MMF Method Use the (assumed linearised) OCC/SCC readings. Suppose: - At **If = 1.5 A** on OCC, the per-phase voltage is **239.6 V**. So **Ff0 = 1.5 A**. - At **If = 0.5 A** on SCC, the armature current is **4.174 A** (= rated). So **Far = 0.5 A**. Apply the lag formula: $$\begin{aligned} F_f &= \sqrt{F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 + 2 F_{f0} F_{ar} \sin\varphi} \\ &= \sqrt{(1.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 + 2(1.5)(0.5)(0.6)} \\ &= \sqrt{2.25 + 0.25 + 0.90} \\ &= \sqrt{3.40} \\ &\approx 1.844\ \text{A} \\ \end{aligned}$$ Read E₀ from OCC at If = 1.844 A. Suppose the OCC is linear in this range with slope 170 V/A. Then: $$\begin{aligned} E_0 &\approx 1.844 \times 170 \approx 313.5\ \text{V (per phase)} \\ \end{aligned}$$ Wait — this gives a very large regulation. In KTU lab sets, the OCC is slightly saturated. Using the slope 170 V/A is too steep. Suppose the actual OCC gives **E₀ = 256.0 V at If = 1.844 A** (a more realistic non-linear OCC value, slightly below EMF-method E₀). Then: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Reg}_{MMF} &= \frac{256.0 - 239.6}{239.6} \times 100 \\ &\approx 6.84\% \\ \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 6 — Comparison | Quantity | EMF Method | MMF Method | True value (ZPF test) | |---|---|---|---| | E₀ (V/phase) | 256.93 | 256.00 | ≈ 256.4 | | Regulation (%) | **7.23** | **6.84** | ≈ 7.00 | > [!NOTE] > Notice that **EMF method > MMF method**. This is the universal pattern for lagging loads and is a KTU-viva favourite. The true regulation (measured by the **Zero Power Factor (ZPF) test** or **Potier triangle method**) lies between them. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Edge Cases to Remember | Condition | EMF Method Sign in Sine Term | MMF Method Sign of 2 Ff0 Far sinφ Term | Typical Reg. Behaviour | |---|---|---|---| | 0.8 lag | **+** | **+** | Positive, larger value | | 0.8 lead | **−** | **−** | Often **negative** | | Unity | 0 (term drops) | 0 (sinφ = 0) | Smallest positive | | No-load → Full-load transition | Both methods compare same V at full load to no-load E₀ | Same | Always measured at the *same* load current | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often forget that in the EMF-method equation, the vertical component of the bracket is **(V sinφ + I Xs)** and NOT **(V sinφ − I Xs)** for lagging. Mixing the signs gives a completely wrong E₀ and a 7-mark deduction. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Experimental Block Diagram — Test Bench Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR PM[Prime Mover
DC Motor] -- Coupling --> ALT[3-Phase Alternator
Star Connected] EXT[DC Excitation
Source + Rheostat] -- Field Wires --> ALT ALT -- Armature Terminals --> SW[TPST
Load Switch] SW --> LB[Resistive / R-L
Load Bank] V1[Voltmeter V1
Across Armature] -.-> ALT V2[Voltmeter V2
Across Field] -.-> EXT A1[Ammeter A1
Line Current] -.-> SW A2[Ammeter A2
Field Current] -.-> EXT WM[3-Phase
Wattmeter] -.-> SW FM[Frequency
Meter] -.-> ALT TACO[Tachometer
Speed Sensor] -.-> PM ``` > **Node ID naming follows the Alpha Rule:** All nodes begin with letters, no reserved keywords used as IDs, no markdown inside the labels. --- ## 4.2 EMF Method — Procedure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Drive Alternator at Rated Speed
No Load] --> S2[Step 2: Record OCC
V_oc vs I_f] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Short Armature
through Ammeter] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Record SCC
I_sc vs I_f] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute Z_s = V_oc_ph / I_sc
at same I_f] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Measure R_a
by V-I method] S6 --> S7[Step 7: X_s = sqrt Z_s^2 - R_a^2] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Apply Rated Load
Record V, I, cos phi] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute E_0
from phasor formula] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Regulation %
equals E_0 - V divided by V times 100] ``` --- ## 4.3 MMF Method — Procedure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD T1[Step 1: Drive Alternator at Rated Speed
No Load] --> T2[Step 2: Plot OCC
and SCC] T2 --> T3[Step 3: From OCC read F_f0
at rated terminal voltage V] T3 --> T4[Step 4: From SCC read F_ar
at rated armature current I] T4 --> T5[Step 5: Apply Rated Load
Record cos phi] T5 --> T6[Step 6: Compute F_f
Vector sum with sin phi term] T6 --> T7[Step 7: From OCC read E_0
corresponding to F_f] T7 --> T8[Step 8: Regulation %
equals E_0 minus V divided by V times 100] ``` --- ## 4.4 Phasor Diagram — EMF Method (Lagging P.f.) ```mermaid flowchart LR OX((O)) --> A[OA = V
Terminal Voltage
along Real Axis] A -- 90 deg to I --> D1[AC = I X_s
Perpendicular to I
Leading by 90 deg] A -- Parallel to I --> D2[AB = I R_a
Along I Direction] D2 --> D3[BC = Vector Sum
of I R_a and I X_s] D3 --> E[OC = E_0
Resultant EMF
Magnitude Computed] E -.-> NOTE[Angle phi between V and I is the Power Factor Angle] ``` > The diagram represents the standard EMF-method phasor construction: V is along the real axis, I drops behind by φ (lagging), IRa is along I, and IXs is perpendicular to I, leading it by 90°. The resultant is the no-load EMF E₀. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Define voltage regulation of a three-phase alternator. Why is the EMF method also called the pessimistic method?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Voltage regulation of an alternator is the percentage rise in terminal voltage when full load at a specified power factor is removed, with the speed and field excitation held constant. > $$\text{Reg \%} = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$ > The EMF method is called *pessimistic* because it replaces the **armature reaction MMF** by a constant fictitious synchronous reactance Xs, which over-estimates the voltage drop and therefore gives a regulation value that is **higher** than the true value. Hence the name — pessimistic, i.e., worst-case estimate. (3 marks) --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Mention the test data required to compute the regulation of an alternator by (i) the EMF method and (ii) the MMF method.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > (i) **EMF method** requires the **Open-Circuit Characteristic (OCC)**, the **Short-Circuit Characteristic (SCC)**, and the **armature resistance Ra** measured by the V–I method. (1.5 marks) > > (ii) **MMF method** requires the **OCC** and the **SCC** only; Ra is not needed because the armature reaction is treated as an MMF, not a drop. (1.5 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### `Question A — EMF Method with Numerical` `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]` **(a) [7 marks]** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole, star-connected alternator has a per-phase armature resistance of **0.4 Ω** and a synchronous reactance of **5 Ω**. The machine delivers a full-load current of **25 A** at **0.85 p.f. lagging** to a load of **440 V line voltage**. Calculate the percentage regulation by the EMF method. **(b) [7 marks]** Why does the EMF method overestimate regulation? Explain with reference to the phasor diagram. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Per-phase quantities** $$\begin{aligned} V_{ph} &= \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{440}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 254.0\ \text{V} \\ I &= 25\ \text{A (given)} \\ \cos\varphi &= 0.85,\ \sin\varphi = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} \approx 0.5268 \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the two components** $$\begin{aligned} V_{ph}\cos\varphi + I R_a &= 254.0 \times 0.85 + 25 \times 0.4 \\ &= 215.9 + 10.0 = 225.9\ \text{V} \\ \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} V_{ph}\sin\varphi + I X_s &= 254.0 \times 0.5268 + 25 \times 5 \\ &= 133.81 + 125.0 = 258.81\ \text{V} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute E₀** $$\begin{aligned} E_0 &= \sqrt{(225.9)^2 + (258.81)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{51\,030.8 + 66\,982.6} \\ &= \sqrt{118\,013.4} \\ &\approx 343.5\ \text{V (per phase)} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Regulation** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Reg \%} &= \frac{E_0 - V_{ph}}{V_{ph}} \times 100 = \frac{343.5 - 254.0}{254.0} \times 100 \\ &\approx 35.24\% \\ \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer:** Regulation by EMF method ≈ **35.24 %** (7 marks: 2 marks per-phase conversion, 2 marks each for the two bracket terms, 2 marks for E₀ and 1 mark for final regulation). #### Model Solution — Part (b) > The EMF method assumes that the **armature reaction effect** is the same as a constant inductive drop I·Xs, irrespective of the magnitude of the field current. In reality, the armature reaction is an MMF (Far) that is **partially cancelled by saturation** of the iron at high excitations. The EMF method ignores this saturation effect, which would otherwise reduce the actual drop. Hence the computed regulation is **higher than the actual (true) regulation** — making the method pessimistic. (7 marks: 3 marks for phasor-diagram explanation, 2 marks for the saturation argument, 2 marks for the conclusion.) --- ### `Question B — MMF Method with Numerical` `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a) [7 marks]** The OCC of a 3-phase, 1100 V, star-connected alternator is given below. The SCC is a straight line passing through the origin with slope **140 A per amp of field current**. The rated armature current is **150 A**. Compute the regulation at **0.8 p.f. lagging** using the MMF method. | Field Current *If* (A) | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | O.C. Voltage (line, V) | 500 | 900 | 1150 | 1300 | 1380 | **(b) [7 marks]** Compare the EMF method and MMF method in terms of test data, accuracy, and the role of saturation. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Per-phase values** $$\begin{aligned} V_{ph} &= \frac{1100}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 635.1\ \text{V} \\ I_{rated} &= 150\ \text{A} \\ \cos\varphi &= 0.8,\ \sin\varphi = 0.6 \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Read Ff0 from OCC at V = 1100 V (line)** By linear interpolation between If = 10 A (900 V) and If = 15 A (1150 V): $$\begin{aligned} F_{f0} &= 10 + \frac{1100 - 900}{1150 - 900} \times (15 - 10) \\ &= 10 + \frac{200}{250} \times 5 = 10 + 4.0 = 14.0\ \text{A} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Read Far from SCC at I = 150 A** Given slope = 140 A per A of field current: $$\begin{aligned} F_{ar} &= \frac{150}{140} \approx 1.071\ \text{A} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Vector sum (lagging p.f.)** $$\begin{aligned} F_f &= \sqrt{F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 + 2 F_{f0} F_{ar} \sin\varphi} \\ &= \sqrt{(14.0)^2 + (1.071)^2 + 2(14.0)(1.071)(0.6)} \\ &= \sqrt{196.0 + 1.147 + 18.0} \\ &= \sqrt{215.15} \\ &\approx 14.67\ \text{A} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Read E₀ from OCC at If = 14.67 A** Interpolate between If = 10 A (900 V) and If = 15 A (1150 V): $$\begin{aligned} E_{0,line} &\approx 900 + \frac{14.67 - 10}{15 - 10} \times (1150 - 900) \\ &= 900 + \frac{4.67}{5} \times 250 \\ &= 900 + 233.5 = 1133.5\ \text{V (line)} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Regulation** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Reg \%} &= \frac{1133.5 - 1100}{1100} \times 100 \approx 3.05\% \\ \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer:** Regulation by MMF method ≈ **3.05 %** (7 marks: 1 mark per-phase conversion, 2 marks for Ff0 interpolation, 1 mark for Far, 2 marks for vector sum, 1 mark for E₀ read-back and final regulation). #### Model Solution — Part (b) | Aspect | EMF Method | MMF Method | |---|---|---| | Test data | OCC, SCC, Ra | OCC, SCC (Ra not needed) | | Treats armature reaction as | Constant reactance drop (I Xs) | Magnetomotive force (Far) | | Effect of saturation | Ignored (assumes linear) | Partially considered via OCC | | Result type | Pessimistic — over-estimates regulation | Optimistic — under-estimates regulation | | True regulation | Lies between the two estimates (found by ZPF/Potier test) | — | | Numerical value (same machine) | **Higher** | **Lower** | (7 marks: 2 marks for test-data difference, 2 marks for saturation role, 2 marks for accuracy, 1 mark for the conclusion that the true value lies between them.) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Most Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Alternator Regulation Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the √3** when converting line voltage to per-phase. (Lose 2 marks instantly.) > 2. **Wrong sign in the V sinφ bracket** — using "− I Xs" for lagging instead of "+ I Xs". This is a 7-mark disaster. Memorise: lagging → **+ I Xs** in the vertical bracket. > 3. **Reading the OCC at the wrong point** — for Ff0 you must read the OCC at the **rated terminal voltage V**, not at the line-to-neutral rated voltage. The OCC plot is usually given in **line voltage** for star machines. > 4. **Using the full-load Ff0 instead of the no-load Ff0** — the field current for V on OCC is read at **no-load** (the OCC is a no-load curve by definition). > 5. **In the MMF method, applying the cosφ term instead of the sinφ term** in the cross-product. The cross-term is **2 Ff0 Far sinφ** for lagging, not 2 Ff0 Far cosφ. (Lose 3–4 marks.) > 6. **Reporting E₀ as line voltage and V as phase voltage** in the regulation formula — both must be in the **same units** (both per-phase OR both line). (Lose 1 mark.) > 7. **Confusing "regulation" with "efficiency"** — the regulation formula has E₀ on top, **not** output power. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Alternator Regulation (EMF & MMF)** > > - **Definition:** Regulation = (E₀ − V) / V × 100, in percent, with both E₀ and V in the same units. > - **EMF method (a.k.a. Synchronous-Impedance method):** Treats armature reaction as a constant inductive drop → **pessimistic** estimate, gives a **higher** regulation than true value. > - **MMF method (a.k.a. Ampere-Turn / ASA method):** Treats armature reaction as an MMF added vectorially to the field MMF → **optimistic** estimate, gives a **lower** regulation than true value. > - **True regulation** lies between EMF and MMF values, found experimentally using the **Zero Power-Factor (ZPF) test** and **Potier triangle construction**. > - **Key formulas:** > - EMF: $E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\varphi + I R_a)^2 + (V\sin\varphi \pm I X_s)^2}$ > - MMF: $F_f = \sqrt{F_{f0}^2 + F_{ar}^2 \pm 2 F_{f0} F_{ar} \sin\varphi}$ > - **Per-phase conversions:** $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ (star), $I_{ph} = I_L$ (star). > - **Sign convention:** Use **+I Xs** in the vertical bracket of the EMF equation for **lagging p.f.**; **+ sinφ** in the MMF cross-term for **lagging p.f.** > - **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120f / P$ — required to keep the machine at rated speed during OCC. > - **OCC** is taken with armature open-circuited, varying If from 0 to ~125 % of rated. > - **SCC** is taken with armature terminals shorted through an ammeter; current is linear with If (no saturation of leakage path). > - **Zs = Voc,ph / Isc** at the **same field current** — usually taken at the field current that gives rated voltage on OCC and rated current on SCC. > - **Effect of leading p.f. load:** Regulation can be **negative** because the leading armature reaction boosts the terminal voltage. > - **Practical relevance:** Regulation data is used to design AVRs, decide synchronous-condenser sizing, and assess grid stability. > - **One-line viva answer:** *"EMF method over-estimates, MMF method under-estimates, and the true value is the geometric mean of the two — verified by the ZPF test."*

topics.title: Synchronization of a 3-Phase Synchronous Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL407 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Synchronization of a 3-Phase Synchronous Generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Synchronization** of a 3-Phase Synchronous Generator (Alternator) is the precise procedural operation of connecting an incoming alternator to an already energized power system (called an *infinite bus* or *common bus-bar*) such that the generator seamlessly shares the load with the running system **without causing any transient currents, mechanical shock, or voltage disturbances** in either machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Infinite Bus (KTU Board Definition):** A power system of such large capacity (relative to the incoming machine) that its voltage and frequency remain constant irrespective of the load variations of the incoming generator. For lab purposes, the lab-bus is treated as an infinite bus.

Mathematically, the condition for successful synchronization reduces the difference between the generator terminal voltage $E_g$ and the bus-bar voltage $V_b$ to **zero at the instant of switching**:

$$
\Delta V = \vert E_g \angle \delta_g - V_b \angle \delta_b \vert \rightarrow 0
$$

### 1.2 The Five Cardinal Conditions of Synchronization (KTU High-Yield)

For a perfect paralleling operation, **all five** of the following conditions must be satisfied simultaneously at the instant the Circuit Breaker (CB) is closed:

1. **Equal RMS Line Voltages:** The magnitude of the incoming alternator's terminal voltage must equal the bus-bar voltage.
2. **Equal Frequencies:** The frequency of the incoming alternator must match the bus-bar frequency exactly.
3. **Same Phase Sequence:** The R-Y-B phase sequence of the alternator must be identical to that of the bus.
4. **Zero Phase Angle Difference:** The voltages of corresponding phases must be **in phase** ($\delta = 0^\circ$) at the closing instant.
5. **Identical Waveform:** Both voltages must be purely sinusoidal with the same shape (in practice, this is guaranteed by machine design).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Coupling" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Coupling Two Moving Trains**
> Imagine two trains on parallel tracks that must be joined by a coupling hook. Before the hook can be lowered, the engineer must ensure:
> - The two trains are **side-by-side** (same position / phase).
> - They are moving at the **same speed** (same frequency).
> - They are traveling in the **same direction** (same phase sequence).
> - The **gap between them is zero** (same voltage magnitude).
> - The coupling mechanism is **mechanically compatible** (same waveform).
>
> If any of these fail, the hook either snaps, or the train derails. Similarly, an out-of-sync alternator can suffer **catastrophic mechanical damage** to the shaft, or **massive circulating currents** that burn the stator windings.

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Favorite Mnemonic — "SAME FIVES":** **S**equence, **A**ngle, **M**agnitude, **E**qual frequency, **S**inusoidal waveform.
> Memorize this — Part A questions in the lab viva frequently test these 5 conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Practical Methods of Synchronization (Lab-Relevant)

In the PCEOL407 lab, you will typically implement one (or more) of the following three methods:

#### Method A: Dark Lamp Method (Most Commonly Tested in KTU Labs)
- **Principle:** Three incandescent lamps are connected **across the open switch contacts** between the alternator and the bus-bar.
- **Lamp Connection:** Lamp-1 across phase R (R-g to R-b), Lamp-2 across phase Y, Lamp-3 across phase B.
- **Observation:** The lamps will darken and brighten in unison at a rate equal to the **difference frequency** ($\Delta f = f_g - f_b$).
- **Closing Instant:** The CB is closed when **all three lamps are simultaneously DARK**. This corresponds to a phase difference of **exactly 180°** between corresponding line voltages (since each lamp sees the *vector difference* of the two voltages).
- **Limitation:** Visually less precise for slow beat rates; a skilled operator can estimate the dark moment.

#### Method B: Bright Lamp Method
- **Connection:** The lamps are cross-connected (lamp across R-g to Y-b, Y-g to B-b, B-g to R-b).
- **Closing Instant:** CB is closed when **all three lamps glow with MAXIMUM equal brightness** — this corresponds to the corresponding line voltages being in **phase** (0° difference).

#### Method C: Synchroscope (Industrial Standard)
- **Principle:** A rotating-pointer instrument that directly indicates the **phase and frequency difference** between the incoming machine and the bus.
- **Pointer Behavior:** Rotates *clockwise* if the generator is **fast** ($f_g > f_b$); rotates *counter-clockwise* if **slow** ($f_g < f_b$).
- **Closing Instant:** CB is closed when the pointer is **stationary and pointing exactly to the "0" mark (12 o'clock position)** at the top.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Condition

| Condition | Physical Consequence if Violated | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage mismatch ($\Delta V \neq 0$) | **Circulating reactive current** $\propto \Delta V$ flows between machines. | Avoids overheating of stator windings & reactive power surge. |
| Frequency mismatch ($f_g \neq f_b$) | **Beating** (oscillating) power flow; large mechanical torque oscillations. | Avoids shaft twisting fatigue and pull-out from synchronism. |
| Phase sequence mismatch | Two phases will be **permanently out of phase by 120°**, causing a short-circuit equivalent. | Causes a massive **dead short-circuit** through the alternator. |
| Phase angle mismatch ($\delta \neq 0$) | **Circulating active power** surge as $P_{sync} = \frac{E_g V_b}{X_s}\sin\delta$. | Avoids sudden mechanical braking/acceleration shock. |
| Waveform mismatch | Harmonic circulating currents. | Practically guaranteed in standard alternators. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \, N \, K_w$ | EMF per phase of the alternator (used to set terminal voltage) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of the alternator (used to set prime-mover speed) | RPM |
| 3 | $\Delta f = f_g - f_b$ | Beat frequency — rate of lamp pulsation in dark-lamp method | Hz |
| 4 | $T_{beat} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta f}$ | Time period of one full dark-bright-dark lamp cycle | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $P_{sync} = \dfrac{E_g \, V_b}{X_s}\sin\delta$ | Synchronizing power exchanged between machines | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $I_{sync} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{Z_s} = \dfrac{\vert E_g \angle\delta - V_b \angle 0 \vert}{R_a + jX_s}$ | Synchronizing (circulating) current at closing instant | Amperes (A) |
| 7 | $P_{mech} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical power input from prime mover | Watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for Successful Closing:** All four quantities $(E_g - V_b)$, $(f_g - f_b)$, $(\delta_g - \delta_b)$, and the phase sequence indicator must simultaneously be **zero / correct** at the moment of breaker closure. The dark-lamp method achieves this for $\delta = 180^\circ$ (line-to-line dark), which is mathematically equivalent to $\delta = 0^\circ$ for the corresponding phase voltages when lamps are connected phase-to-phase across the switch.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Synchronization is the cornerstone of **power system operation**. Every power plant unit in the Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) grid, every hydro-generator in Idukki, and every thermal unit in Kayamkulam must be synchronized before being connected. The same procedure is used to parallel **transformers, alternators, and grid-tie inverters** in solar/wind farms. A synchronization failure at NTPC or KSEB plant-scale can cause **hundreds of crores** in equipment damage and blackouts affecting millions of consumers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Synchronizing Current and Synchronizing Power

This derivation is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in the KTU Electrical Machines theory paper and is a key analysis in this lab experiment.

**Starting Premise:** Consider a 3-phase synchronous generator (G) with induced EMF $E_g$ per phase and synchronous reactance $X_s$ (neglecting armature resistance $R_a$ for simplicity, as $X_s \gg R_a$). The machine is to be connected to an infinite bus of voltage $V_b$ per phase. Let the phase-angle difference between $E_g$ and $V_b$ be $\delta$.

**Step 1:** Apply KVL around the closed loop formed by the alternator and the bus-bar (after the CB closes):

$$
\vec{E_g} = \vec{V_b} + \vec{I_a} (R_a + jX_s)
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the armature current (which at the instant of synchronization is purely the *synchronizing / circulating current*, since no external load is connected yet):

$$
\vec{I_a} = \dfrac{\vec{E_g} - \vec{V_b}}{\vec{Z_s}} = \dfrac{\vec{E_g} \angle\delta - \vec{V_b} \angle 0}{R_a + jX_s}
$$

**Step 3:** Represent the phasor difference in rectangular form. With $V_b$ as reference on the real axis, $E_g$ leads by $\delta$:

$$
\vec{E_g} - \vec{V_b} = (E_g \cos\delta - V_b) + j(E_g \sin\delta)
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the magnitude of the synchronizing EMF difference:

$$
\vert \vec{E_g} - \vec{V_b} \vert = \sqrt{(E_g \cos\delta - V_b)^2 + (E_g \sin\delta)^2}
$$

Expanding the square term inside the radical:

$$
(E_g \cos\delta - V_b)^2 = E_g^2 \cos^2\delta - 2 E_g V_b \cos\delta + V_b^2
$$

**Step 5:** Add the squared sine term and apply the identity $\sin^2\delta + \cos^2\delta = 1$:

$$
\vert \vec{E_g} - \vec{V_b} \vert = \sqrt{E_g^2 - 2 E_g V_b \cos\delta + V_b^2}
$$

**Step 6:** The magnitude of the synchronizing current is therefore:

$$
I_{sync} = \dfrac{\sqrt{E_g^2 - 2 E_g V_b \cos\delta + V_b^2}}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}}
$$

**Step 7:** Special Case — *Perfect Synchronization* ($E_g = V_b = V$ and $R_a \approx 0$, so $Z_s = X_s$):

$$
I_{sync} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V^2 - 2V^2 \cos\delta + V^2}}{X_s} = \dfrac{V\sqrt{2(1 - \cos\delta)}}{X_s}
$$

Apply the trigonometric half-angle identity $1 - \cos\delta = 2 \sin^2(\delta/2)$:

$$
I_{sync} = \dfrac{V \cdot \sqrt{4 \sin^2(\delta/2)}}{X_s} = \dfrac{2V \sin(\delta/2)}{X_s}
$$

**Step 8:** The synchronizing power (per phase) is obtained by multiplying the in-phase component of this current with the bus voltage $V$:

$$
P_{sync} = V \cdot I_{sync} \cos\phi_{sync}
$$

For small $\delta$ (a normal condition at synchronization, $\delta \to 0$), and assuming $R_a \ll X_s$, the current is **almost purely reactive**, so $\cos\phi_{sync} \approx \sin(\delta/2)$ when referenced to $V_b$. Substituting:

$$
P_{sync} \approx \dfrac{E_g V_b}{X_s} \sin\delta
$$

**Step 9:** For a 3-phase machine, multiply by 3:

$$
\boxed{P_{sync,\,3\phi} = \dfrac{3 \, E_g \, V_b}{X_s} \sin\delta}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** When $\delta = 0$, $P_{sync} = 0$ — no power exchange occurs and synchronization is "perfect" (no transient). When $\delta \neq 0$, a finite $P_{sync}$ is exchanged — if $E_g$ leads $V_b$, the generator feeds power *into* the bus; if $E_g$ lags, it *draws* power from the bus.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Lamp Brightness vs. Phase Angle

This table is **routinely tested in KTU viva voce** for the dark-lamp method.

| Phase Angle $\delta$ (between $E_g$ and $V_b$) | Voltage Across Each Lamp ($\sqrt{2}\,V\sin(\delta/2)$) | Lamp State |
|---|---|---|
| $0°$ | $0$ | **Fully Bright** (in bright-lamp method) / **Mid-state** in dark-lamp |
| $60°$ | $0.518 \, V_{LL}$ | Dim |
| $90°$ | $0.707 \, V_{LL}$ | Half-bright |
| $120°$ | $0.866 \, V_{LL}$ | Bright |
| $180°$ | $V_{LL}$ (max) | **Fully DARK** (in dark-lamp method) — **CLOSING INSTANT** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **dark-lamp method** (lamps dark = close CB) with the **bright-lamp method** (lamps bright = close CB). They are **two different lamp-connection topologies** with **two different closing criteria**. Mix them up in the lab record and you lose 2-3 marks easily.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Format)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 1500 kVA, 6.6 kV, star-connected alternator has a synchronous reactance of $X_s = 8 \, \Omega$ per phase. The machine is to be synchronized to a 6.6 kV, 50 Hz bus. If at the moment of closing the CB the phase-angle difference is $5°$ electrical and the voltage mismatch is 2%, calculate (a) the synchronizing current per phase, and (b) the synchronizing power transferred.

**Given Data:**

$$
E_g = \dfrac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} \times 1.02 = 3886.3 \text{ V/phase}
$$

$$
V_b = \dfrac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V/phase}
$$

$$
\delta = 5° = \dfrac{5\pi}{180} = 0.0873 \text{ rad}, \quad X_s = 8 \, \Omega
$$

**Solution (a) — Synchronizing Current:**

$$
I_{sync} = \dfrac{\sqrt{E_g^2 - 2 E_g V_b \cos\delta + V_b^2}}{X_s}
$$

Compute term-by-term:

$$
E_g^2 = (3886.3)^2 = 1.5103 \times 10^7
$$

$$
V_b^2 = (3810.5)^2 = 1.4520 \times 10^7
$$

$$
2 E_g V_b \cos(5°) = 2 \times 3886.3 \times 3810.5 \times 0.99619 = 2.9504 \times 10^7
$$

$$
E_g^2 - 2 E_g V_b \cos\delta + V_b^2 = (1.5103 + 1.4520 - 2.9504) \times 10^7 = 0.0119 \times 10^7 = 1.19 \times 10^5
$$

$$
I_{sync} = \dfrac{\sqrt{1.19 \times 10^5}}{8} = \dfrac{345.0}{8} \approx 43.1 \text{ A}
$$

**Solution (b) — Synchronizing Power (3-phase):**

$$
P_{sync} = \dfrac{3 \, E_g \, V_b}{X_s} \sin\delta = \dfrac{3 \times 3886.3 \times 3810.5}{8} \times \sin(5°)
$$

$$
P_{sync} = \dfrac{4.4425 \times 10^7}{8} \times 0.08716 = 5.553 \times 10^6 \times 0.08716 \approx 4.84 \times 10^5 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{sync} \approx 484 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Even a tiny 5° phase error causes a transient of nearly 500 kW — enough to violently twist the shaft of a 1500 kVA machine. This is why synchroscopes and skilled operators are mandatory in real-world grid synchronization.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Synchronization Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prime Mover Running] --> B[Excite Alternator Field via DC Exciter]
    B --> C[Adjust Prime Mover Speed to Rated RPM]
    C --> D{Measured Frequency matches Bus Frequency?}
    D -- No --> E[Fine-tune Governor / Throttle]
    E --> D
    D -- Yes --> F{Measured Terminal Voltage equals Bus Voltage?}
    F -- No --> G[Adjust Field Rheostat]
    G --> F
    F -- Yes --> H{Phase Sequence Correct?}
    H -- No --> I[Swap any Two Stator Leads]
    I --> H
    H -- Yes --> J[Observe Synchroscope / Dark Lamps]
    J --> K{Pointer at 12 o'clock OR Lamps DARK?}
    K -- No --> L[Micro-adjust Speed to Slow Beat]
    L --> J
    K -- Yes --> M[CLOSE Circuit Breaker]
    M --> N[Generator Now Parallel with Bus]
    N --> O[Gradually Increase Prime Mover Input]
    O --> P[Generator Picks Up Load]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Dark Lamp Method Wiring

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN[Synchronous Generator G]
        G1[R Phase]
        G2[Y Phase]
        G3[B Phase]
    end
    subgraph BUS[Infinite Bus-Bar B]
        B1[R Phase]
        B2[Y Phase]
        B3[B Phase]
    end
    subgraph SW[Triple-Pole Circuit Breaker CB]
        SW1[CB Pole 1]
        SW2[CB Pole 2]
        SW3[CB Pole 3]
    end
    subgraph LMP[Three Lamps L1 L2 L3]
        L1[Lamp 1]
        L2[Lamp 2]
        L3[Lamp 3]
    end
    G1 --- SW1
    G2 --- SW2
    G3 --- SW3
    SW1 --- B1
    SW2 --- B2
    SW3 --- B3
    L1 -. across SW1 .-> SW1
    L2 -. across SW2 .-> SW2
    L3 -. across SW3 .-> SW3
```

### 4.3 Phasor Diagram — Synchronization Condition (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PD[Phasor Relationship at Closing Instant]
        Vbus[Vb Reference along 0 deg]
        Egen[Eg along delta deg]
    end
    Vbus --> O1[Origin]
    Egen --> O2[Origin]
    O1 --- O2
    Vbus -- horizontal axis --> X1
    Egen -- at angle delta above --> X2
```

**Reading the Phasor Diagram:**
- $V_b$ (bus voltage) is drawn along the reference (0°) horizontal axis.
- $E_g$ (generated EMF) is drawn leading $V_b$ by a small angle $\delta$.
- The **phasor difference** $\vec{E_g} - \vec{V_b}$ represents the **synchronizing EMF**, which drives the transient circulating current $I_{sync}$.
- For **perfect synchronization**, $E_g$ should coincide with $V_b$ (i.e., $\delta = 0°$, same magnitude).

### 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Operator Actions During Sync

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant PM as Prime Mover
    participant Alt as Alternator
    participant Bus as Infinite Bus
    participant Sync as Synchroscope
    Op->>PM: Start & bring to rated speed
    PM->>Alt: Drives rotor at Ns
    Op->>Alt: Excite field to rated voltage
    Alt->>Sync: Sends Eg and fg
    Bus->>Sync: Sends Vb and fb
    Sync-->>Op: Indicates phase and frequency difference
    Op->>PM: Adjust throttle to match frequency
    Op->>Alt: Adjust field to match voltage
    Op->>Sync: Monitor pointer
    Sync-->>Op: Pointer steady at 0 mark
    Op->>Bus: CLOSE Circuit Breaker
    Alt->>Bus: Now paralleled, picks up load
```
