## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define synchronization of a 3-phase synchronous generator. List the conditions to be satisfied before closing the circuit breaker.

**Model Answer:**
Synchronization is the process of connecting an incoming alternator in parallel with an already operating alternator or infinite bus-bar such that the machines operate in unison without any transient current or mechanical shock. The essential conditions are: (i) Same RMS terminal voltage, (ii) Same frequency, (iii) Same phase sequence (R-Y-B), (iv) Zero phase-angle difference between corresponding phases, and (v) Identical sinusoidal waveform.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2.** What is an "infinite bus"? Why is the lab-bus treated as an infinite bus in synchronization experiments?

**Model Answer:**
An infinite bus is a power system of such enormous capacity compared to the incoming machine that its voltage and frequency remain essentially constant regardless of the load drawn or supplied by the incoming generator. In lab experiments, the lab-bus is fed by a large mains transformer (typically several hundred kVA) which is many times larger than the experimental alternator (a few kVA), so its voltage and frequency are negligibly affected by the experimental machine — hence it is treated as an infinite bus.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model 14-Mark Pattern]**

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the dark-lamp method of synchronization of a 3-phase synchronous generator with a neat sketch. What are the conditions indicated when all three lamps are dark? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 1000 kVA, 3.3 kV, star-connected synchronous generator with $X_s = 5 \, \Omega$/phase is synchronized to a 3.3 kV, 50 Hz bus. If the voltage mismatch is 3% and the phase-angle difference is $4°$ at the moment of closing, compute: (i) the synchronizing current per phase, and (ii) the synchronizing power. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

In the **dark-lamp method**, three incandescent lamps are connected one across each pole of the triple-pole circuit breaker, i.e., between the generator-side terminal and the corresponding bus-side terminal. The lamps darken and brighten cyclically at a rate equal to the *difference frequency* $\Delta f = f_g - f_b$ between the alternator and the bus.

When all three lamps are **simultaneously dark**, the voltage across each lamp (which is the vector difference between the corresponding generator and bus line voltages) is zero. This corresponds to a phase difference of exactly **180°** between corresponding line voltages — or equivalently, a **0°** difference between corresponding phase voltages (when measured phase-to-neutral). The CB is closed at this exact moment to achieve successful synchronization.

The five required conditions — same voltage, same frequency, same phase sequence, same phase, and same waveform — are all confirmed by observing that the lamps darken and brighten *together* (sequence correct), *uniformly* (voltage matched), at a *slow controllable rate* (frequency matched), and *fully extinguish* (phase matched) at the closing instant.

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Given:** Star-connected, 3.3 kV (line), $X_s = 5 \, \Omega$/phase, $\delta = 4°$, 3% voltage mismatch.

**Per-phase voltages:**

$$
V_b = \dfrac{3300}{\sqrt{3}} = 1905.3 \text{ V}
$$

$$
E_g = V_b \times 1.03 = 1962.4 \text{ V}
$$

**Synchronizing current per phase:**

$$
I_{sync} = \dfrac{\sqrt{E_g^2 - 2 E_g V_b \cos\delta + V_b^2}}{X_s}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
E_g^2 = (1962.4)^2 = 3.851 \times 10^6
$$

$$
V_b^2 = (1905.3)^2 = 3.630 \times 10^6
$$

$$
2 E_g V_b \cos(4°) = 2 \times 1962.4 \times 1905.3 \times 0.99756 = 7.458 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\text{Numerator term} = (3.851 + 3.630 - 7.458) \times 10^6 = 0.023 \times 10^6 = 2.3 \times 10^4
$$

$$
I_{sync} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2.3 \times 10^4}}{5} = \dfrac{151.7}{5} \approx 30.3 \text{ A}
$$

**Synchronizing power (3-phase):**

$$
P_{sync} = \dfrac{3 \, E_g \, V_b}{X_s} \sin\delta = \dfrac{3 \times 1962.4 \times 1905.3}{5} \times \sin(4°)
$$

$$
P_{sync} = \dfrac{1.122 \times 10^7}{5} \times 0.06976 = 2.244 \times 10^6 \times 0.06976 \approx 1.565 \times 10^5 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{sync} \approx 156.5 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat phasor diagram, derive the expression for the synchronizing power of a 3-phase synchronous generator connected to an infinite bus. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a synchroscope. How does the operator interpret clockwise and counter-clockwise rotation of the pointer? What are the advantages of a synchroscope over the dark-lamp method? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

Consider a 3-phase synchronous generator with induced EMF $E_g$ per phase and synchronous impedance $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$ connected to an infinite bus of voltage $V_b$ per phase. Let the angle between $E_g$ and $V_b$ be $\delta$.

At the instant of closing the CB, the KVL gives:

$$
\vec{E_g} = \vec{V_b} + \vec{I_a}\vec{Z_s}
$$

Therefore the synchronizing current is:

$$
\vec{I_a} = \dfrac{\vec{E_g} - \vec{V_b}}{\vec{Z_s}}
$$

With $V_b$ as reference:

$$
\vec{E_g} - \vec{V_b} = (E_g\cos\delta - V_b) + j(E_g\sin\delta)
$$

The magnitude:

$$
\vert \vec{E_g} - \vec{V_b} \vert = \sqrt{E_g^2 - 2 E_g V_b \cos\delta + V_b^2}
$$

The synchronizing current magnitude:

$$
I_{sync} = \dfrac{\sqrt{E_g^2 - 2 E_g V_b \cos\delta + V_b^2}}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}}
$$

The synchronizing power per phase is the real power component transferred, which after simplifying (for $R_a \ll X_s$ and small $\delta$) reduces to:

$$
P_{sync,\,per\,phase} = \dfrac{E_g V_b}{X_s}\sin\delta
$$

For a 3-phase machine:

$$
\boxed{P_{sync,\,3\phi} = \dfrac{3 E_g V_b}{X_s}\sin\delta}
$$

**Assumptions:** (i) $R_a \ll X_s$ (so impedance is purely reactive), (ii) Negligible saturation, (iii) Salient-pole effects ignored (cylindrical rotor), (iv) Both voltages are balanced 3-phase sinusoids.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

A **synchroscope** is a specialized instrument that indicates *both* the phase-angle difference and the frequency difference between an incoming alternator and the bus-bar. Internally, it is essentially a split-phase induction-type or moving-iron instrument with two orthogonal coils — one energized by the bus voltage (reference) and another by the generator voltage (rotating field).

**Pointer Rotation Interpretation:**
- If the **pointer rotates clockwise**, the generator is running **fast** ($f_g > f_b$).
- If the pointer rotates **counter-clockwise**, the generator is **slow** ($f_g < f_b$).
- The **speed of rotation** is proportional to $\Delta f = \vert f_g - f_b \vert$.
- The CB is closed when the **pointer is stationary** (i.e., $\Delta f \to 0$) and pointing at the **12 o'clock "in-phase" mark** ($\delta = 0°$).

**Advantages over Dark-Lamp Method:**
1. **Higher accuracy** — can detect phase differences of less than 1° reliably.
2. **Direction information** — instantly tells the operator whether to speed up or slow down the prime mover.
3. **Suitable for slow beats** — when $\Delta f$ is very small, lamp flickering is hard to judge visually; the synchroscope gives a clear, slow, readable pointer motion.
4. **Single instrument** — replaces three lamps with one consolidated indicator.
5. **Safer** — eliminates risk of operator misjudgment of the dark instant.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing line and phase voltage** in numericals — always divide line voltage by $\sqrt{3}$ for star-connected alternators.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 3** in the synchronizing power formula for 3-phase machines.
> 3. **Skipping the trig simplification step** ($1 - \cos\delta = 2\sin^2(\delta/2)$) — examiners award marks for the algebraic transition.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations (especially $R_a \ll X_s$ and cylindrical rotor).
> 5. **Mixing up dark-lamp and bright-lamp methods** in viva — know the **lamp-connection topology** and the **closing criterion** for each.
> 6. **Skipping the unit conversion** ($\delta$ in degrees vs. radians in $\sin\delta$ — calculator must be in degree mode for typical 5°–10° problems).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Synchronization Experiment**

- [x] **Synchronization** = paralleling an alternator to an existing energized bus without transients.
- [x] **Five conditions:** Equal voltage, equal frequency, same phase sequence, in-phase ($\delta = 0°$), same waveform.
- [x] **Infinite bus** = voltage & frequency remain constant regardless of load; lab-bus qualifies due to large upstream transformer.
- [x] **Three lab methods:** Dark-lamp (close when dark), Bright-lamp (close when bright), Synchroscope (close when pointer at 12 o'clock).
- [x] **Synchroscope pointer:** CW = fast, CCW = slow, stationary at top = close CB.
- [x] **EMF per phase formula:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 f \Phi N K_w$.
- [x] **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P$.
- [x] **Beat frequency:** $\Delta f = f_g - f_b$ (rate of lamp pulsation).
- [x] **Synchronizing current (general):** $I_{sync} = \dfrac{\sqrt{E_g^2 - 2 E_g V_b \cos\delta + V_b^2}}{\sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}}$.
- [x] **Synchronizing power (3-phase):** $P_{sync} = \dfrac{3 E_g V_b}{X_s} \sin\delta$.
- [x] **Ideal closing condition:** $\delta = 0°$, $E_g = V_b$, $f_g = f_b$, correct sequence.
- [x] **Phase-sequence error** = most dangerous (acts like a 3-phase short circuit).
- [x] **Pre-closing checklist:** voltage match → frequency match → sequence → phase → close.
- [x] **Industrial equipment:** Synchroscope, synchro-tie, and modern digital auto-sync relays (e.g., ABB REL670, Siemens 7UT85).
- [x] **KTU exam weightage:** ~14 marks in ESE lab viva + 1–2 Part-A questions in theory.
